stem_en: A client from a cultural background where family members traditionally serve as the primary decision-makers is approaching the end of life. Which nursing actions demonstrate culturally sensitive care? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Consult cultural or spiritual resources identified by the client or family.
Document the client's preferred decision-making structure in the care plan.
Invite family members the client designates to participate in care discussions.
Ask the client privately whether they wish to make their own decisions or prefer family involvement.
Assume the family will make all decisions because that is the cultural norm.
Insist that the client complete an individual advance directive before receiving care.

rationale_en: Culturally sensitive end-of-life care begins by assessing the individual's actual preferences rather than assuming cultural norms apply universally. Documenting, involving designated family, and utilizing cultural resources all honor the client's values.

stem_en: An older adult client is admitted to the medical unit. Which findings are characteristic of delirium rather than dementia? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Severe inattention with difficulty following a simple conversation.
Acute onset of confusion over the past 12 hours.
Sleep-wake cycle reversal: sleeping during the day and awake and agitated at night.
Memory loss that has been gradually worsening over the past 3 years.
Level of consciousness that fluctuates between alertness and drowsiness throughout the day.
Difficulty recalling a granddaughter's name, present for approximately 18 months.

rationale_en: Delirium is characterized by acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, and disturbed sleep-wake cycle. Dementia has a gradual onset and progressive decline without fluctuating consciousness.

stem_en: A client taking haloperidol reports muscle stiffness and an inability to remain still. Which findings indicate extrapyramidal side effects (EPS)? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Shuffling gait and masklike facial expression.
Constant restless urge to move; unable to sit still.
Sudden painful sustained neck muscle contraction causing twisting to one side.
Involuntary arm and leg muscle rigidity.
Elevated mood and pressured speech.
Dry mouth and urinary retention.

rationale_en: EPS includes drug-induced parkinsonism (rigidity, shuffling gait, masked face), akathisia (restless urge to move), and acute dystonia (sudden muscle contractions causing abnormal postures). These are caused by dopamine D2 blockade in the basal ganglia.

stem_en: A rehabilitation nurse is evaluating the healing progress of a client's sacral pressure injury over a 4-week period. Arrange the following wound assessment findings in the order that indicates progressive healing from earliest sign of improvement (first) to most advanced healing (last).

options_or_rows_en.text: Necrotic tissue loosens and separates from the wound bed through autolytic debridement
Pink epithelial tissue migrates inward from wound margins
Inflammatory exudate decreases and wound base transitions from yellow slough to red granulation tissue
Wound edges contract and wound dimensions decrease measurably
Wound fully resurfaces with new epithelium; scar tissue matures and remodels

rationale_en: Wound healing follows a predictable sequence: during the inflammatory/debridement phase, necrotic tissue must first separate and be removed to prepare the wound bed; as the proliferative phase begins, slough clears and healthy red granulation tissue fills the wound bed; wound contraction then draws the edges closer together, reducing the wound size; epithelial cells migrate from the wound margins across the granulation tissue to resurface the wound; finally, in the maturation/remodeling phase, epithelial coverage is complete and the underlying collagen scar matures and strengthens over weeks to months.

stem_en: Based on the case exhibits, identify whether each finding is a cue relevant to caregiver role strain or a finding not directly relevant to caregiver role strain.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Observation Notes
options_or_rows_en.content: Home environment: Grab bars installed in bathroom; hospital bed in living room; medication organizer on kitchen counter is filled correctly for the current week. Mrs. Chen appears fatigued, with dark circles under her eyes. She is wearing the same stained clothing noted at last week's visit. The house, previously well-kept, has unwashed dishes piled in the sink and unopened mail on the table. Mrs. Chen moves slowly and winces when transferring Mr. Chen from wheelchair to hospital bed, stating, 'My back has been acting up, but I can't leave him to see the doctor.' Mr. Chen is clean, appropriately dressed, and appears well-nourished.

options_or_rows_en.text: Mrs. Chen reports sleeping only 3–4 hours per night
Medication organizer is filled correctly for the week
Mrs. Chen winces during transfers and reports back pain but has not sought medical care
Mrs. Chen withdrew from her support group and has lost social connections
Mr. Chen is clean, well-dressed, and well-nourished
Mrs. Chen expresses fear of making a medication error or dropping her husband
Unwashed dishes and unopened mail are accumulating in the home
Grab bars are installed in the bathroom
Cue relevant to caregiver role strain
Finding not directly relevant to caregiver role strain

rationale_en: Chronic sleep deprivation, neglect of one's own health, social isolation, fear of caregiving errors, and declining self-care (evidenced by household deterioration and unchanged clothing) are classic cues of caregiver role strain. The correctly filled medication organizer, Mr. Chen's good condition, and bathroom grab bars reflect adequate care of the patient and appropriate environmental modifications — they do not directly indicate caregiver burden.

stem_en: The nurse is analyzing the factors that contribute most to Mrs. Chen's caregiver burden. Complete the following clinical analysis by selecting the correct option from each dropdown.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Observation Notes
options_or_rows_en.content: Home environment: Grab bars installed in bathroom; hospital bed in living room; medication organizer on kitchen counter is filled correctly for the current week. Mrs. Chen appears fatigued, with dark circles under her eyes. She is wearing the same stained clothing noted at last week's visit. The house, previously well-kept, has unwashed dishes piled in the sink and unopened mail on the table. Mrs. Chen moves slowly and winces when transferring Mr. Chen from wheelchair to hospital bed, stating, 'My back has been acting up, but I can't leave him to see the doctor.' Mr. Chen is clean, appropriately dressed, and appears well-nourished.

options.text: Mr. Chen's nocturnal confusion and attempts to get out of bed
Mr. Chen's dysphagia requiring dietary modifications
loss of personal identity and role engulfment

rationale_en: A Zarit score of 34 on the 12-item Zarit Burden Interview falls in the high burden range (>20; 21–48 in this exhibit). Nocturnal confusion with attempts to get out of bed is the direct cause of Mrs. Chen's sleep interruption, as she explicitly describes waiting for the bed alarm and sleeping only 3–4 hours. The metaphor of 'disappearing' reflects loss of personal identity and role engulfment — the caregiver's own identity, interests, and social roles are progressively consumed by the caregiving role until the person feels they no longer exist as an individual.

stem_en: The nurse has identified multiple concerns for Mrs. Chen. Rank the following nursing priorities from highest (first) to lowest (last) based on the urgency of the identified needs.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Observation Notes
options_or_rows_en.content: Home environment: Grab bars installed in bathroom; hospital bed in living room; medication organizer on kitchen counter is filled correctly for the current week. Mrs. Chen appears fatigued, with dark circles under her eyes. She is wearing the same stained clothing noted at last week's visit. The house, previously well-kept, has unwashed dishes piled in the sink and unopened mail on the table. Mrs. Chen moves slowly and winces when transferring Mr. Chen from wheelchair to hospital bed, stating, 'My back has been acting up, but I can't leave him to see the doctor.' Mr. Chen is clean, appropriately dressed, and appears well-nourished.

options_or_rows_en.text: Explore options for nighttime respite or supervision to restore Mrs. Chen's sleep
Facilitate evaluation and treatment for Mrs. Chen's chronic back pain
Re-engage Mrs. Chen in social support networks and the caregiver support group
Discuss long-term care planning and community resources with Mrs. Chen and her daughter
Address Mrs. Chen's safety concern about potential medication errors and unsafe transfers due to exhaustion

rationale_en: Safety is always the highest priority: Mrs. Chen has identified a real risk of medication errors and unsafe transfers that could directly harm Mr. Chen. Next, addressing the root cause of her exhaustion — nocturnal caregiving without relief — is essential because sleep deprivation compounds every other problem. Her own untreated back pain is the third priority because it affects her physical ability to provide safe care and will worsen without treatment. Social reconnection addresses emotional well-being and is important but less immediately urgent. Long-term planning is critical for sustainability but is appropriately addressed after acute safety and health needs are stabilized.

stem_en: During the visit, Mrs. Chen becomes tearful and says, 'I know I should ask for help, but I feel like I'm failing him if I can't do this alone.' Select the most therapeutic nurse response for each part of the interaction.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Observation Notes
options_or_rows_en.content: Home environment: Grab bars installed in bathroom; hospital bed in living room; medication organizer on kitchen counter is filled correctly for the current week. Mrs. Chen appears fatigued, with dark circles under her eyes. She is wearing the same stained clothing noted at last week's visit. The house, previously well-kept, has unwashed dishes piled in the sink and unopened mail on the table. Mrs. Chen moves slowly and winces when transferring Mr. Chen from wheelchair to hospital bed, stating, 'My back has been acting up, but I can't leave him to see the doctor.' Mr. Chen is clean, appropriately dressed, and appears well-nourished.

options.text: "Have you considered that accepting help would actually be better for your husband's safety?"
"Caring for someone you love this much is incredibly demanding. It sounds like you're carrying a heavy weight and it's taking a toll on you."
"You shouldn't feel guilty — you're doing an amazing job and he's lucky to have you."
"Many caregivers feel the way you do. Let me give you some brochures about respite services."
reframe seeking help as an act of strength by saying, "Accepting support is not failing — it's making sure you can continue to be there for Wei safely and for a long time."
advise Mrs. Chen to call her daughter and insist on more frequent visits
tell Mrs. Chen that if she does not accept help, the nurse may need to report the situation as caregiver neglect
change the subject to discuss Mr. Chen's medication schedule to reduce Mrs. Chen's emotional distress

rationale_en: The therapeutic sequence is to first validate the caregiver's feelings with empathy before offering reframing or solutions. Option 1b uses reflective listening and empathic acknowledgment without dismissing the guilt or rushing to fix the problem. After feelings are validated, option 2a gently reframes help-seeking as a means to sustain caregiving — addressing the guilt directly while respecting Mrs. Chen's autonomy and devotion.

stem_en: The nurse is developing a comprehensive support plan for Mrs. Chen. For each proposed intervention, determine whether it is an appropriate or inappropriate action for the nurse to include in the plan at this time.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Observation Notes
options_or_rows_en.content: Home environment: Grab bars installed in bathroom; hospital bed in living room; medication organizer on kitchen counter is filled correctly for the current week. Mrs. Chen appears fatigued, with dark circles under her eyes. She is wearing the same stained clothing noted at last week's visit. The house, previously well-kept, has unwashed dishes piled in the sink and unopened mail on the table. Mrs. Chen moves slowly and winces when transferring Mr. Chen from wheelchair to hospital bed, stating, 'My back has been acting up, but I can't leave him to see the doctor.' Mr. Chen is clean, appropriately dressed, and appears well-nourished.

options_or_rows_en.text: Refer to a social worker or case manager to explore payer and community resources for expanded home aide or respite hours
Arrange a nighttime home health aide or overnight respite volunteer to address sleep deprivation
Recommend Mrs. Chen place Mr. Chen in a long-term care facility immediately
Train the home health aide on managing Parkinson freezing episodes so Mrs. Chen can step away with confidence
Schedule a telehealth appointment for Mrs. Chen's back pain to eliminate the barrier of leaving Mr. Chen
Contact the daughter without Mrs. Chen's consent to demand she provide more caregiving support
Inappropriate intervention
Appropriate intervention

rationale_en: Appropriate interventions address identified needs while respecting Mrs. Chen's autonomy, values, and stated preference to keep her husband at home. Social work or case management referral for payer and community-resource assistance, nighttime respite to address sleep deprivation, aide training to build trust in delegated care, and telehealth for her own health barrier are all client-centered and practical. Recommending immediate facility placement disregards Mrs. Chen's clearly expressed wishes and values; contacting the daughter without consent violates patient confidentiality and the caregiver's autonomy.

stem_en: Two weeks after implementing the support plan, the nurse conducts a follow-up visit. For each finding, determine whether it indicates the interventions are effective or whether the finding requires further intervention.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Observation Notes
options_or_rows_en.content: Home environment: Grab bars installed in bathroom; hospital bed in living room; medication organizer on kitchen counter is filled correctly for the current week. Mrs. Chen appears fatigued, with dark circles under her eyes. She is wearing the same stained clothing noted at last week's visit. The house, previously well-kept, has unwashed dishes piled in the sink and unopened mail on the table. Mrs. Chen moves slowly and winces when transferring Mr. Chen from wheelchair to hospital bed, stating, 'My back has been acting up, but I can't leave him to see the doctor.' Mr. Chen is clean, appropriately dressed, and appears well-nourished.

options_or_rows_en.text: Mrs. Chen reports sleeping 6–7 hours per night since a trained overnight aide began three nights per week
Mrs. Chen attended one caregiver support group meeting but says, 'It was okay, but I kept checking my phone the whole time'
The telehealth provider prescribed physical therapy for Mrs. Chen's back pain, but she has not scheduled the first appointment
Mrs. Chen's Zarit Burden Interview score has decreased from 34 to 27
Mrs. Chen states, 'I still feel guilty when the aide is here, but I know Wei is safe and I actually ate dinner sitting down last night'
Intervention effective
Requires further intervention

rationale_en: Improvement in sleep duration from 3–4 hours to 6–7 hours demonstrates effective nighttime respite. The Zarit score decrease from 34 to 27 shows measurable burden reduction, although both scores remain in the high-burden range for the 12-item short form. Mrs. Chen's statement about eating dinner sitting down — while acknowledging residual guilt — shows emerging self-care behavior and growing trust in the aide, indicating positive progress. However, constant phone-checking at the support group suggests persistent separation anxiety that limits the benefit of social re-engagement. Failure to schedule physical therapy indicates a continuing barrier to self-care that needs to be explored and addressed.

stem_en: A client is diagnosed with an inferior wall myocardial infarction. Which of the following clinical manifestations or complications should the nurse specifically monitor for in this client? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Nausea and vomiting
Bradycardia
Second-degree AV block, Mobitz Type I
Left bundle branch block
Right ventricular infarction

rationale_en: Inferior wall MIs (Right Coronary Artery involvement) often involve the SA and AV nodes, leading to bradycardia and Type I AV blocks. They are frequently associated with RV infarction. Vagal stimulation in inferior MIs commonly causes N/V (E). Left bundle branch block is more common in anterior wall MIs.

stem_en: The nurse is caring for a client with a T4 spinal cord injury who develops a pounding headache and nasal congestion. After sitting the client upright, which of the following actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Lower the head of the bed to improve cerebral perfusion.
Check the urinary drainage system for kinks.
Perform a digital rectal exam to check for fecal impaction.
Check the skin for pressure sores or restrictive clothing.
Administer an antihypertensive medication if BP remains elevated.

rationale_en: Appropriate management of AD involves removing the stimulus. Most common triggers are bladder distension, fecal impaction, or skin irritation. Antihypertensives are used if initial measures don't lower BP. Sitting the client up is the first step; lowering the HOB (E) would worsen hypertension-induced intracranial pressure.

stem_en: The nurse is monitoring a client who has been taking chlorpromazine for several months. Which findings would suggest the development of extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS)? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Frequent thirst and excessive urination
Shuffling gait and mask-like facial expression
Involuntary tongue protrusion and smacking of lips
Restlessness and an uncontrollable need to be in motion
Sudden onset of severe muscle spasms in the neck

rationale_en: EPS includes akathisia, tardive dyskinesia, pseudoparkinsonism, and acute dystonia.

stem_en: Which statements accurately describe the psychosocial challenges this client is facing? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client states, 'I am terrified my hands are going to become deformed. I work as a graphic designer, and I need my hands. Sometimes I just pretend I don't have this disease, but then my joints ache so badly in the morning.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Acceptance of the chronic illness
Fear of altered body image
Use of denial as a coping mechanism
Threat to occupational role
Anxiety regarding future disability

rationale_en: The client expresses fear of deformity (body image), fear of losing her job (role), anxiety about the future, and explicitly states she sometimes 'pretends' it isn't real (denial).

options_or_rows_en.content: Client states, 'I am terrified my hands are going to become deformed. I work as a graphic designer, and I need my hands. Sometimes I just pretend I don't have this disease, but then my joints ache so badly in the morning.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Tell the client that new medications prevent all deformities now.
Provide a referral to an occupational therapist.
Encourage the client to express her fears about her career.
Teach joint protection techniques.
Inappropriate
Appropriate

rationale_en: Validating fears, providing practical tools (joint protection), and involving specialists (occupational therapy) empower the client. False reassurance about medications preventing *all* deformities is inappropriate.

options_or_rows_en.text: 'Denial is a normal first step, but you need to move past it now.'
'If you keep pretending you don't have RA, your joints will get worse.'
'It is common to want to ignore a new diagnosis. Let's talk about what makes it hardest to accept.'

rationale_en: This response normalizes the feeling (validates) while gently inviting the client to explore the underlying fears preventing acceptance.

stem_en: Which statement by the nurse best addresses the client's use of denial?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client states, 'I am terrified my hands are going to become deformed. I work as a graphic designer, and I need my hands. Sometimes I just pretend I don't have this disease, but then my joints ache so badly in the morning.'

options_or_rows_en.text: 'Denial is a normal first step, but you need to move past it now.'
'If you keep pretending you don't have RA, your joints will get worse.'
'It is common to want to ignore a new diagnosis. Let's talk about what makes it hardest to accept.'
'You have a mild case, so there is no need to worry right now.'

rationale_en: This response normalizes the feeling (validates) while gently inviting the client to explore the underlying fears preventing acceptance.

stem_en: The client attends follow-up appointments.

wearing gloves to hide her hands
decided to quit her job as a designer

rationale_en: Adaptation means integrating the illness into life (taking meds) and modifying routines to maintain function (using adaptive equipment).

Decreased mobility

Recent surgery and anesthesia
Age of 70

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports abdominal fullness and no bowel movement for 4 days. Bowel sounds are hypoactive in all quadrants. She is taking oral hydrocodone-acetaminophen for pain. She is on strict bed rest except for physical therapy. Fluid intake is 800 mL/day.

options_or_rows_en.text: Encourage fluid intake of 2000-3000 mL/day (if not contraindicated).
Administer a prescribed daily stimulant laxative long-term.
Provide high-fiber snacks like prunes or bran.
Administer docusate sodium daily as prescribed.
Question
Implement

rationale_en: Hydration, fiber, and stool softeners (docusate) are first-line treatments. Long-term use of stimulant laxatives causes bowel dependence and should be questioned.

stem_en: The client states, 'I feel like I need to go, but I can't.' What is the nurse's first action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports abdominal fullness and no bowel movement for 4 days. Bowel sounds are hypoactive in all quadrants. She is taking oral hydrocodone-acetaminophen for pain. She is on strict bed rest except for physical therapy. Fluid intake is 800 mL/day.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform a digital rectal examination to check for impaction.
Tell the client to strain harder.
Administer an enema immediately.
Provide privacy and assist the client to the bedside commode.

rationale_en: Positioning and privacy are critical. A commode allows for a natural sitting position which uses gravity to aid defecation, unlike a bedpan.

stem_en: Which cues indicate the wife is experiencing caregiver role strain? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: The wife states, 'I am just so tired all the time. He wanders at night so I can't sleep. I haven't been to my own doctor in a year, and I sometimes forget to take my own blood pressure medicine.' The wife begins to cry during the interview.

options_or_rows_en.text: Sleep deprivation
Neglect of personal health needs
Living in the same home as the client
Constant fatigue
Emotional lability (crying)

rationale_en: Caregiver strain manifests as physical (fatigue, sleep loss), emotional (crying), and behavioral (neglecting own meds/appointments) changes.

stem_en: Click to specify if each intervention is an appropriate or inappropriate recommendation for the caregiver.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: The wife states, 'I am just so tired all the time. He wanders at night so I can't sleep. I haven't been to my own doctor in a year, and I sometimes forget to take my own blood pressure medicine.' The wife begins to cry during the interview.

options_or_rows_en.text: Suggesting the wife ask adult children to sit with the husband one afternoon a week.
Advising the wife to focus harder on the husband's needs to prevent his wandering.
Recommending an adult day care program for the husband.
Providing information on Alzheimer's caregiver support groups.
Inappropriate
Appropriate

rationale_en: Interventions must focus on respite (relief) for the caregiver and peer support. Telling her to try harder exacerbates the guilt and burnout.

stem_en: How should the nurse interpret the client's presentation?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports a 5 lb (2.3 kg) weight loss since his wife died. He states, 'I miss her terribly, and the house feels so empty. I haven't felt like going to my weekly card game.' He also states, 'I know she is in a better place and no longer suffering.'

options_or_rows_en.text: He is experiencing disenfranchised grief.
He is in the denial stage of grief.
He is experiencing normal, uncomplicated grief.
He is experiencing complicated grief requiring psychiatric intervention.

rationale_en: At 2 months post-loss, sadness, mild weight loss, and temporary social withdrawal are normal grieving behaviors. His ability to find meaning ('she is in a better place') indicates healthy processing.

stem_en: What is the nurse's most therapeutic response to the client's statement, 'The house feels so empty'?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports a 5 lb (2.3 kg) weight loss since his wife died. He states, 'I miss her terribly, and the house feels so empty. I haven't felt like going to my weekly card game.' He also states, 'I know she is in a better place and no longer suffering.'

options_or_rows_en.text: 'Perhaps you should consider downsizing to a smaller apartment.'
'You should force yourself to go to your card game to take your mind off it.'
'It sounds like you are feeling very lonely without her here.'
'It will get easier as time passes; you just need to stay busy.'

rationale_en: Reflecting feelings (validating) encourages the client to explore their emotions. Giving advice or offering false reassurance shuts down communication.

options_or_rows_en.title: Health History
options_or_rows_en.content: Medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Has not seen a healthcare provider in 4 years. He lives independently. Reports a mild chronic cough. Asks, 'What shots do I need at my age?'

options_or_rows_en.text: Tdap or Td booster (if due or immunization history unknown)
Pneumococcal vaccine
HPV vaccine
Recombinant zoster vaccine
Influenza vaccine (high-dose)

rationale_en: Adults ≥65 need high-dose influenza, pneumococcal, zoster, and Tdap (if due) vaccines. HPV is not indicated for this age group.

stem_en: The client states, 'I don't want the flu shot because it gave me the flu last time.' What is the nurse's best response?

options_or_rows_en.title: Health History
options_or_rows_en.content: Medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Has not seen a healthcare provider in 4 years. He lives independently. Reports a mild chronic cough. Asks, 'What shots do I need at my age?'

options_or_rows_en.text: 'We can skip the flu shot this year since you had a bad reaction.'
'The injected flu vaccine is inactivated and cannot cause the flu. You may have experienced a mild immune response or a cold.'
'You should take acetaminophen before the injection to prevent getting sick.'
'The flu shot contains a live virus, so mild symptoms are normal.'

rationale_en: The injected influenza vaccine contains inactivated virus and cannot cause influenza. Mild systemic reactions (muscle aches, low fever) are a normal immune response.

Alert and cooperative cognitive status
First time out of bed after illness

stem_en: The nurse is preparing to transfer the client from the bed to the chair. Order the steps of the transfer.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client has been on bed rest for 5 days. Provider orders: 'Out of bed to chair twice daily.' Client is alert and cooperative but states, 'My legs feel like jelly.' Physical therapy has recommended the use of a standard walker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply a gait belt around the client's waist.
Position the chair parallel to the bed and lock the wheels.
Assist the client to dangle legs on the side of the bed for 1-2 minutes.
Instruct the client to pivot and grasp the chair armrests.
Ensure non-slip footwear is on the client.

rationale_en: Safety requires preparing the environment first (lock chair), preparing the client (footwear, dangling to prevent orthostatic hypotension), applying safety equipment (gait belt), and executing the transfer safely.

stem_en: The nurse is teaching the client how to use the standard walker. Click to specify whether each instruction is correct or incorrect.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client has been on bed rest for 5 days. Provider orders: 'Out of bed to chair twice daily.' Client is alert and cooperative but states, 'My legs feel like jelly.' Physical therapy has recommended the use of a standard walker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Adjust the walker height so the handgrips are at the level of the wrist crease.
Move the walker forward 6-8 inches, then step forward into it.
Look down at your feet while walking to ensure placement.
When rising from a chair, pull yourself up using the walker handles.
Correct
Incorrect

rationale_en: Walker height should allow a slight elbow bend (wrist crease level). The walker moves first, then the client steps in. Looking down alters balance. Pulling up on a walker can tip it over.

stem_en: The client successfully ambulates with the walker to the door and back.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client has been on bed rest for 5 days. Provider orders: 'Out of bed to chair twice daily.' Client is alert and cooperative but states, 'My legs feel like jelly.' Physical therapy has recommended the use of a standard walker.

rationale_en: The standard walker sequence is: advance the walker 6–8 inches, step the weaker leg into it, then bring the stronger leg forward. This allows the arms to bear weight through the device during the weaker leg's step. An upright posture maintains the center of gravity over the base of support.

stem_en: The client successfully ambulates with the walker to the door and back.

rationale_en: The standard walker sequence is: advance the walker 6–8 inches, step the weaker leg into it, then bring the stronger leg forward. This allows the arms to bear weight through the device during the weaker leg's step. An upright posture maintains the center of gravity over the base of support.

rationale_en: A T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 indicates osteopenia (low bone mass). A score of -2.5 or lower indicates osteoporosis.

Swimming
Light weight training
Does not directly build bone density

rationale_en: Weight-bearing and resistance exercises (walking, light weights) stimulate bone remodeling and directly build bone density. Swimming provides cardiovascular benefit but is non-weight-bearing and does not build bone. High-impact aerobics are not recommended for bone health in this client due to fracture risk with osteopenia.

stem_en: The client expresses concern that if she quits smoking, she will gain weight. What is the nurse's most appropriate intervention?

options_or_rows_en.title: Health History
options_or_rows_en.content: Smokes 1 pack per day for 25 years. Has tried quitting twice before using nicotine patches, but relapsed when feeling stressed at work. Currently taking an oral contraceptive.

options_or_rows_en.text: Acknowledge that mild weight gain is common and focus the plan on keeping her hands and mouth busy with healthy snacks.
Reassure her that weight gain is a myth and will not happen.
Advise her to start a strict low-calorie diet on the same day she quits smoking.
Tell her to delay quitting until she has lost 10 pounds.

rationale_en: Weight gain is a common reality due to metabolic changes and substituting food for cigarettes. Acknowledging this and providing healthy alternatives is the best approach. Too many strict changes at once (like a strict diet) can lead to failure.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after infectious stool studies and viral PCRs return negative and the tacrolimus trough is found to be subtherapeutic, the nurse must analyze the cluster of findings (new maculopapular rash on acral surfaces, high-volume watery diarrhea, mildly elevated bilirubin with rising liver enzymes, and onset at day 26 post-transplant). Which conclusion is best supported by these clinical data?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Presentation & Baseline Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
The patient presents to the transplant unit stating that she has developed progressively worsening watery diarrhea over the past two days, now occurring five to six times daily, with crampy abdominal pain that is diffuse and rated five out of ten. She also notes a new rash on her palms, the soles of her feet, and her ears that appeared yesterday and has been spreading to her upper chest and back. She reports decreased appetite and mild nausea but no vomiting. She denies fever, cough, or dysuria. She appears fatigued and mildly uncomfortable but is alert, oriented, and conversational.

Baseline vital signs on admission to the unit:
Temperature: 37.3°C (99.1°F)
Heart rate: 96 beats/min
Blood pressure: 108/68 mmHg
Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
Oxygen saturation: 97% on room air
Weight: 64.2 kg (down 2.1 kg from clinic visit 5 days ago)

Skin assessment reveals a diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash on the palms bilaterally, the soles of the feet, the pinnae of both ears, and scattered across the upper chest and posterior shoulders, estimated to cover approximately 30% of body surface area. The rash blanches with pressure and is non-tender. No bullae, desquamation, or mucosal involvement is noted. Oral mucosa is moist but slightly erythematous. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with diffuse tenderness to palpation, hyperactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants. No guarding or rebound. Stool is greenish-watery without visible blood. Central line site is clean, dry, and without erythema or drainage. Peripheral edema is absent.

options_or_rows_en.text: The patient has developed a delayed Clostridioides difficile infection that requires empiric antibiotic therapy until repeat testing.
The presentation represents a severe allergic drug reaction to prophylactic medications requiring their immediate discontinuation.
The symptoms indicate engraftment syndrome, expected during the first month following allogeneic transplantation.
The patient is experiencing acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver.

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes that the constellation of rash predominantly on palms, soles, and ears, combined with voluminous watery diarrhea and cholestatic liver enzyme elevation, arising in the third to fourth week after allogeneic transplant with a subtherapeutic immunosuppressant level, is the classic triad of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver. The negative infectious workup further supports this conclusion.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after infectious stool studies and viral PCRs return negative and the tacrolimus trough is found to be subtherapeutic, the nurse must analyze the cluster of findings (new maculopapular rash on acral surfaces, high-volume watery diarrhea, mildly elevated bilirubin with rising liver enzymes, and onset at day 26 post-transplant). Which conclusion is best supported by these clinical data?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Update
options_or_rows_en.content:
84 hours into corticosteroid therapy and 48 hours after the addition of ruxolitinib: The patient has shown early but measurable improvement. Stool output over the past 24 hours has decreased to 900 mL with no further visible blood. The rash has stabilized and begun to fade on the upper chest and ears, now covering approximately 40% of body surface area, with no bullae or desquamation. Abdominal cramping has decreased to 3/10. 

Vital signs:
Temperature: 37.2°C (99.0°F)
Heart rate: 92 beats/min
Blood pressure: 110/70 mmHg
Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
Oxygen saturation: 98% on room air

Urine output over the past 8 hours is 320 mL. She is more alert and reports improved comfort.

Repeat labs:
Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L
Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL
Total bilirubin: 2.4 mg/dL
Direct bilirubin: 1.6 mg/dL
ALT: 72 U/L
Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL
Blood urea nitrogen: 26 mg/dL
Albumin: 2.4 g/dL
Tacrolimus trough: 12.1 ng/mL
Glucose: 162 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 8.8 g/dL (post-transfusion)

Blood cultures drawn at the time of fever in Stage 2 have returned with no growth at 48 hours. The team discusses a slow oral diet advancement beginning with clear liquids and ongoing close monitoring.

options_or_rows_en.text: The patient has developed a delayed Clostridioides difficile infection that requires empiric antibiotic therapy until repeat testing.
The presentation represents a severe allergic drug reaction to prophylactic medications requiring their immediate discontinuation.
The symptoms indicate engraftment syndrome, expected during the first month following allogeneic transplantation.
The patient is experiencing acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver.

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes that the constellation of rash predominantly on palms, soles, and ears, combined with voluminous watery diarrhea and cholestatic liver enzyme elevation, arising in the third to fourth week after allogeneic transplant with a subtherapeutic immunosuppressant level, is the classic triad of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver. The negative infectious workup further supports this conclusion.

stem_en: At Stage 1, before high-dose corticosteroids are administered, the nurse reviews the repeat electrolyte values (potassium 3.1 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL). Which action is the highest priority for the nurse regarding these findings?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Presentation & Baseline Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
The patient presents to the transplant unit stating that she has developed progressively worsening watery diarrhea over the past two days, now occurring five to six times daily, with crampy abdominal pain that is diffuse and rated five out of ten. She also notes a new rash on her palms, the soles of her feet, and her ears that appeared yesterday and has been spreading to her upper chest and back. She reports decreased appetite and mild nausea but no vomiting. She denies fever, cough, or dysuria. She appears fatigued and mildly uncomfortable but is alert, oriented, and conversational.

Baseline vital signs on admission to the unit:
Temperature: 37.3°C (99.1°F)
Heart rate: 96 beats/min
Blood pressure: 108/68 mmHg
Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
Oxygen saturation: 97% on room air
Weight: 64.2 kg (down 2.1 kg from clinic visit 5 days ago)

Skin assessment reveals a diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash on the palms bilaterally, the soles of the feet, the pinnae of both ears, and scattered across the upper chest and posterior shoulders, estimated to cover approximately 30% of body surface area. The rash blanches with pressure and is non-tender. No bullae, desquamation, or mucosal involvement is noted. Oral mucosa is moist but slightly erythematous. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with diffuse tenderness to palpation, hyperactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants. No guarding or rebound. Stool is greenish-watery without visible blood. Central line site is clean, dry, and without erythema or drainage. Peripheral edema is absent.

options_or_rows_en.text: Document the findings and plan to address the mildly low potassium with dietary supplementation.
Administer the methylprednisolone as ordered and monitor the next scheduled metabolic panel.
Hold the ordered methylprednisolone until the electrolyte imbalances spontaneously correct.
Promptly communicate the findings to the provider to obtain orders for intravenous electrolyte replacement.

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes that hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia in the setting of ongoing diarrheal losses require prompt intravenous or protocol-based replacement, especially because impending high-dose corticosteroids will further exacerbate renal potassium wasting. Magnesium repletion is also necessary to correct the potassium deficit effectively.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the nurse assesses worsening vital signs (heart rate 118, blood pressure 92/56, reduced urine output of 180 mL over 8 hours) alongside escalating stool output with visible blood, a rising creatinine, supratherapeutic tacrolimus level, hyperglycemia, worsening bilirubin, and a hemoglobin of 7.9 g/dL. Which problem is the highest priority for the nurse to address first?

options_or_rows_en.text: Decreased tissue perfusion related to dropping hemoglobin.
Hemodynamic instability from hypovolemia.
Steroid-induced hyperglycemia.
Supratherapeutic tacrolimus level with nephrotoxicity.

rationale_en: The nurse prioritizes the hemodynamic instability from hypovolemia as the most immediately life-threatening problem. The tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, dry mucous membranes, and decreased skin turgor indicate significant intravascular volume depletion requiring immediate intervention.

stem_en: At Stage 2, with the tacrolimus trough of 18.4 ng/mL and a creatinine rising from 1.0 to 1.6 mg/dL, the nurse determines the appropriate action regarding the tacrolimus infusion. Which action is correct?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Presentation & Baseline Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
The patient presents to the transplant unit stating that she has developed progressively worsening watery diarrhea over the past two days, now occurring five to six times daily, with crampy abdominal pain that is diffuse and rated five out of ten. She also notes a new rash on her palms, the soles of her feet, and her ears that appeared yesterday and has been spreading to her upper chest and back. She reports decreased appetite and mild nausea but no vomiting. She denies fever, cough, or dysuria. She appears fatigued and mildly uncomfortable but is alert, oriented, and conversational.

Baseline vital signs on admission to the unit:
Temperature: 37.3°C (99.1°F)
Heart rate: 96 beats/min
Blood pressure: 108/68 mmHg
Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
Oxygen saturation: 97% on room air
Weight: 64.2 kg (down 2.1 kg from clinic visit 5 days ago)

Skin assessment reveals a diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash on the palms bilaterally, the soles of the feet, the pinnae of both ears, and scattered across the upper chest and posterior shoulders, estimated to cover approximately 30% of body surface area. The rash blanches with pressure and is non-tender. No bullae, desquamation, or mucosal involvement is noted. Oral mucosa is moist but slightly erythematous. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with diffuse tenderness to palpation, hyperactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants. No guarding or rebound. Stool is greenish-watery without visible blood. Central line site is clean, dry, and without erythema or drainage. Peripheral edema is absent.

options_or_rows_en.text: Independently discontinue the tacrolimus infusion to prevent permanent renal damage.
Maintain the current tacrolimus infusion rate, as the rising creatinine is solely due to hypovolemia.
Reduce the tacrolimus infusion rate per the provider order and closely monitor urine output.
Administer a loop diuretic to counteract the tacrolimus-induced nephrotoxicity.

rationale_en: The nurse should reduce the tacrolimus infusion rate per the provider order, monitor urine output and fluid balance, and anticipate repeat trough levels. Tacrolimus is a narrow-therapeutic-index calcineurin inhibitor; its supratherapeutic level contributes to acute kidney injury through renal vasoconstriction. Risk factors here are multifactorial, including hypovolemia from diarrhea, the recent rate increase, hepatic dysfunction impairing metabolism, and concomitant fluconazole prophylaxis. The medication must not be independently discontinued due to the high risk of triggering an aGVHD flare.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stool volume decreased to 900 mL/24h with no visible blood
Stabilization and fading of rash on upper chest and ears
Blood glucose of 162 mg/dL
Total bilirubin decreased to 2.4 mg/dL
Absence of fever while on ruxolitinib and high-dose corticosteroids
Requires Continued Vigilance
Indicates Therapeutic Response

rationale_en: The nurse correctly identifies that improvements in stool volume, rash extent, and bilirubin levels directly reflect resolution of the aGVHD. However, the nurse must remain vigilant about iatrogenic complications, such as steroid-induced hyperglycemia and the profound immunosuppression masked by the lack of a febrile response.

stem_en: A client was admitted 36 hours after the last alcohol intake. Which findings suggest worsening withdrawal that requires prompt intervention? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Disorientation and severe agitation.
Asks for help sleeping because the unit is noisy.
Blood pressure 184/106 mm Hg and heart rate 128/min.
New generalized tonic-clonic seizure.
Fine tremor of the hands with mild anxiety.
Visual hallucinations of insects on the wall.

rationale_en: Hallucinations, severe autonomic instability, seizures, disorientation, and severe agitation can indicate severe withdrawal or delirium tremens.

stem_en: Which statements by a postpartum client indicate correct understanding of breastfeeding? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: I should hear swallowing during effective feeding.
I need to wash my nipples with soap before each feeding.
I will count wet diapers to help know if intake is adequate.
The baby's mouth should cover much of the areola, not just the nipple.
I should feed my baby when early hunger cues appear.
Painful cracked nipples are expected for the first month.

rationale_en: Effective breastfeeding includes feeding on cues, deep latch, audible swallowing, and monitoring output. Cracked nipples and harsh washing suggest problems or poor latch.

stem_en: Which nursing actions help reduce the risk of central line-associated bloodstream infection? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Leave old blood under the dressing if the edges remain sealed.
Perform hand hygiene before accessing the line.
Use the central line for routine blood draws even when not needed.
Review daily whether the central line is still needed.
Change transparent dressings when loose, damp, or soiled.
Scrub the catheter hub before each access.

rationale_en: Hand hygiene, hub disinfection, clean intact dressings, and prompt removal of unnecessary lines reduce infection risk.

stem_en: Complete the clinical judgment statement using the updated assessment data.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency department triage
options_or_rows_en.content: A 72-year-old client with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction arrives with 2 days of worsening dyspnea and orthopnea. The client missed furosemide for 3 days after running out of tablets. Vital signs: T 37.1° C, HR 112/min, RR 26/min, BP 168/94 mm Hg, SpO2 89% on room air. Lung sounds: bibasilar crackles. 2+ bilateral ankle edema. Reports needing 4 pillows to sleep.

options.text: acute pulmonary edema with respiratory failure
ankle edema without a respiratory change
a normal potassium and a non-elevated troponin
pink frothy sputum, worsening hypoxemia, and crackles extending upward

rationale_en: Pink frothy sputum, rapidly worsening hypoxemia, restlessness, and diffuse crackles indicate acute pulmonary edema that can progress to respiratory failure.

stem_en: Calculate the client's net fluid balance since arrival in mL. Enter a negative number for a net loss.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency department triage
options_or_rows_en.content: A 72-year-old client with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction arrives with 2 days of worsening dyspnea and orthopnea. The client missed furosemide for 3 days after running out of tablets. Vital signs: T 37.1° C, HR 112/min, RR 26/min, BP 168/94 mm Hg, SpO2 89% on room air. Lung sounds: bibasilar crackles. 2+ bilateral ankle edema. Reports needing 4 pillows to sleep.

rationale_en: Net balance is intake minus output: 920 mL - 2,100 mL = -1,180 mL. The negative balance supports an initial diuretic response, but oxygenation and perfusion still require reassessment.

options_or_rows_en.text: Activate rapid response/provider notification for suspected adrenal crisis with shock.
Administer prescribed IV hydrocortisone, isotonic saline, and dextrose without delaying for cortisol results.
Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, and bedside glucose.
Maintain IV access, apply cardiac monitoring, and place the client supine with legs elevated if tolerated.

rationale_en: Immediate assessment identifies shock and hypoglycemia, supportive measures reduce collapse risk, urgent escalation obtains emergency therapy, and treatment should not wait for confirmatory cortisol testing when crisis is suspected.

options_or_rows_en.title: Hospital day 2
options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old client is receiving a continuous unfractionated heparin infusion for an acute pulmonary embolism. Baseline platelet count was 226,000/mm3 and hemoglobin was 13.2 g/dL. Current vital signs: HR 96/min, BP 118/70 mm Hg, SpO2 95% on 2 L/min. The client reports mild pleuritic chest pain that is improving.

rationale_en: The decrease is 226,000 - 96,000 = 130,000. 130,000 ÷ 226,000 × 100 = 57.5%, rounded to 58%. A fall of about 50% or more during heparin therapy requires HIT evaluation while bleeding is also addressed.

Instruct the client to weigh each evening after dinner.
Explore affordable lower-sodium food choices and referral options.
Tell the client to drink only small sips of fluid until the next appointment.

rationale_en: The plan should use plain language, teach-back, demonstration, resource-sensitive nutrition counseling, and clear escalation instructions. Unsafe fluid restriction and evening weighing conflict with individualized instructions and accurate tracking.

options_or_rows_en.title: Discharge rounds note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is 72 years old and lives alone in a second-floor apartment. Medically stable after treatment for a leg wound and weakness. Uses a walker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Notify case management/social work and the provider about barriers affecting discharge safety.
Ask the client to describe food, transportation, medication, and home-support barriers and priorities.
Ask the client to explain the plan, warning signs, and whom to call if a service does not occur.

rationale_en: The nurse first assesses the client's priorities and barriers, then involves the interprofessional team, confirms services and medication access, and finally verifies understanding with teach-back. This sequence avoids making assumptions or discharging before resources are real.

Caregiver strain cue
Protective factor
Immediate safety risk

stem_en: The daughter begins crying and says, “I promised I would never put Mom in a facility, but I am exhausted.” Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic?

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-health nurse note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 79-year-old with moderate dementia discharged 5 days ago after observation for dehydration. Lives with adult daughter, who works remotely.
Daughter reports the client has slept poorly, wanders toward the front door after dinner, and asks repeatedly to “go home.”
Two morning medication doses were found in a coffee cup. The daughter says she slept about 3 hours most nights this week.
Yesterday the client turned on the stove while the daughter was showering. No injury occurred.

options_or_rows_en.text: “You are doing the right thing by keeping her at home, so try to take it one day at a time.”
“You sound overwhelmed. Tell me what has been hardest since your mother came home.”
“A facility placement would break your promise, so we should avoid discussing that today.”
“I will give you a list of agencies, and you can decide which one to call later.”

rationale_en: The best response validates the caregiver's distress and invites further assessment without judgment. It opens the door to discussing safety, respite, and realistic supports.

stem_en: Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic after the client says, “I heard dialysis and stopped listening. I think my life is over”?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nephrology clinic nurse note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 46-year-old recently told they have stage 4 chronic kidney disease and may need renal replacement education. The client came with a spouse after missing the first education class because of work.
Client states, “I heard dialysis and stopped listening. I think my life is over.” The client denies thoughts of self-harm. Spouse says, “We cannot tell our teenagers. I will handle all the decisions so they do not worry.”
The family reports difficulty paying for fresh foods and confusion about which diet changes matter first.

options_or_rows_en.text: “Your spouse can make the decisions until you feel ready.”
“Hearing that word felt overwhelming. Tell me what dialysis means to you right now.”
“Let me explain the entire dialysis schedule so you can stop worrying.”
“Many people do fine on dialysis, so try not to think about the worst.”

rationale_en: The best response acknowledges the emotion, invites the client to explain their understanding, and keeps the client involved in decisions. It does not minimize the feeling, overload the client with details, or transfer decision-making to the spouse.

stem_en: Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic after the client says, “I heard dialysis and stopped listening. I think my life is over”?

stem_en: What is the nurse’s priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Primary care screening visit
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 66-year-old seen for a wellness visit 3 months after spouse died. The client reports sleeping 3–4 hours, losing 8 lb, drinking 2–3 alcoholic drinks most nights, and stopping church and walking group activities.
PHQ-9 score: 18. Item 9 (“thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself”) marked “several days.”
Client says quietly, “Some mornings I wish I would not wake up.” When asked about a plan, the client says, “No plan. I just feel empty.” The spouse’s handgun is locked in a closet at home. Adult daughter calls weekly and offered to come to appointments.

options_or_rows_en.text: Call the daughter immediately and disclose all screening results before speaking further with the client.
Schedule a routine follow-up visit in 1 month to repeat the PHQ-9.
Ask directly about suicidal thoughts, intent, plan, and access to lethal means, and notify the provider or crisis clinician before the client leaves.
Reassure the client that grief commonly lasts longer than 3 months and provide a brochure.

rationale_en: The exhibit includes passive death wishes, a positive PHQ-9 item 9, alcohol use, withdrawal, weight loss, and firearm access. The priority is direct suicide risk assessment and escalation according to clinic policy before the client leaves.

stem_en: What is the nurse’s priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinic safety policy excerpt
options_or_rows_en.content: If a client reports suicidal thoughts or passive death wishes, complete a direct suicide risk assessment, notify the provider or crisis clinician before the client leaves, assess access to lethal means, involve supports with the client’s permission when possible, and provide crisis resources and a written safety plan as indicated.

options_or_rows_en.text: Call the daughter immediately and disclose all screening results before speaking further with the client.

Ask directly about suicidal thoughts, intent, plan, and access to lethal means, and notify the provider or crisis clinician before the client leaves.

rationale_en: The exhibit includes passive death wishes, a positive PHQ-9 item 9, alcohol use, withdrawal, weight loss, and firearm access. The priority is direct suicide risk assessment and escalation according to clinic policy before the client leaves.

rationale_en: Positive item 9, passive death wishes, alcohol use, firearm access, and withdrawal increase safety concern and depression risk. Daughter support is protective. Grief after loss is expected, but the severity and safety cues require more than reassurance.

options_or_rows_en.title: Primary care screening visit
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 66-year-old seen for a wellness visit 3 months after spouse died. The client reports sleeping 3–4 hours, losing 8 lb, drinking 2–3 alcoholic drinks most nights, and stopping church and walking group activities.
PHQ-9 score: 18. Item 9 (“thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself”) marked “several days.”
Client says quietly, “Some mornings I wish I would not wake up.” When asked about a plan, the client says, “No plan. I just feel empty.” The spouse’s handgun is locked in a closet at home. Adult daughter calls weekly and offered to come to appointments.

options.text: denial of a current plan only
positive PHQ-9 item 9, passive death wish, alcohol use, and firearm access
weekly calls from the adult daughter only

rationale_en: The best-supported risk is self-harm/suicide risk because the record includes a positive PHQ-9 safety item, passive death wish, alcohol use, and access to a firearm. The client denies a plan, but denial does not remove the need for direct assessment and safety planning.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stoma is moist and beefy red.
Client says, “If the stoma turns dark purple or black, I will wait until the clinic visit.”

Requires follow-up before discharge
Expected/therapeutic support

rationale_en: A moist red stoma is expected early after surgery. A dusky stoma, repeated leakage with skin burning, and inability to explain pouch emptying need follow-up. Embarrassment requires supportive communication but is not the same as a physiologic complication.

stem_en: Which client statement indicates that discharge teaching about pouch emptying was effective?

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 3 assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client has a new descending colostomy after surgery for bowel obstruction. Stoma is moist and beefy red. Peristomal skin is intact this morning.
Pouch output: soft brown stool. Client has been watching staff empty the pouch but has not done it independently.

options_or_rows_en.text: “I will wait until the pouch is completely full so I do not waste supplies.”
“I will empty the pouch when it is about one-third to one-half full.”
“I should scrub the stoma if it looks red and moist.”
“I only need to empty the pouch once each morning.”

rationale_en: The correct statement reflects practical pouch management: emptying before the pouch becomes heavy helps prevent leakage and skin injury. The other statements delay care or show misunderstanding of normal care.

options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask permission to include a chosen support person in teaching.
Teach the adult child outside the room because the client is embarrassed.
Ask the client to demonstrate emptying and securing the pouch before leaving.
Show severe skin-breakdown photos to make the client take pouching seriously.
Replace dense written instructions with plain-language steps and pictures.
Confirm the client has starter supplies, delivery arrangements, and a number to call for leakage or skin breakdown.

rationale_en: Teaching should be adapted to the client's literacy needs, privacy preferences, and actual ability to perform care. The nurse should verify supply access and escalation instructions. Avoid excluding the client or relying on shame-based teaching.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 48-year-old client with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back is admitted with suspected gallstone pancreatitis. Pain 9/10, persistent vomiting. Vital signs: T 38.0° C, HR 116/min, RR 24/min, BP 104/62 mm Hg. Abdomen is distended and tender. NPO status is initiated.

options.text: NPO status and opioid administration only
third spacing, hemoconcentration, hypotension, and falling urine output

rationale_en: Severe pancreatitis can shift large volumes out of the vascular space. Hypotension, tachycardia, oliguria, rising hematocrit, and rising BUN indicate poor perfusion.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 48-year-old client with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back is admitted with suspected gallstone pancreatitis. Pain 9/10, persistent vomiting. options_or_rows_en.text: Prepare large-bore IV access and administer prescribed isotonic fluid resuscitation.
Monitor response: blood pressure, lung sounds, oxygenation, urine output, BUN/hematocrit, and calcium.
Apply oxygen and raise the head of the bed while reassessing respiratory effort.
Assess airway, breathing, circulation, pain, abdomen, mental status, and urine output trend.
Activate rapid response/provider notification for hypotension, hypoxemia, and oliguria.

rationale_en: The nurse first verifies ABC and perfusion status, supports breathing, escalates for shock, implements prescribed resuscitation, and evaluates for both improved perfusion and fluid overload/electrolyte complications.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-health rehabilitation visit
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 74-year-old, 3 weeks after discharge from inpatient rehabilitation following a left-hemisphere stroke. The client has expressive aphasia and right-sided facial weakness. The client points accurately to pictures and follows one-step commands.
Caregiver reports giving thin water through a straw because the client “hates thickener.” During lunch, the nurse notes coughing after sips of water, a wet-sounding voice, and food pocketed in the right cheek. The client has lost 4 lb since discharge.
Two outpatient speech therapy visits were canceled because the caregiver could not arrange transportation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Coughing after thin water through a straw
Client points accurately to pictures
Two speech therapy visits canceled because transportation was unavailable
Food pocketed in the right cheek
Lost 4 lb since discharge
Care-coordination barrier
Swallowing or nutrition risk
Communication support

rationale_en: Coughing with thin liquids, wet voice, pocketing, and weight loss indicate swallowing/nutrition risk. Accurate picture pointing and one-step command following show useful communication supports. Missed speech therapy due to transportation is a care-coordination barrier.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin-risk huddle note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Resident is 86 years old, admitted for long-term care after functional decline. Uses wheelchair for meals and needs moderate assistance to turn in bed.
Sacral skin has blanchable redness after sitting for breakfast. Both heels rest directly on mattress when supine.
Brief was wet at 0700; loose stool noted near sacrum. Resident reports, “My bottom burns sometimes.”
Ate 25% of meals yesterday and drank little fluid.

options_or_rows_en.text: Resident cannot reposition independently in bed.
Brief is wet; loose stool noted near sacrum.
Ate 25% of meals yesterday and drank little fluid.
Both heels rest directly on the mattress.
Sacral redness blanches when gently pressed.
Nutrition/hydration
Moisture/skin protection
Pressure/mobility

rationale_en: Immobility and heel pressure require offloading and repositioning; moisture requires incontinence care and barrier protection; low intake requires nutrition/hydration support; blanchable redness requires pressure relief and monitoring, not staging as an open injury.

options_or_rows_en.text: Consult the dietitian for poor intake and protein/calorie needs.
Provide prompt incontinence care and apply moisture-barrier product as ordered or per protocol.
Massage reddened areas over bony prominences each shift.
Offload the heels so they do not rest directly on the mattress.

options_or_rows_en.text: Explain in plain language that an abnormal screening result needs diagnostic follow-up.
Send another home stool test first because the client is afraid.
Ask the client whether the daughter may help with scheduling and transportation.
Confirm the appointment date, transportation plan, and a number to call if the ride falls through.
Offer qualified interpreter services for health information and scheduling.
Ask what the client has heard about the test and what worries them most.

rationale_en: The nurse should explain the abnormal result in plain language, use qualified interpreter services if needed, assess concerns, obtain permission before involving family, confirm scheduling and transportation, and provide clear call-back instructions. Repeating the screening test, using family as an interpreter by default, or documenting refusal without assessment undermines follow-up.

stem_en: Place the nursing actions in the safest order for the first assisted transfer today.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is postoperative day 6 after internal fixation of a right hip fracture. Weight bearing as tolerated with walker.
PT note: requires one-person assist for transfers; ambulated 15 ft yesterday with walker, stopped due to fatigue. Goal today: chair for meals and short assisted walks before meals as tolerated.
Nursing note: attempted to stand twice without calling. Reports dizziness when first sitting up. Pain 7/10 before therapy and 3/10 about 45 minutes after prescribed pain medicine.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply the gait belt snugly over clothing while the client is sitting upright.
Clear the path, lock the bed and wheelchair, place nonskid footwear, and position the walker.
Use the gait belt and walker to assist the client to stand and pivot to the chair.
Review the activity order, pain level, recent analgesic timing, and dizziness history.
Assist the client to sit at the bedside and assess dizziness before standing.

rationale_en: Before mobility, the nurse reviews activity orders and pain/orthostatic risk, prepares the environment and assistive devices, has the client sit and assess dizziness, then applies the gait belt and transfers with assistance.

stem_en: Which statements by the client indicate understanding of fall-prevention teaching? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is postoperative day 6 after internal fixation of a right hip fracture. Weight bearing as tolerated with walker.
PT note: requires one-person assist for transfers; ambulated 15 ft yesterday with walker, stopped due to fatigue. Goal today: chair for meals and short assisted walks before meals as tolerated.
Nursing note: attempted to stand twice without calling. Reports dizziness when first sitting up. Pain 7/10 before therapy and 3/10 about 45 minutes after prescribed pain medicine.

options_or_rows_en.text: “At home, I can hold on to furniture instead of using the walker.”
“I will sit at the bedside and tell staff if I feel dizzy before walking.”
“I should wear the nonskid socks and use the walker every time I get up.”
“I will press the call light before I stand, even if I only need the bathroom.”
“I will keep my phone, water, and call light where I can reach them.”
“If I feel embarrassed asking for help, I will move quickly so I do not bother anyone.”

rationale_en: Effective teaching includes calling before standing, using nonskid footwear and the walker, pausing for dizziness, and keeping needed items within reach. Rushing to the bathroom or using furniture instead of the walker is unsafe.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 data, classify each finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium 7.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves and widening QRS
Phosphorus 8.4 mg/dL with calcium 6.8 mg/dL and fingertip tingling
Creatinine increased from 1.1 to 2.4 mg/dL with 80 mL urine output in 4 hours
Uric acid decreased from 9.2 to 4.1 mg/dL after rasburicase
Temperature 37.4°C
LDH increased from 1,450 to 3,200 U/L
Expected treatment effect
Danger cue consistent with TLS
Not a priority TLS cue

rationale_en: TLS causes rapid release of intracellular potassium and phosphate, secondary hypocalcemia, kidney injury, and high cell-turnover markers. The falling uric acid is expected after rasburicase and should not reassure the nurse when the client has dangerous hyperkalemia and oliguria. A temperature of 37.4°C is not the priority cue in this pattern.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 data, classify each finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response data
options_or_rows_en.content: After IV calcium gluconate, insulin with dextrose, and initiation of dialysis, the client is more alert. HR 96/min, BP 126/78 mm Hg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air. ECG shows narrowing QRS complexes and less prominent T waves. Labs: potassium 5.4 mEq/L, phosphorus 6.2 mg/dL, calcium 7.5 mg/dL, creatinine 2.0 mg/dL, uric acid 2.8 mg/dL. Urine output is 110 mL over the past 4 hours.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium 7.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves and widening QRS
Phosphorus 8.4 mg/dL with calcium 6.8 mg/dL and fingertip tingling
Creatinine increased from 1.1 to 2.4 mg/dL with 80 mL urine output in 4 hours

Temperature 37.4°C
LDH increased from 1,450 to 3,200 U/L
Expected treatment effect
Danger cue consistent with TLS
Not a priority TLS cue

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options.text: it creates immediate risk for ventricular dysrhythmia and cardiac arrest
it confirms that chemotherapy should be continued without interruption
it means the client is no longer at risk for kidney injury
it indicates the client has developed an infectious emergency
evidence that the ECG changes are unrelated to TLS
a sign that dialysis is no longer needed regardless of potassium
an expected effect of rasburicase, not resolution of the emergency
a reason to restart allopurinol immediately

rationale_en: The priority is hyperkalemia with ECG changes because it threatens circulation immediately. Hypocalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, and kidney injury are serious, but the widened QRS and loss of P waves point to imminent dysrhythmia. Rasburicase can lower uric acid even while other TLS derangements worsen.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Verify continuous cardiac monitoring and obtain/print the current ECG tracing.
Prepare the client for temporary dialysis catheter placement and emergent hemodialysis.
Administer IV calcium gluconate as prescribed to stabilize the myocardium.
Administer regular insulin with dextrose as prescribed to shift potassium into cells.
Monitor blood glucose, repeat potassium results, ECG pattern, and strict intake/output after interventions begin.

rationale_en: With severe hyperkalemia and ECG changes, the nurse first ensures continuous ECG surveillance, then administers prescribed calcium to stabilize the myocardium, then prescribed insulin/dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly. Dialysis preparation follows because dialysis removes potassium definitively. Ongoing monitoring evaluates safety and response, including hypoglycemia after insulin.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

rationale_en: Eighty mL divided by 4 hours equals 20 mL/hr. This is below the common adult minimum benchmark of about 30 mL/hr and supports oliguria in the setting of TLS-associated kidney injury.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: QRS complexes are narrowing and T waves are less prominent
Potassium decreased from 7.1 to 5.4 mEq/L
Calcium remains low at 7.5 mg/dL
Creatinine remains elevated at 2.0 mg/dL
Urine output is 110 mL over the past 4 hours
Uric acid is 2.8 mg/dL after rasburicase
Immediate escalation
Improving response
Continue close monitoring

rationale_en: The ECG pattern and potassium are improving, and uric acid remains controlled after rasburicase. However, calcium, creatinine, and urine output have not normalized, so the nurse should continue close monitoring and report worsening trends. None of the Stage 3 findings listed requires immediate escalation because the immediate arrhythmia markers are improving.

options_or_rows_en.text: Activate the sepsis/rapid response pathway and communicate suspected urinary source with obstruction.
Administer prescribed broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly.
Prepare for vasopressor support and urgent source-control procedures if hypotension persists.
Ensure blood and urine cultures and lactate are obtained or verified without delaying antibiotics.
Administer prescribed isotonic fluid resuscitation and monitor lung sounds and perfusion.

rationale_en: The nurse escalates immediately, verifies cultures/lactate as part of the sepsis bundle, gives antibiotics promptly, supports perfusion with ordered fluids, and anticipates vasopressors/source control when shock persists.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial clinic visit
options_or_rows_en.content: A 47-year-old client assigned female at birth comes to a community clinic for the first visit in 9 years. The client has no acute symptoms and says, 'I feel fine, so I do not think I need tests.' Cervix is intact. No personal history of cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, or immunocompromise. No first-degree relative with colorectal cancer. Last cervical cancer screening was 9 years ago. The client has never had colorectal cancer screening or a mammogram. Primary language is Mandarin; the client answers short English questions but hesitates with medical words.

options.text: the client's adult relative as the default interpreter
a warning that refusal means cancer is likely
all possible guidelines without checking understanding

rationale_en: A qualified interpreter and plain-language options support informed, feasible participation without coercion or jargon.

Schedule follow-up colonoscopy and explain that a positive screening test needs diagnostic evaluation.

rationale_en: A positive FIT is a screening result that requires diagnostic follow-up, usually colonoscopy; it does not diagnose cancer by itself.
