stem_en: Complete the nurse's therapeutic response.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home health visit
options_or_rows_en.content: A 68-year-old client with Parkinson disease lives with a spouse who provides most daily care. The client needs help bathing, dressing, and transferring but can feed self slowly. During a home visit, the spouse says, 'I am awake most nights. I have not seen my friends in months. Yesterday I yelled when he froze in the doorway. I hate that I did that.' The spouse denies wanting to harm the client but says, 'I am afraid I will lose control if nothing changes.'

options.text: focus only on the client's motor symptoms
tell the spouse that yelling is unacceptable and move on
acknowledge the spouse's exhaustion without judgment
assess immediate safety and arrange concrete support options
ask the daughter to take over all care immediately
advise the spouse to wait until the next routine visit

rationale_en: The response should validate distress, assess safety, and move toward specific supports such as respite or shared care.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's therapeutic response.

options_or_rows_en.title: Family update
options_or_rows_en.content: The adult daughter can visit on Saturdays but is unsure how to help. The spouse says, 'I do not want strangers in the home, but I cannot keep doing this alone.' The client says, 'I feel like a burden.' The nurse has information about respite care, adult day programs, caregiver support groups, and home safety evaluation.

Preserved client ability or protective cue
Caregiver strain cue

rationale_en: Sleep loss, guilt after yelling, and social isolation are caregiver strain cues. Self-feeding and denial of intent to harm are protective but do not eliminate strain.

rationale_en: Work schedule, poor fit, and decreased sensation create barriers or risks. Family involvement and intact skin are strengths for planning.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's interpretation of the day-3 finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation admission
options_or_rows_en.content: A 54-year-old client is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation after an incomplete spinal cord injury. The client uses a wheelchair for most mobility and reports decreased sensation over the sacrum and heels. Braden score is 13. Skin is intact on admission except for blanchable redness on the coccyx after sitting for 3 hours. The client says, 'I do not feel pain there, so I thought it was fine.' Appetite is fair, and occasional urinary leakage is reported.

options.text: offload pressure and update the prevention plan
massage the reddened area to improve circulation
continue the same sitting schedule because the skin is intact

rationale_en: Intact, nonblanchable redness over a pressure point is consistent with stage 1 pressure injury and requires pressure relief, not massage.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's interpretation of the day-3 finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin rounds note
options_or_rows_en.content: On day 3, the nurse finds a 3 cm area of nonblanchable redness over the sacrum. The skin is intact and warm. Heels are boggy but intact. The client has been sitting for long therapy sessions and says, 'The donut cushion my neighbor gave me feels comfortable.'

options.text: offload pressure and update the prevention plan
massage the reddened area to improve circulation
continue the same sitting schedule because the skin is intact

rationale_en: Intact, nonblanchable redness over a pressure point is consistent with stage 1 pressure injury and requires pressure relief, not massage.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation admission
options_or_rows_en.content: A 54-year-old client is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation after an incomplete spinal cord injury. The client uses a wheelchair for most mobility and reports decreased sensation over the sacrum and heels. Braden score is 13. Skin is intact on admission except for blanchable redness on the coccyx after sitting for 3 hours. The client says, 'I do not feel pain there, so I thought it was fine.' Appetite is fair, and occasional urinary leakage is reported.

options_or_rows_en.text: Keep the client on bedrest for the rest of the rehabilitation stay.
Relieve pressure from the sacrum and request an appropriate pressure-redistribution surface.
Rub lotion firmly over the sacrum every shift.
Place the client back on the donut cushion because it feels comfortable.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's teaching strategy.

options_or_rows_en.title: Primary care follow-up
options_or_rows_en.content: A 62-year-old client with newly diagnosed hypothyroidism returns for follow-up after 6 weeks of treatment. The client brings three medicine bottles in a plastic bag and says, 'I take the thyroid pill when I remember. The label has too many words.' The client reads at an elementary level, uses a basic cell phone, and prefers Spanish but understands some English. The client says breakfast is at 6 AM before work and asks, 'Can you just tell my nephew what to do?'

options.text: a complex chart of thyroid physiology
the full pharmacy label read aloud once in English
a plain-language picture schedule in the client's preferred language
teach-back using the client's own words

rationale_en: Plain-language, language-concordant visual teaching plus teach-back addresses health literacy and confirms practical understanding.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's teaching strategy.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse call and lab note
options_or_rows_en.content: At a nurse call 4 weeks later, the client says, 'The picture schedule helped. I take the thyroid pill on the table when I wake up, then I eat later at work. I missed two doses when the refill ran out.' TSH is improved but still above goal. The client gives permission for the nephew to help set up refills but wants to speak directly with the nurse about health decisions.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-Health Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 74-year-old client with moderate dementia lives with the spouse in a two-story home. The spouse says, "He wakes up at night looking for his mother and tries to leave the house. Yesterday I yelled and held his wrists to stop him. I am scared I will lose control." The spouse reports sleeping 3 to 4 hours per night and has not attended a caregiver support group.

options.text: sleep deprivation and escalating wandering behavior
lack of interest in the client's diagnosis
teaching the spouse to physically block exits
delaying referrals until bruising is observed

rationale_en: The spouse describes exhaustion, fear of losing control, and unsafe escalation while managing nighttime wandering. Respite and safety planning are the priority because they address both caregiver strain and immediate risk.

The spouse reports sleeping 3 to 4 hours per night and has not attended a caregiver support group.

options_or_rows_en.text: "Most families go through this, so try not to feel guilty."
"You are exhausted and trying to keep him safe. Let's talk about what happened and what support is needed today."
"You must promise that you will never grab him again, or he may need to be placed immediately."
"Because there are no bruises, we can focus on medication reminders instead."

rationale_en: The best response validates distress without excusing unsafe behavior, invites assessment, and moves toward immediate support and safety planning.

stem_en: The spouse says, "I feel like a terrible person. I cannot keep doing this." Which response by the nurse is best?

options_or_rows_en.content: The agency can arrange a social work visit, respite aide hours, a home-safety occupational therapy consult, and referral to an adult day program. The provider can review nighttime agitation and sleep disruption. The adult son lives 45 minutes away and is willing to join care-planning calls if the client and spouse agree.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-Health Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 74-year-old client with moderate dementia lives with the spouse in a two-story home. The spouse says, "He wakes up at night looking for his mother and tries to leave the house. Yesterday I yelled and held his wrists to stop him. I am scared I will lose control." The spouse reports sleeping 3 to 4 hours per night and has not attended a caregiver support group.

options_or_rows_en.text: Assess whether the client and spouse are safe to remain at home today.
Request provider follow-up for nighttime agitation, sleep disruption, and missed medication doses.
Ask permission to involve the adult son and social worker in a same-week support plan.
Teach a written and picture-based wandering response plan, including whom to call during escalation.
Re-enable or replace the smoke alarm and remove obvious tripping hazards with the spouse's permission.

rationale_en: The nurse first determines immediate safety, then addresses obvious hazards, mobilizes supports, requests clinical follow-up, and teaches the practical plan for continuity.

stem_en: Complete the statement about the priority teaching need.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 58-year-old client arrives for follow-up 2 weeks after starting glipizide in addition to metformin. The client says, 'My wife reads the bottles for me. I take the small white pill when I feel shaky because I think it lowers the sugar faster.' The client reports skipping breakfast on workdays and keeping medications in a pillbox without labels. The spouse says, 'I am afraid to leave him alone because he does not know what to do if his sugar drops.'

rationale_en: Shakiness with low fasting readings indicates hypoglycemia risk. The immediate teaching priority is to use fast-acting carbohydrate rather than taking more glucose-lowering medication.

stem_en: Complete the statement about the priority teaching need.

options_or_rows_en.content: The provider asks the nurse to reinforce medication safety, assess barriers to meal consistency, and identify whether the spouse needs additional support. The clinic has a diabetes educator, dietitian, social worker, and bilingual printed teaching materials.

rationale_en: Shakiness with low fasting readings indicates hypoglycemia risk. The immediate teaching priority is to use fast-acting carbohydrate rather than taking more glucose-lowering medication.

Needs focused teaching
Expected or reassuring

rationale_en: Misusing glipizide and skipping meals require focused safety teaching. Recurrent fasting readings below 70 mg/dL require prompt provider follow-up because the medication plan may need adjustment. Intact foot skin and a stated learning preference are useful baseline findings.

stem_en: Place the nurse's teaching actions in the best order.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 58-year-old client arrives for follow-up 2 weeks after starting glipizide in addition to metformin. The client says, 'My wife reads the bottles for me. I take the small white pill when I feel shaky because I think it lowers the sugar faster.' The client reports skipping breakfast on workdays and keeping medications in a pillbox without labels. The spouse says, 'I am afraid to leave him alone because he does not know what to do if his sugar drops.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask the client to teach back the low-blood-glucose action plan using the labeled pillbox and picture sheet.
Use plain language and pictures to explain when to take glipizide and what to do if a meal is skipped.
Ask the client to show how each medication is taken on a usual workday.
Clarify which self-management task the client most wants to handle without the spouse's help.
Document the client's remaining barriers and arrange follow-up with the diabetes educator and dietitian.

rationale_en: The nurse first assesses the client's actual routine, then identifies a patient-centered goal, teaches using appropriate literacy supports, verifies understanding with teach-back, and finally documents barriers and coordinates follow-up.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 58-year-old client arrives for follow-up 2 weeks after starting glipizide in addition to metformin. The client says, 'My wife reads the bottles for me. I take the small white pill when I feel shaky because I think it lowers the sugar faster.' The client reports skipping breakfast on workdays and keeping medications in a pillbox without labels. The spouse says, 'I am afraid to leave him alone because he does not know what to do if his sugar drops.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Tell the spouse to continue managing all medications because the client has low health literacy.
Request a dietitian consult for a simple workday breakfast and snack plan.
Ask the provider whether recurrent low fasting readings require a medication-plan review.
Refer the client to the diabetes educator for medication and glucose-monitoring skills.
Offer social work support for caregiver strain and access to medication-labeling resources.

rationale_en: The plan should build self-management skills, address recurrent low readings with the provider, support practical meal planning, and respond to caregiver strain. The nurse should not reinforce total dependence on the spouse or rely only on written instructions.

stem_en: Complete the statement about safe discharge communication.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 34-year-old client arrives with right wrist pain after "falling at home." The partner answers most questions, keeps a hand on the client's shoulder, and says, "We are in a hurry. Just wrap it." The client avoids eye contact. The nurse notes bruising on the upper arm in different stages of healing. The client is alert, oriented, and reports pain 5/10. Distal pulse, movement, and sensation are intact.

options.text: objective documentation of the injury assessment
asking whether the client has a safe contact
sending domestic violence resources through the portal or printing obvious instructions
a discreet safety card and neutral follow-up wording
a voicemail on the shared phone
a confrontation with the partner before discharge

rationale_en: When communication is monitored, routine electronic messages or obvious printed resources can increase danger. A discreet resource and neutral follow-up plan better protect confidentiality while preserving access to help.

stem_en: Complete the statement about safe discharge communication.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinic Resources
options_or_rows_en.content: A social worker and domestic violence advocate can speak with the client privately. The clinic can document injuries objectively, avoid placing sensitive resources in the after-visit summary, use a neutral follow-up reason, and provide a small safety card that fits inside a shoe. The partner is pacing near the front desk and asking when the client will be done.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 34-year-old client arrives with right wrist pain after "falling at home." rationale_en: Two once-daily medications require 2 tablets per day. For 7 days, 2 × 7 = 14 tablets total.

stem_en: Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic after the client says, "I'm not ready to leave"?

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 34-year-old client arrives with right wrist pain after "falling at home." The partner answers most questions, keeps a hand on the client's shoulder, and says, "We are in a hurry. Just wrap it." The client avoids eye contact. The nurse notes bruising on the upper arm in different stages of healing. The client is alert, oriented, and reports pain 5/10. Distal pulse, movement, and sensation are intact.

options_or_rows_en.text: "I will bring your partner in so we can hear both sides of the story."
"You are the expert on your safety. I am concerned about what happened, and we can talk about options that do not require you to decide today."
"You should promise me you will call the police as soon as you get home."
"If you do not leave now, there is nothing useful we can do for you today."

rationale_en: The therapeutic response validates autonomy, expresses concern, and offers options without pressuring the client to leave immediately. Pressuring, blaming, or involving the partner can increase danger and reduce trust.

stem_en: Complete the exposure-management statement.

options_or_rows_en.title: Incident Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A nurse sustains a puncture wound to the left index finger while clearing a tray after giving an adult vaccine. The safety device on the used needle had not been activated. The source client has left the injection area but registration has the client's name and phone number. The nurse says, "It is barely bleeding. I will finish the line first."

options.text: wash the puncture site with soap and water
squeeze the finger until bleeding stops
contact employee health for risk assessment
discard all documentation to protect the source client's privacy
wait until symptoms develop before reporting

rationale_en: A needlestick is a percutaneous exposure. The nurse should perform immediate wound care and promptly contact employee health for risk assessment and recommendations.

options_or_rows_en.content: The sharps container at one station is above the fill line. The exposed nurse completed a hepatitis B vaccine series years ago but does not know whether a post-vaccination titer was documented.

options_or_rows_en.title: Incident Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A nurse sustains a puncture wound to the left index finger while clearing a tray after giving an adult vaccine. The safety device on the used needle had not been activated. The source client has left the injection area but registration has the client's name and phone number. options_or_rows_en.text: Place an approved sharps container at each injection station.
Replace sharps containers before they exceed the fill line.
Require staff to recap needles before carrying them to the shared container.
Teach staff to activate the safety device immediately after use as designed.
Use nonpunitive reporting to identify workflow barriers and near misses.

rationale_en: Point-of-use disposal, timely replacement of full containers, correct safety-device activation, and nonpunitive reporting address system causes of sharps injuries. Recapping and storing used syringes on trays increase risk.

One staff member says some needles are recapped when the safety device is hard to activate quickly. The exposed nurse completed a hepatitis B vaccine series years ago but does not know whether a post-vaccination titer was documented.

options_or_rows_en.text: Place an approved sharps container at each injection station.
Replace sharps containers before they exceed the fill line.
Require staff to recap needles before carrying them to the shared container.
Teach staff to activate the safety device immediately after use as designed.
Use nonpunitive reporting to identify workflow barriers and near misses.

rationale_en: Point-of-use disposal, timely replacement of full containers, correct safety-device activation, and nonpunitive reporting address system causes of sharps injuries. Recapping and storing used syringes on trays increase risk.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinic Intake Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 52-year-old client comes to a community clinic for a school volunteer clearance form. The client says, "I feel fine, so I do not understand why I need all these tests. I cannot miss work for a colonoscopy." The client has not had a primary care visit in 7 years. options_or_rows_en.text: Ask what the client already knows and what worries the client most about screening and vaccines.
Schedule referrals or appointments and document barriers that still need follow-up.
Prioritize one or two overdue preventive actions that the client is willing to complete first.
Ask the client to teach back the selected plan, including when and where each step will happen.
Use large-print, plain-language materials to explain options, benefits, and next steps.

rationale_en: The nurse first assesses understanding and concerns, then gives accessible information, helps the client choose a realistic starting plan, verifies understanding with teach-back, and finally schedules and documents follow-up needs.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use teach-back to confirm how the client will complete the selected screening steps.
Offer a stool-based colorectal cancer screening kit with picture-based instructions.

rationale_en: The nurse should reduce access and literacy barriers, coordinate reachable screening, address vaccine concerns, and confirm understanding. Screening options should not be falsely limited, and vaccines do not need to wait for completion of cancer screening.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 76-year-old client is postoperative day 5 after open reduction and internal fixation of a left hip fracture. The client can stand with assistance and a walker but tires quickly. The client says, 'I avoid drinking because I do not want to wet the bed.' The adult daughter says, 'I work nights and I am scared Mom will fall if she tries to go to the bathroom alone.'

options.text: limited mobility and moisture from urinary leakage
keeping the head of bed elevated at all times
limiting oral fluids until urinary leakage stops
scheduled repositioning, moisture management, and pressure redistribution

rationale_en: Limited mobility, moisture, and early sacral color changes place the client at risk for pressure injury. Prevention should combine repositioning, moisture control, and pressure redistribution rather than fluid restriction or constant head elevation.

stem_en: The client needs to transfer from bed to a chair with a walker. Place the nursing actions in the best order.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 76-year-old client is postoperative day 5 after open reduction and internal fixation of a left hip fracture. The client can stand with assistance and a walker but tires quickly. The client says, 'I avoid drinking because I do not want to wet the bed.' The adult daughter says, 'I work nights and I am scared Mom will fall if she tries to go to the bathroom alone.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply nonskid footwear and a gait belt.
Assist the client to sit at the bedside and assess dizziness before standing.
Ensure the call light and needed items are within reach after the client sits.
Confirm the chair is locked, close to the bed, and positioned on the client's stronger side when possible.
Cue the client to push from the bed, stand, grasp the walker, and pivot toward the chair.

rationale_en: The nurse first prepares the environment, then prepares the client, assesses tolerance in sitting, assists the stand-pivot transfer safely, and finally restores access to the call light and needed items.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the active MAR and sees loperamide 2 mg PO PRN for diarrhea. Which action is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.text: Give loperamide only after the stool specimen is collected so the medication does not interfere with testing.
Withhold the loperamide and notify the provider that the order should be discontinued or held for suspected CDI.
Administer loperamide and increase oral fluids before contacting the provider.
Request diphenoxylate-atropine instead because it is stronger than loperamide for severe diarrhea.
Administer loperamide now because reducing stool frequency will prevent dehydration and skin injury.

rationale_en: The client has suspected CDI with fever, leukocytosis, abdominal distension, recent high-risk antibiotics, and frequent watery stools. Antimotility agents can worsen toxin retention and may contribute to ileus or toxic megacolon, so the nurse should hold loperamide and notify the provider.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: Hospital days 17–18 discharge planning
options_or_rows_en.content: After three full days of oral vancomycin, she has two to three semi-formed stools per day, no cramping, improving appetite, and no fever for 48 hours. Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 76, BP 126/74, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% on room air. Labs: WBC 11,200/µL, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, lactate 0.8 mmol/L. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended. Discharge plan: complete a 10-day oral vancomycin course with 6 days remaining at home, continue prednisone, keep methotrexate held pending rheumatology follow-up, home PT, and no repeat C. difficile test if symptoms remain resolved.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.text: Delay notifying environmental services until the client has a negative follow-up stool test.
Use alcohol-based hand rub as the preferred method because gloves were worn during care.
Initiate contact precautions while CDI is suspected rather than waiting for a confirmed result.
Send an unformed stool specimen for C. difficile testing per facility protocol once ordered.
Perform hand hygiene with soap and water after removing gloves and before leaving the room.
Place a dedicated commode and dedicated assessment equipment in the room.

rationale_en: Suspected CDI requires early contact precautions, soap-and-water hand hygiene, appropriate testing of unformed stool, and dedicated equipment to reduce environmental spread. Alcohol-based hand rub is not the preferred exit hand hygiene for CDI, and environmental cleaning should not be delayed.

options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1: Hospital day 14, 0700 assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Night shift reports five loose watery stools from 2200 to 0600, increasing in frequency; the client reports diffuse cramping, malaise, and poor appetite. Stool is watery, greenish-brown, foul-smelling, and without visible blood. Vital signs: T 38.1 °C, HR 94, BP 108/62, RR 18, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented but fatigued. Skin turgor is decreased; mucous membranes are dry and lips are cracked. Abdomen is distended with diffuse tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds, and no guarding, rebound tenderness, or rigidity. Perianal skin is erythematous and moist but intact. Active MAR includes loperamide 2 mg PO PRN for diarrhea.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1: Laboratory data
options_or_rows_en.content: Morning labs: WBC 21,400/µL with 84% neutrophils and 6% bands (WBC was 8,200/µL two days ago), hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, platelets 204,000/µL, sodium 136 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (was 4.1 two days ago), chloride 98 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.1), eGFR 38 mL/min, glucose 142 mg/dL, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, albumin 2.4 g/dL, CRP 68 mg/L. Stool testing has not yet been ordered.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1: Provider orders after SBAR
options_or_rows_en.content: The nurse initiates contact precautions for suspected CDI, holds loperamide, and pages the provider. Orders: C. difficile testing on an unformed stool specimen per facility protocol; normal saline 250 mL bolus then 125 mL/hr; potassium chloride 40 mEq IV over 4 hours; discontinue/hold loperamide; hold methotrexate pending rheumatology input; continue prednisone 7.5 mg daily; begin oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily while awaiting results. The first oral vancomycin dose is given at 0900. Soap-and-water hand hygiene, dedicated equipment, strict intake/output, stool count, perianal barrier cream, and sporicidal environmental cleaning are implemented.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 2: Hospital day 15 confirmed CDI
options_or_rows_en.content: C. difficile testing is positive overnight. At 0700, the client reports six watery stools overnight, still with cramping but slightly improved. Vital signs: T 37.8 °C, HR 88, BP 118/70, RR 16, SpO₂ 97%. Labs: WBC 18,600/µL, potassium 3.6 mEq/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, BUN 24 mg/dL, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, lactate 1.2 mmol/L. Abdomen is mildly less distended; tenderness is diffuse but improved; bowel sounds active; no guarding, rebound tenderness, rigidity, hypotension, altered mental status, or ileus. Oral vancomycin continues.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 2: Isolation and environmental plan
options_or_rows_en.content: All staff and visitors are instructed to wear gown and gloves for room entry, use dedicated patient-care equipment, and wash hands with soap and water when leaving the room. The nurse explains that alcohol-based hand rub does not reliably remove C. difficile spores. Environmental services initially plans routine quaternary ammonium cleaning; the nurse requests a bleach-based or EPA-registered sporicidal product for high-touch surfaces, the bathroom, and terminal cleaning at discharge.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: Hospital days 17–18 discharge planning
options_or_rows_en.content: After three full days of oral vancomycin, she has two to three semi-formed stools per day, no cramping, improving appetite, and no fever for 48 hours. Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 76, BP 126/74, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% on room air. Labs: WBC 11,200/µL, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, lactate 0.8 mmol/L. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended. Discharge plan: complete a 10-day oral vancomycin course with 6 days remaining at home, continue prednisone, keep methotrexate held pending rheumatology follow-up, home PT, and no repeat C. difficile test if symptoms remain resolved.

options_or_rows_en.text: Delay notifying environmental services until the client has a negative follow-up stool test.
Use alcohol-based hand rub as the preferred method because gloves were worn during care.
Initiate contact precautions while CDI is suspected rather than waiting for a confirmed result.
Send an unformed stool specimen for C. difficile testing per facility protocol once ordered.
Perform hand hygiene with soap and water after removing gloves and before leaving the room.
Place a dedicated commode and dedicated assessment equipment in the room.

rationale_en: Suspected CDI requires early contact precautions, soap-and-water hand hygiene, appropriate testing of unformed stool, and dedicated equipment to reduce environmental spread. Alcohol-based hand rub is not the preferred exit hand hygiene for CDI, and environmental cleaning should not be delayed.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

rationale_en: CDI is a luminal colonic infection. Oral vancomycin is minimally absorbed, allowing high concentrations to remain in the intestinal lumen. IV vancomycin produces systemic levels but does not reliably deliver therapeutic drug into the colonic lumen.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stool frequency decreases gradually while the abdomen is softer and vital signs are stable.
Diarrhea abruptly stops while abdominal distension increases and bowel sounds become absent.
WBC decreases from 21,400/µL to 18,600/µL with improving temperature and blood pressure.
New abdominal rigidity, guarding, and rebound tenderness develop.
Blood pressure falls to 86/50, heart rate rises to 118, and lactate increases to 3.0 mmol/L.
Stools become semi-formed, creatinine returns to baseline, and abdominal pain resolves.
Immediate escalation
Expected improvement

rationale_en: Improvement is gradual and paired with softer abdomen, better vital signs, improving labs, and more formed stool. Sudden cessation of diarrhea with worsening distension, peritoneal signs, hypotension, tachycardia, or rising lactate suggests ileus, toxic megacolon, perforation risk, or shock and requires immediate escalation.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. options_or_rows_en.text: Initiate contact precautions, soap-and-water hand hygiene, and dedicated equipment while CDI is suspected.

Collect and send an unformed stool specimen as ordered using appropriate precautions, then administer ordered fluids, potassium replacement, and oral vancomycin; continue stool counts, intake/output, abdominal checks, and skin protection.
Perform a focused assessment and recognize the CDI risk pattern from new watery diarrhea, fever, leukocytosis, recent antibiotics, immunosuppression, and abdominal distension.
Notify the provider using SBAR and request stool testing, fluids, electrolyte replacement, and CDI-directed therapy.
Hold the PRN loperamide and flag the order for provider review.

rationale_en: The nurse first recognizes the risk pattern and assesses severity. Because CDI may spread before confirmation, infection-control measures begin immediately. The unsafe PRN antimotility medication is held before provider communication. SBAR then obtains orders for testing and treatment; the stool specimen is collected as ordered before or alongside therapy, and ordered fluids, electrolytes, oral vancomycin, monitoring, and skin protection follow.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1: Hospital day 14, 0700 assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Night shift reports five loose watery stools from 2200 to 0600, increasing in frequency; the client reports diffuse cramping, malaise, and poor appetite. Stool is watery, greenish-brown, foul-smelling, and without visible blood. Vital signs: T 38.1 °C, HR 94, BP 108/62, RR 18, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented but fatigued. Skin turgor is decreased; mucous membranes are dry and lips are cracked. Abdomen is distended with diffuse tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds, and no guarding, rebound tenderness, or rigidity. Perianal skin is erythematous and moist but intact. Active MAR includes loperamide 2 mg PO PRN for diarrhea.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1: Laboratory data
options_or_rows_en.content: Morning labs: WBC 21,400/µL with 84% neutrophils and 6% bands (WBC was 8,200/µL two days ago), hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, platelets 204,000/µL, sodium 136 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (was 4.1 two days ago), chloride 98 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.1), eGFR 38 mL/min, glucose 142 mg/dL, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, albumin 2.4 g/dL, CRP 68 mg/L. Stool testing has not yet been ordered.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1: Provider orders after SBAR
options_or_rows_en.content: The nurse initiates contact precautions for suspected CDI, holds loperamide, and pages the provider. Orders: C. difficile testing on an unformed stool specimen per facility protocol; normal saline 250 mL bolus then 125 mL/hr; potassium chloride 40 mEq IV over 4 hours; discontinue/hold loperamide; hold methotrexate pending rheumatology input; continue prednisone 7.5 mg daily; begin oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily while awaiting results. The first oral vancomycin dose is given at 0900. Soap-and-water hand hygiene, dedicated equipment, strict intake/output, stool count, perianal barrier cream, and sporicidal environmental cleaning are implemented.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 2: Hospital day 15 confirmed CDI
options_or_rows_en.content: C. difficile testing is positive overnight. At 0700, the client reports six watery stools overnight, still with cramping but slightly improved. Vital signs: T 37.8 °C, HR 88, BP 118/70, RR 16, SpO₂ 97%. Labs: WBC 18,600/µL, potassium 3.6 mEq/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, BUN 24 mg/dL, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, lactate 1.2 mmol/L. Abdomen is mildly less distended; tenderness is diffuse but improved; bowel sounds active; no guarding, rebound tenderness, rigidity, hypotension, altered mental status, or ileus. Oral vancomycin continues.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 2: Isolation and environmental plan
options_or_rows_en.content: All staff and visitors are instructed to wear gown and gloves for room entry, use dedicated patient-care equipment, and wash hands with soap and water when leaving the room. The nurse explains that alcohol-based hand rub does not reliably remove C. difficile spores. Environmental services initially plans routine quaternary ammonium cleaning; the nurse requests a bleach-based or EPA-registered sporicidal product for high-touch surfaces, the bathroom, and terminal cleaning at discharge.

stem_en: Which discharge teaching statement by the nurse is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use hand sanitizer after toileting because it is less irritating than frequent soap-and-water handwashing.
Restart methotrexate at home as soon as the diarrhea improves so rheumatoid arthritis does not flare.
You do not need a repeat C. difficile test if symptoms have resolved. Finish the oral vancomycin course and call the provider if watery diarrhea, fever, or worsening abdominal pain returns.
A repeat PCR must be negative before you can safely stop contact precautions at home.
Stop oral vancomycin when stools become formed for 24 hours because taking extra doses increases resistance.

rationale_en: Resolution is judged clinically. Repeat C. difficile testing is not recommended when symptoms have resolved because colonization or residual genetic material may persist. The client should complete the prescribed oral vancomycin course, use soap-and-water hand hygiene after toileting, follow the plan for holding methotrexate, and report recurrent watery diarrhea, fever, or worsening abdominal pain.

stem_en: At 1030, warfarin 7.5 mg nightly appears on the home medication list. The INR is 5.2 and the client has active gingival oozing and new ecchymoses. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the scheduled warfarin because the client has a mechanical mitral valve.
Hold the warfarin, verify the hold order or obtain clarification, and notify the provider of the INR and bleeding findings.
Ask the wife whether she wants the warfarin held because she manages the pill organizer at home.
Give half of the warfarin dose and recheck the INR after the medication is absorbed.

rationale_en: The nurse should hold and clarify a high-risk medication when current objective data show likely harm. INR 5.2 with active mucocutaneous bleeding makes another warfarin dose unsafe; notifying the provider preserves the medication-order chain while preventing iatrogenic worsening.

stem_en: At 1030, warfarin 7.5 mg nightly appears on the home medication list. The INR is 5.2 and the client has active gingival oozing and new ecchymoses. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Orders, interventions, and 1200 status
options_or_rows_en.content:
Provider orders: vitamin K 10 mg IV diluted in normal saline and infused over 30 minutes; type and crossmatch 2 units PRBCs; administer 4-factor PCC per weight/INR protocol; hold all anticoagulation; GI consult; urology consult; noncontrast head CT; continue close neurologic and hemodynamic monitoring.
Nursing actions: vitamin K infused by pump over 30 minutes while monitoring for flushing, dyspnea, chest tightness, and hypotension; 4-factor PCC verified with a second nurse and administered per protocol; PRBC transfusion initiated; vital signs every 15 minutes during acute reversal; strict intake/output and hourly urine color assessment; neurologic checks continued. Head CT is negative for intracranial hemorrhage.
1100-1200 status: HR 88, BP 114/70, urine light pink and clearing, INR 3.8, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL after 1 unit PRBC with second unit infusing, abdominal tenderness mild and stable. GI and urology evaluations are pending. Warfarin has not yet been restarted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the scheduled warfarin because the client has a mechanical mitral valve.
Hold the warfarin, verify the hold order or obtain clarification, and notify the provider of the INR and bleeding findings.
Ask the wife whether she wants the warfarin held because she manages the pill organizer at home.
Give half of the warfarin dose and recheck the INR after the medication is absorbed.

rationale_en: The nurse should hold and clarify a high-risk medication when current objective data show likely harm. INR 5.2 with active mucocutaneous bleeding makes another warfarin dose unsafe; notifying the provider preserves the medication-order chain while preventing iatrogenic worsening.

stem_en: During medication reconciliation, the wife reports giving naproxen 220 mg twice in the past 2 days. Which nursing responses are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.text: Teach the client and wife to avoid NSAIDs unless specifically approved by the anticoagulation prescriber.
Tell the wife that over-the-counter medications should be checked with the anticoagulation clinic or prescriber before use.
Instruct the client to stop eating dark green vegetables permanently to prevent future warfarin interactions.
Explain that NSAIDs can increase bleeding risk through platelet inhibition and gastrointestinal mucosal injury, even when the INR does not fully explain the bleeding.
Reassure the family that two doses of naproxen are too few to be clinically relevant.
Document the naproxen dose and timing and notify the provider because this exposure changes the bleeding-risk assessment.

rationale_en: Naproxen exposure is clinically relevant in a client taking warfarin with active bleeding. The nurse should document and escalate the finding, teach avoidance of unapproved NSAIDs, explain the bleeding mechanisms in practical terms, and involve the caregiver who manages medications at home.

options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0900-1030 initial assessment and labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
Presentation: blood on toothbrush, blood-tinged saliva, new large ecchymoses on both forearms and left shin, and a nosebleed last evening that stopped after 10 minutes of direct pressure. Denies headache, confusion, focal weakness, hematuria, melena, hematemesis, and hemoptysis. Reports mild dizziness when standing.
Vital signs: T 36.9 C, HR 88 irregularly irregular, BP 106/64, RR 18, SpO2 95% on room air. Alert and oriented x4.
Assessment: pale skin; slow gingival oozing; no active epistaxis; sternal incision clean and dry; mechanical valve click audible; bibasilar fine crackles unchanged from discharge; abdomen soft and nontender; urine clear yellow.
Labs: INR 5.2, PT 58.4 seconds, aPTT 42 seconds, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (12.8 g/dL at discharge), hematocrit 34%, platelets 188,000/uL, BUN 34 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, albumin 3.0 g/dL. Urinalysis: no blood. Stool guaiac: pending.

options_or_rows_en.title: Provider orders and response
options_or_rows_en.content:
ED provider orders warfarin held pending INR results. Cardiology hospitalist evaluates the client and orders: continue holding warfarin; vitamin K 2.5 mg PO once now; recheck INR in 6 hours; continue IV normal saline at 75 mL/hr; stool guaiac with next bowel movement; neurologic checks every 4 hours.
Nursing actions: fall precautions, telemetry, strict intake and output, soft-bristle oral care, avoidance of IM injections, medication reconciliation, and provider notification of naproxen exposure. Stool guaiac at 1300 is negative. Vital signs remain stable at 1400; gingival oozing has not worsened and urine remains clear yellow.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1800-2200 update
options_or_rows_en.content:
Repeat labs at 1800: INR 4.6, hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL. Vital signs: T 37.0 C, HR 84, BP 118/72, RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air. Provider orders: continue holding warfarin; no additional vitamin K now; recheck INR in the morning.
Teaching completed with client and wife: purpose of warfarin for mechanical valve and atrial fibrillation; target INR 2.5-3.5; keep dietary vitamin K intake consistent rather than eliminating green vegetables; avoid NSAIDs unless specifically approved by the anticoagulation prescriber; call the anticoagulation clinic before starting new medications; report hematuria, melena, hematemesis, persistent epistaxis, heavy gum bleeding, expanding bruises, severe headache, confusion, vision changes, dizziness, fainting, or any fall/head injury.
At 2000: gingival oozing has stopped; no new ecchymoses; urine clear yellow; neurologically intact; HR 82, BP 120/74.

options_or_rows_en.title: New bleeding and stat labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
At 0500, the client reports bright pink urine. Foley bag contains pink to light-red urine without visible clots. Urine output over previous 8 hours: 480 mL.
0600 vital signs: T 37.1 C, HR 98, BP 100/60, RR 20, SpO2 95% on room air. Client reports lightheadedness when sitting up and mild diffuse abdominal discomfort. No headache, confusion, visual changes, or focal neurologic symptoms. Abdomen soft with mild suprapubic and bilateral flank tenderness, no guarding or rebound. Stool guaiac overnight: positive.
0645 stat labs: INR 6.8, PT 76.2 seconds, hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, hematocrit 27.6%, platelets 174,000/uL, BUN 42 mg/dL, creatinine 1.5 mg/dL, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. Urinalysis: large blood, RBCs too numerous to count, no bacteria, no casts.

options_or_rows_en.title: Orders, interventions, and 1200 status
options_or_rows_en.content:
Provider orders: vitamin K 10 mg IV diluted in normal saline and infused over 30 minutes; type and crossmatch 2 units PRBCs; administer 4-factor PCC per weight/INR protocol; hold all anticoagulation; GI consult; urology consult; noncontrast head CT; continue close neurologic and hemodynamic monitoring.
Nursing actions: vitamin K infused by pump over 30 minutes while monitoring for flushing, dyspnea, chest tightness, and hypotension; 4-factor PCC verified with a second nurse and administered per protocol; PRBC transfusion initiated; vital signs every 15 minutes during acute reversal; strict intake/output and hourly urine color assessment; neurologic checks continued. Head CT is negative for intracranial hemorrhage.
1100-1200 status: HR 88, BP 114/70, urine light pink and clearing, INR 3.8, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL after 1 unit PRBC with second unit infusing, abdominal tenderness mild and stable. GI and urology evaluations are pending. Warfarin has not yet been restarted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Teach the client and wife to avoid NSAIDs unless specifically approved by the anticoagulation prescriber.
Tell the wife that over-the-counter medications should be checked with the anticoagulation clinic or prescriber before use.
Instruct the client to stop eating dark green vegetables permanently to prevent future warfarin interactions.
Explain that NSAIDs can increase bleeding risk through platelet inhibition and gastrointestinal mucosal injury, even when the INR does not fully explain the bleeding.
Reassure the family that two doses of naproxen are too few to be clinically relevant.
Document the naproxen dose and timing and notify the provider because this exposure changes the bleeding-risk assessment.

rationale_en: Naproxen exposure is clinically relevant in a client taking warfarin with active bleeding. The nurse should document and escalate the finding, teach avoidance of unapproved NSAIDs, explain the bleeding mechanisms in practical terms, and involve the caregiver who manages medications at home.

stem_en: The provider orders vitamin K 2.5 mg by mouth during Stage 1. Complete the teaching statement the nurse should use to explain the plan.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options.text: minor mucocutaneous bleeding without hemodynamic instability
a mechanical mitral valve that requires ongoing anticoagulation
a contraindication to all vitamin K products
a bioprosthetic valve that never requires anticoagulation
loss of platelet production from vitamin K
overcorrection to a subtherapeutic INR and valve thrombosis risk
immediate hemolysis from oral vitamin K

rationale_en: At Stage 1 the client is bleeding, but the bleeding is mucocutaneous and hemodynamics are stable. Low-dose oral vitamin K provides a controlled partial reversal, which is important because mechanical mitral valves require ongoing anticoagulation and overcorrection can create thrombosis risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Page/call the provider with a concise STAT report of INR, hemoglobin drop, new bleeding, tachycardia, and hypotension.
Position the client supine, raise the legs slightly if tolerated, maintain telemetry, and increase vital signs to every 15 minutes.
Verify IV access and prepare isotonic fluid bolus/transfusion support according to protocol or rapid provider order.
Administer the scheduled metoprolol and furosemide because they are cardiac medications.
Perform a focused neurologic assessment and continue close neurologic monitoring.
Priority action
Unsafe or inappropriate

rationale_en: The client has evolved from minor bleeding to major bleeding with early hemodynamic compromise. The nurse prioritizes escalation, circulation support, preparation for reversal/transfusion, frequent monitoring, and neurologic surveillance while holding medications that may worsen hypovolemia or mask compensation until clarified.

stem_en: The provider orders IV vitamin K and 4-factor PCC for major bleeding. Which nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.text: Dilute vitamin K as ordered and infuse it by pump over the ordered time rather than giving a rapid IV push.
Reassess vital signs, bleeding sites, urine color and output, neurologic status, hemoglobin, and repeat INR as ordered.
Administer 4-factor PCC promptly according to institutional protocol with required verification and monitoring.
Delay PCC until the vitamin K infusion is complete and a repeat INR confirms that the vitamin K has not corrected the coagulopathy.
Verify the orders, allergies, baseline vital signs, IV patency, product availability, and required second-nurse checks before administration.
Monitor during vitamin K infusion for hypotension, flushing, dyspnea, chest tightness, or other hypersensitivity findings.

rationale_en: Appropriate nursing care includes verification before high-risk products, slow diluted IV vitamin K administration with hypersensitivity monitoring, prompt PCC administration per protocol for rapid factor replacement, and close reassessment of bleeding and laboratory response. These actions may overlap in real practice and should not be forced into a single linear sequence.

stem_en: At 1200, the INR is 3.8, hemoglobin is 9.8 g/dL after transfusion support, blood pressure has improved, urine is clearing, head CT is negative, and GI/urology consults are pending. Which handoff statement best reflects the client's current priority?

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.text: Warfarin should be restarted immediately by the nurse to prevent mechanical valve thrombosis.
Because the head CT is negative, neurologic checks and fall precautions are no longer necessary.
The INR is close enough to the target range that routine every-4-hour vital signs are sufficient and hourly urine assessment can stop.
The hemorrhagic crisis is improving, but the client still requires close monitoring for both rebleeding and valve thrombosis as reversal continues.

rationale_en: The trend is improving but not resolved. The client remains in a transition period: bleeding appears controlled but the bleeding source has not been fully evaluated, and ongoing vitamin K effect may push INR below target, increasing mechanical valve thrombosis risk. Restarting anticoagulation is anticipated but is a provider decision after bleeding control.

stem_en: A client is on day 3 of hospitalization after elective hip replacement and has not had a bowel movement. Arrange the nursing interventions from least invasive to most invasive.

options_or_rows_en.text: Increase fluids to 2000–2500 mL/day as tolerated and offer high-fiber foods.
Promote early ambulation and physical activity as cleared by the orthopedic team.
Administer the prescribed stool softener, such as docusate sodium.
Administer the prescribed stimulant laxative if there is no result after 24 hours of the above measures.
Assess the last bowel movement, usual pattern, diet, opioid use, fluid intake, and mobility.

rationale_en: Constipation management begins with assessment and modifiable nonpharmacologic measures, then progresses to prescribed medications. A stimulant laxative is more aggressive than a stool softener and is used if earlier measures are ineffective.

stem_en: A nurse discovers a near-miss medication error: the wrong dose of metoprolol was selected in the automated dispenser, but the error was caught before administration. Place the quality and safety actions in the correct order.

options_or_rows_en.text: Assess the client and confirm that no medication was administered and no client harm occurred.
Notify the charge nurse and the prescribing provider according to facility policy.
Document the clinical assessment in the medical record without referencing the occurrence report.
Participate in a debrief or root-cause review if initiated by the unit manager.
Complete the institutional occurrence or safety report in the electronic reporting system.

rationale_en: A near-miss still requires a safety response. The nurse first confirms no client harm, then notifies appropriate clinical leaders/providers per policy, documents objective clinical assessment in the medical record without mentioning the occurrence report, completes the institutional safety report, and participates in follow-up review if initiated.

stem_en: A client develops fever, chills, and flank pain 15 minutes after a packed red blood cell transfusion is started. Place the nursing actions in the correct order.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stop the transfusion immediately and disconnect the blood tubing at the IV hub.
Obtain blood specimens and a urine sample as required by facility transfusion-reaction protocol.
Maintain IV access and begin infusing 0.9% sodium chloride through new tubing.
Return the blood bag and original tubing to the blood bank with a completed transfusion reaction report.
Document the reaction, interventions, notifications, specimens obtained, and the client's response.
Notify the prescribing provider and charge nurse of the suspected transfusion reaction.

rationale_en: The first priority is to stop exposure to the suspected blood product while preserving venous access with normal saline through new tubing. The nurse then escalates care, returns the implicated product and tubing for investigation, obtains required specimens, and documents the clinical course.

stem_en: Before the client becomes hypotensive and if no contraindications are present, which medications may be anticipated for initial STEMI care? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 12-Lead ECG
options_or_rows_en.content: ST-segment elevation in leads V3-V4.

options_or_rows_en.text: Morphine
Clopidogrel
Warfarin
Nitroglycerin
Aspirin

rationale_en: Initial STEMI care commonly includes immediate antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor. Nitroglycerin may be used for ischemic chest pain if not contraindicated, and morphine may be used for severe or persistent ischemic pain when appropriate. Warfarin is not used for acute STEMI stabilization.

stem_en: Based on the 0800 triage note, which of the following actions should the nurse anticipate as immediate priorities? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800 Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: Client is conscious but agitated. Singed nasal hairs and facial erythema noted. Hoarse voice. Deep partial-thickness burns on the anterior trunk, anterior bilateral arms, and face. Estimated weight is 80 kg.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply ice packs to the burn wounds
Administer prophylactic IV antibiotics
Administer 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask
Prepare for immediate endotracheal intubation
Initiate continuous electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring

rationale_en: The client has facial burns, singed nasal hairs, and a hoarse voice, which are classic signs of inhalation injury and impending airway loss. Preparing for intubation and administering 100% oxygen are top priorities. ECG monitoring is standard for major trauma/burns to monitor for arrhythmias related to hypovolemia or electrolyte shifts.

stem_en: Using the Rule of Nines, calculate the Total Body Surface Area (TBSA) percentage burned. Then, use the Parkland formula (4 mL/kg/%TBSA) to calculate the total fluid volume in mL needed for the first 24 hours. The client weighs 80 kg. Enter the total volume in mL.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800 Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: Client is conscious but agitated. Singed nasal hairs and facial erythema noted. Hoarse voice. Deep partial-thickness burns on the anterior trunk, anterior bilateral arms, and face. Estimated weight is 80 kg.

rationale_en: Rule of nines calculation: Anterior trunk = 18%. Anterior arms = 4.5% + 4.5% = 9%. Face (anterior head) = 4.5%. Total TBSA = 31.5%. Parkland formula = 4 mL x 80 kg x 31.5 = 10,080 mL for the first 24 hours.

stem_en: Using the Rule of Nines, calculate the Total Body Surface Area (TBSA) percentage burned. Then, use the Parkland formula (4 mL/kg/%TBSA) to calculate the total fluid volume in mL needed for the first 24 hours. The client weighs 80 kg. Enter the total volume in mL.

rationale_en: Rule of nines calculation: Anterior trunk = 18%. Anterior arms = 4.5% + 4.5% = 9%. Face (anterior head) = 4.5%. Total TBSA = 31.5%. Parkland formula = 4 mL x 80 kg x 31.5 = 10,080 mL for the first 24 hours.

stem_en: Based on the clinic assessment, the nurse recognizes the blistering rash on the elbows and knees as associated with which condition?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinic Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports severe fatigue and a new itchy, blistering rash on her elbows and knees. Laboratory results reveal microcytic anemia and a positive tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) test.

options_or_rows_en.text: Dermatitis herpetiformis
Contact dermatitis
Systemic lupus erythematosus
Herpes zoster

rationale_en: Dermatitis herpetiformis is a chronic, intensely itchy, blistering skin manifestation of celiac disease. It resolves with a strict gluten-free diet.

stem_en: Based on the clinic assessment, the nurse recognizes the blistering rash on the elbows and knees as associated with which condition?

options_or_rows_en.title: Endoscopy Results
options_or_rows_en.content: A duodenal biopsy confirms the diagnosis of celiac disease with villous atrophy. The nurse is providing dietary teaching to the client.

rationale_en: Clients with celiac disease must strictly avoid gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, rye, and triticale. Oats are frequently cross-contaminated and should be avoided unless certified gluten-free. Quinoa and corn are naturally gluten-free and safe substitutes.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: Client brought in by family. Skin is pale and clammy. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. Client is lethargic but arousable. Family reports the client vomited 'a large bowl' of bright red blood at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Insert two large-bore IV lines.
Administer prescribed IV octreotide.
Assess airway patency and breathing.
Obtain blood for type and crossmatch.

rationale_en: According to the ABCs, assessing the airway and breathing is always the first priority, especially in a lethargic client who is actively vomiting blood. Next, establishing circulation through large-bore IV access is critical for volume resuscitation. Once access is established, obtaining a type and crossmatch is necessary for blood transfusions. Finally, pharmacological interventions like octreotide can be administered to reduce portal pressure and bleeding.

rationale_en: Signs of hypovolemic shock include tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status (lethargy), and delayed capillary refill (greater than 3 seconds). An SpO2 of 94% is within normal limits and not a specific indicator of shock.

options_or_rows_en.text: To correct coagulation abnormalities.
To prevent hepatic encephalopathy.
To decrease portal venous pressure.
To reverse the effects of alcohol intoxication.

rationale_en: Octreotide is a synthetic somatostatin analog that causes splanchnic vasoconstriction, which decreases portal blood flow and pressure, helping to stop bleeding from esophageal varices.

rationale_en: Guillain-Barré syndrome typically presents as ascending, symmetric muscle weakness and absent reflexes. The most life-threatening complication is respiratory failure due to neuromuscular weakness of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles.

stem_en: Which initial nursing actions are the highest priority upon the client's arrival to the ED? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial ED Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Time of symptom onset clearly established as 1 hour ago. Client exhibits right facial droop, right upper extremity drift, and non-fluent speech. Vital signs: BP 180/100 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air.

options_or_rows_en.text: Facilitate a stat non-contrast head CT scan
Administer enteric-coated aspirin 325 mg immediately
Establish at least two large-bore IV lines
Perform a baseline neurologic assessment (e.g., NIHSS)
Assess capillary blood glucose

rationale_en: Initial stroke management requires rapid assessment to determine tPA eligibility. A non-contrast head CT is critical to rule out hemorrhage. Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke symptoms, so blood glucose must be checked immediately. A baseline NIHSS quantifies deficit severity. Large-bore IV access is needed for potential tPA administration. Aspirin is contraindicated until hemorrhage is ruled out and should not be given if tPA is planned.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial ED Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Time of symptom onset clearly established as 1 hour ago. Client exhibits right facial droop, right upper extremity drift, and non-fluent speech. Vital signs: BP 180/100 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 96% on room air.

options_or_rows_en.text: Blood pressure 175/95 mmHg
Platelet count 85,000/mm3
Symptom onset 2.5 hours ago
Gastrointestinal bleeding 10 days ago
Permissible
Contraindication

rationale_en: Alteplase criteria are strict. BP must be < 185/110 mmHg (175/95 is permissible). Platelets must be ≥ 100,000 (85,000 is a contraindication). Time of onset must be < 3-4.5 hours (2.5 hours is permissible). Recent active bleeding, including GI bleed within the last 21 days, is a strict contraindication due to hemorrhage risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer PRN acetaminophen for the headache
Reassure the client that headache is a common side effect
Stop the alteplase infusion immediately
Prepare to administer labetalol IV push

rationale_en: A new, severe headache and decreased level of consciousness during alteplase therapy strongly suggest intracranial hemorrhage, the most dangerous complication of thrombolytic therapy. The infusion must be stopped immediately, and an emergent CT scan obtained.

stem_en: For each immunization, indicate whether it is currently recommended (indicated now) for this 66-year-old client or not indicated at this visit.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record
options_or_rows_en.content: Type 2 diabetes, on metformin. No known immunodeficiency. Immunizations: last Td/Tdap 12 years ago; no influenza vaccine this season; no pneumococcal vaccine; no zoster (shingles) vaccine. Never screened for colorectal cancer. Non-smoker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Seasonal influenza vaccine
Pneumococcal vaccine
Recombinant zoster (shingles) vaccine
Td/Tdap booster
Indicated now
Not indicated at this visit

rationale_en: All four are indicated. Influenza vaccine is recommended annually for nearly all adults and he has not had it this season. Pneumococcal vaccination is recommended for adults age 50 years or older who have not received a pneumococcal conjugate vaccine or whose history is unknown. Recombinant zoster vaccine is recommended for adults 50 and older. A tetanus-containing booster is due because his last dose was 12 years ago (routine interval is every 10 years).

stem_en: The client has never been screened for colorectal cancer and has average risk. Which screening should the nurse expect to be recommended?

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record
options_or_rows_en.content: Type 2 diabetes, on metformin. No known immunodeficiency. Immunizations: last Td/Tdap 12 years ago; no influenza vaccine this season; no pneumococcal vaccine; no zoster (shingles) vaccine. Never screened for colorectal cancer. Non-smoker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Abdominal ultrasound
No colorectal screening is needed until age 75 regardless of prior screening
Colonoscopy (or another approved colorectal screening test)
Serum carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a screening test

rationale_en: Average-risk adults should be screened for colorectal cancer beginning at age 45 and continuing through 75; an unscreened 66-year-old is overdue. Colonoscopy is a recommended option (stool-based tests are alternatives). Ultrasound and CEA are not colorectal cancer screening tests.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record
options_or_rows_en.content: Type 2 diabetes, on metformin. No known immunodeficiency. Immunizations: last Td/Tdap 12 years ago; no influenza vaccine this season; no pneumococcal vaccine; no zoster (shingles) vaccine. Never screened for colorectal cancer. Non-smoker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Live attenuated vaccines (such as MMR or varicella) should be avoided
The inactivated influenza vaccine should be avoided
The tetanus (Tdap) vaccine should be avoided
All vaccines are safe and none should be avoided

rationale_en: Live attenuated vaccines (e.g., MMR, varicella, live nasal influenza) are contraindicated in pregnancy and in significantly immunocompromised clients such as those receiving chemotherapy, because the weakened organism can cause disease. Inactivated vaccines like the injectable influenza and Tdap are not only safe but recommended in pregnancy.

stem_en: Which statements by the client indicate correct understanding of his preventive care teaching? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record
options_or_rows_en.content: Type 2 diabetes, on metformin. No known immunodeficiency. Immunizations: last Td/Tdap 12 years ago; no influenza vaccine this season; no pneumococcal vaccine; no zoster (shingles) vaccine. Never screened for colorectal cancer. Non-smoker.

options_or_rows_en.text: "Keeping my blood sugar controlled lowers my risk of complications."
"Since I feel healthy, I can skip the colorectal cancer screening."
"I should get a flu shot every year."
"The shingles vaccine can help prevent a painful rash and nerve pain."
"Because my last tetanus shot was over 10 years ago, I'm due for a booster."

rationale_en: Annual influenza vaccination, a tetanus booster every 10 years, glycemic control to reduce diabetic complications, and zoster vaccination to prevent shingles and postherpetic neuralgia all reflect correct understanding. Believing that feeling well removes the need for colorectal screening is a misconception, since colorectal cancer is often asymptomatic early.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: 8-10 loose, foul-smelling stools per day. Temperature 38.1 C, mild abdominal cramping. WBC 14,000/mm3. Recently completed clindamycin. Shares a semiprivate room with another client.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use an alcohol-based hand rub only
Rinse hands with water alone
Apply alcohol-based hand rub, then don clean gloves
Wash hands with soap and water

rationale_en: For C. difficile teaching and testing, soap-and-water hand hygiene is preferred after care because spores are not reliably inactivated by alcohol-based hand rubs and mechanical washing helps remove them. Correct glove and gown use remains essential to reduce hand contamination.

stem_en: The nurse observes a student caring for the client. Indicate whether each action is correct or requires correction.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: 8-10 loose, foul-smelling stools per day. Temperature 38.1 C, mild abdominal cramping. WBC 14,000/mm3. Recently completed clindamycin. Shares a semiprivate room with another client.

options_or_rows_en.text: Dons a gown and gloves before entering the room
Uses dedicated (single-client) equipment such as a stethoscope and BP cuff
Cleans the room surfaces with a quaternary-ammonium wipe only
Removes gloves and gown and performs hand hygiene before leaving the room
Requires correction
Correct

rationale_en: Donning gown and gloves before entry, using dedicated equipment, and removing PPE with hand hygiene before leaving are all correct contact-precaution practices. Routine quaternary-ammonium cleaners are not sporicidal; surfaces must be disinfected with a sporicidal agent such as a chlorine-based (bleach) product.

stem_en: For each chest-drainage finding, indicate whether it is expected for this client or requires immediate follow-up.

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tidaling (water level rises/falls with respiration) in the water-seal chamber
Gentle continuous bubbling in the suction-control chamber
Intermittent bubbling in the water-seal chamber
200 mL of bright red drainage in 1 hour
Requires immediate follow-up
Expected

rationale_en: Tidaling reflects pressure changes and can indicate a patent tube. Gentle continuous bubbling in the suction-control chamber is expected with wet suction. Intermittent bubbling in the water-seal chamber can be expected early with a pneumothorax as trapped pleural air evacuates. Bright red drainage of 200 mL/hr is excessive and signals possible hemorrhage requiring immediate provider notification.

Intermittent bubbling in the water-seal chamber can be expected early with a pneumothorax as trapped pleural air evacuates. Bright red drainage of 200 mL/hr is excessive and signals possible hemorrhage requiring immediate provider notification.

stem_en: Two hours later the nurse notes new continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber that was not present before. What does this finding most likely indicate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: The suction pressure is set too low
An air leak in the system or at the insertion site
Re-expansion of the lung is complete
The tube is kinked or obstructed

rationale_en: New or continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber indicates air is entering the closed system — an air leak, either within the system (loose connection) or at the insertion site. The nurse should assess connections and the dressing and locate the leak.

stem_en: At 1330 the chest tube has disconnected from the drainage tubing and the client is short of breath with an SpO2 of 89%. What is the nurse's immediate priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Clamp the chest tube near the insertion site with a padded clamp
Submerge the end of the chest tube in a bottle of sterile water to re-establish a water seal
Lay the client flat and call the rapid response team
Reconnect the tubing after wiping both ends with an alcohol swab

rationale_en: If a chest tube disconnects from the drainage system, the priority is to re-establish a water seal by submerging the open end in sterile water (about 2 cm). This lets pleural air escape while preventing atmospheric air from being drawn back into the pleural space, which would worsen the pneumothorax.
