stem_en: The seal is restored and the system reconnected. Which later finding best indicates that the client's pneumothorax is resolving as expected?

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continuous bubbling returns in the water-seal chamber
Subcutaneous crepitus develops around the insertion site
Tidaling stops and water-seal bubbling ceases, with improved breath sounds and a re-expanded lung on x-ray
The collection chamber fills rapidly with bright red fluid

rationale_en: As a pneumothorax resolves, the pleural air is fully evacuated: bubbling stops, tidaling ceases (lung is re-expanded against the chest wall), breath sounds improve, and the chest x-ray confirms re-expansion. These together indicate the expected outcome.

stem_en: Which findings should the nurse recognize as cues that raise concern for intimate partner violence? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client requesting a written list of her discharge medications
Injuries in various stages of healing
A partner who answers for the client and is reluctant to leave
An explanation of injury that is inconsistent with the findings
A delay of two days before seeking care

rationale_en: Injuries at different healing stages, delayed care, an injury story that does not match the physical findings, and a controlling partner who answers for the client and will not leave are all classic red flags for intimate partner violence. Asking for a medication list is normal, appropriate engagement in care.

stem_en: What is the most appropriate nursing action to screen the client for intimate partner violence?

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: Avoid raising the topic unless the client brings it up first
Find a private moment to interview the client alone, away from the partner
Ask the client about abuse directly in front of the partner
Confront the partner about the suspicious injuries

rationale_en: Screening for IPV must occur privately, with the client alone, so she can answer honestly without fear of the partner. The nurse can use a routine reason (such as a test or specimen) to ask the partner to step out. Asking in front of the partner endangers the client, confronting the partner can escalate violence, and waiting silently misses the chance to offer help.

stem_en: Once alone, the client tearfully discloses that her partner hit her but says, 'I'm not ready to leave him.' What is the nurse's priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: Insist that she leave the partner immediately before discharge
Tell her she must press charges against her partner today
Assess her immediate safety and help develop a safety plan, including resources she can use
Reassure her that the situation will likely improve on its own

rationale_en: The nurse respects the client's autonomy while prioritizing safety: assess immediate danger and collaboratively develop a safety plan and provide resources (hotline, shelter, escape plan). Pressuring her to leave or press charges removes her control, can escalate danger, and may drive her away from help. Falsely reassuring her is unsafe.

stem_en: For each nurse statement or action, indicate whether it is therapeutic/appropriate or non-therapeutic/inappropriate when supporting this client.

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: "This is not your fault, and you did not deserve to be hurt."
"I'm concerned for your safety. Here is a hotline number you can keep."
"Why do you stay with someone who hurts you?"
Document the client's own words and objective findings accurately in the record
Non-therapeutic / inappropriate
Therapeutic / appropriate

rationale_en: Affirming that the abuse is not the client's fault, expressing concern while providing resources, and accurately documenting the client's own words and objective findings are all therapeutic and support safety. Asking 'why do you stay' is judgmental, implies blame, and is non-therapeutic.

stem_en: Match each skin finding to its correct pressure injury stage.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Sacrum: full-thickness loss with visible subcutaneous fat; no bone, tendon, or muscle exposed; small amount of yellow slough. Right heel: intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough. Braden score: 12.

options_or_rows_en.text: Sacrum: full-thickness loss, subcutaneous fat visible, slough present, no bone/tendon/muscle exposed
Right heel: intact skin with non-blanchable redness
Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss, shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough
Stage 2
Stage 3
Stage 1

rationale_en: Stage 3 involves full-thickness skin loss with visible subcutaneous fat and possible slough, but no exposed bone, tendon, or muscle (the sacrum). Stage 1 is intact skin with non-blanchable erythema (the heel). Stage 2 is partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink/red moist wound bed and no slough (the malleolus).

stem_en: Which interventions should the nurse include in the plan of care to prevent further pressure injury? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Sacrum: full-thickness loss with visible subcutaneous fat; no bone, tendon, or muscle exposed; small amount of yellow slough. Right heel: intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough. Braden score: 12.

options_or_rows_en.text: Float the heels off the bed using a pillow under the calves
Keep the head of the bed elevated above 45 degrees at all times
Vigorously massage the reddened heel to improve circulation
Use a pressure-redistribution support surface
Keep skin clean and dry and manage incontinence promptly
Reposition the client at least every 2 hours

rationale_en: Repositioning at least every 2 hours, floating the heels, using a pressure-redistribution surface, and keeping skin clean and dry while managing moisture all reduce sustained pressure and moisture-associated damage. Massaging reddened bony prominences is contraindicated, and keeping the head of bed high increases shear over the sacrum.

stem_en: Given the client's albumin of 2.8 g/dL, poor intake, and weight loss, which nutrition measures best support wound healing? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Sacrum: full-thickness loss with visible subcutaneous fat; no bone, tendon, or muscle exposed; small amount of yellow slough. Right heel: intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough. Braden score: 12.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ensure adequate vitamin C and zinc
Provide adequate calories and hydration
Increase dietary protein intake
Offer oral nutritional supplements between meals
Restrict all dietary fat and protein to reduce metabolic demand

rationale_en: Wound healing requires increased protein for tissue synthesis, vitamin C and zinc as cofactors for collagen formation and immune function, adequate calories and fluids, and oral supplements to bridge poor intake. Restricting protein and fat would deprive the client of substrates essential for healing.

stem_en: Which findings from the initial assessment are most indicative of acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary edema? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tripod positioning
SpO2 of 85% on room air
S3 gallop
Bilateral crackles
Blood pressure of 188/102 mmHg
History of atrial fibrillation

rationale_en: The classic signs of acute pulmonary edema, a life-threatening manifestation of ADHF, include severe dyspnea (leading to tripod positioning), hypoxemia (low SpO2), and diffuse crackles on auscultation due to fluid in the alveoli. An S3 gallop is a hallmark sign of fluid volume overload and failing left ventricular function. While hypertension is a common precipitating factor and atrial fibrillation is a common comorbidity, they are not as specific to the acute fluid shift into the lungs as the other findings.

stem_en: The nurse needs to take immediate action to stabilize the client. Place the following nursing interventions in the correct order of priority.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

options_or_rows_en.text: Place the client in a high-Fowler's position.
Apply supplemental oxygen via a non-rebreather mask.
Administer intravenous furosemide.
Establish intravenous access.

rationale_en: The immediate priorities are to improve oxygenation and reduce the work of breathing. Placing the client upright (high-Fowler's) immediately reduces venous return to the heart and allows for better lung expansion. Applying oxygen addresses the severe hypoxemia. Establishing IV access is necessary before administering IV medications. Finally, administering a potent loop diuretic like furosemide is critical to remove excess fluid, but this requires IV access and follows the immediate stabilization of breathing.

Assessment:

- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

rationale_en: The client's history of severe heart failure, combined with classic signs of fluid overload (S3, edema, crackles, orthopnea) and the reported non-adherence with his diuretic medication, strongly supports the diagnosis of cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The lack of fever or productive cough makes infection less likely, and the bilateral nature of the findings is not typical for a pulmonary embolism.

stem_en: After administering 80 mg of intravenous furosemide, which assessment finding would be the most reliable indicator that the medication has been effective?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client's heart rate decreases to 110 beats/minute.
The client has a urine output of 400 mL in the first hour.
The client's blood pressure decreases to 150/88 mmHg.
The client reports a decrease in anxiety.

rationale_en: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that works by inhibiting water reabsorption in the nephron, leading to significant diuresis. The primary therapeutic effect in pulmonary edema is the rapid removal of excess fluid from the body. Therefore, a brisk and significant increase in urine output is the most direct and reliable indicator of the drug's effectiveness. While other parameters may improve as a consequence of diuresis, they are secondary effects.

Assessment:

options_or_rows_en.text: The client's heart rate decreases to 110 beats/minute.
The client has a urine output of 400 mL in the first hour.
The client's blood pressure decreases to 150/88 mmHg.
The client reports a decrease in anxiety.

rationale_en: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that works by inhibiting water reabsorption in the nephron, leading to significant diuresis. The primary therapeutic effect in pulmonary edema is the rapid removal of excess fluid from the body. Therefore, a brisk and significant increase in urine output is the most direct and reliable indicator of the drug's effectiveness. While other parameters may improve as a consequence of diuresis, they are secondary effects.

stem_en: Specify the correct medication for each clinical situation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical History
options_or_rows_en.content: Child uses fluticasone MDI twice daily and albuterol as needed. Parents report child has been waking up twice a week coughing. Child plays soccer and often needs albuterol before the game.

options_or_rows_en.text: 15 minutes before soccer practice
Daily morning and evening dose
During an acute episode of wheezing
Fluticasone (Controller)
Albuterol (Rescue)

rationale_en: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) and is the fast-acting reliever for both acute symptoms and pre-exercise prophylaxis. Fluticasone is an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) used for daily long-term control; because of its slow onset it has no role as a rescue inhaler. (Note: modern GINA SMART regimens use ICS-formoterol as both controller and reliever, but formoterol is a different drug from plain fluticasone and is not interchangeable with it here.)

Holds their breath for 10 seconds after inhalation.
Takes a quick, shallow breath during inhalation.

rationale_en: Holding the breath for 10 seconds allows the medication to settle in the lower airways. Rinsing should occur *after* using inhaled steroids (like fluticasone) to prevent oral candidiasis.

Increase intake of dark green leafy vegetables.

rationale_en: AV fistula care includes avoiding pressure (heavy bags, BP cuffs) and monitoring patency (thrill/bruit). Phosphate binders must be taken with meals to be effective. High-potassium foods (leafy greens) should be limited.

rationale_en: Hip precautions (posterior approach) include: no hip flexion >90 degrees (low sofas/bending), no adduction (crossing legs), and no internal rotation. Elevated seats help maintain flexion <90 degrees.

options_or_rows_en.text: Place a pillow under the knees.

stem_en: Indicate whether each of the following interventions is indicated or contraindicated for the client upon admission.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports sudden onset of severe, piercing pain in the left upper quadrant. Pain is worsened by eating. Client is restless and guarding the abdomen. Nausea and vomiting present. Vitals: HR 115 bpm, BP 100/60 mmHg, RR 22/min, Temp 38.1°C (100.6°F).

options_or_rows_en.text: Place the client on NPO status
Administer IV opioid analgesics
Provide small, frequent meals
Insert a nasogastric tube for decompression
Contraindicated
Indicated

rationale_en: NPO status is essential to rest the pancreas and reduce enzyme secretion. Opioids are needed for severe pain management. NG tube decompression helps relieve nausea/vomiting and prevents gastric contents from entering the duodenum. Feeding the client (small frequent meals) would stimulate the pancreas and worsen the condition.

options_or_rows_en.text: There are no spies in the vent; you are imagining things.
I don't see any spies, but I understand that it is frightening for you to feel watched.
Why do you think the government wants to watch you specifically?

stem_en: The client states, 'Do you see them? The spies are in the vent watching us!' What is the most therapeutic response?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client is pacing the hallway, looking over his shoulder frequently. States, 'The government has planted a chip in my brain to monitor my thoughts.' Refuses to eat because he believes the food is poisoned. Appears to be talking to someone when no one is present.

options_or_rows_en.text: There are no spies in the vent; you are imagining things.
I don't see any spies, but I understand that it is frightening for you to feel watched.
Why do you think the government wants to watch you specifically?
Let's go look in the vent together so I can prove you are safe.

rationale_en: This response acknowledges the client's feelings (validation) without agreeing with the delusion (presenting reality). Arguing or participating in the delusion is non-therapeutic.

options_or_rows_en.text: High fever and muscle rigidity
Involuntary lip smacking and tongue protrusion
Restlessness and inability to sit still
EPS
NMS

rationale_en: NMS is a medical emergency characterized by high fever and 'lead pipe' rigidity. EPS includes tardive dyskinesia (lip smacking) and akathisia (restlessness).

stem_en: Based on the initial triage assessment at 1400, for each finding, click to specify if it is consistent with classic SIRS criteria (a foundational infection-response framework still tested on the NCLEX-RN), organ dysfunction, or neither.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage Note - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 38.9°C (102.0°F)
- Heart Rate: 118 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 24 breaths/minute
- Blood Pressure: 94/52 mmHg
- SpO2: 91% on room air

Neurological:
- Alert to person only, disoriented to time and place. Restless.

Skin:
- Warm, flushed, and dry.

Urine:
- Dark amber, cloudy, with a strong odor.

options_or_rows_en.text: Temperature 38.9°C (102.0°F)
Heart Rate 118 beats/minute
Blood Pressure 94/52 mmHg
Altered mental status
History of type 2 diabetes
Consistent with Organ Dysfunction
Consistent with Classic SIRS Criteria
Neither

rationale_en: The client's fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea meet the criteria for SIRS. Hypotension and altered mental status are critical indicators of sepsis-induced organ dysfunction, signifying progression towards septic shock. The history of diabetes is a risk factor for infection but is not a direct finding of the current inflammatory response or organ failure. Note: The Sepsis-3 consensus (2016) defines sepsis by organ dysfunction rather than requiring SIRS criteria, using SOFA/qSOFA scoring. SIRS remains foundational for NCLEX-RN education and continues to appear in entry-level nursing curricula.

stem_en: The provider has confirmed septic shock. The nurse should anticipate performing which actions in order of priority? Drag and drop the options into the correct sequence.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage Note - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 38.9°C (102.0°F)
- Heart Rate: 118 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 24 breaths/minute
- Blood Pressure: 94/52 mmHg
- SpO2: 91% on room air

Neurological:
- Alert to person only, disoriented to time and place. Restless.

Skin:
- Warm, flushed, and dry.

Urine:
- Dark amber, cloudy, with a strong odor.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics intravenously.
Initiate a rapid intravenous fluid bolus of 30 mL/kg.
Prepare to administer a vasopressor infusion (e.g., norepinephrine).
Obtain blood and urine cultures.

rationale_en: The highest priority in septic shock is to restore tissue perfusion and intravascular volume, which is achieved by aggressive fluid resuscitation. Cultures must be obtained before starting antibiotics to identify the pathogen, but this should not delay treatment. Antibiotics should be administered within the first hour. Vasopressors are used after initial fluid resuscitation if hypotension persists, to maintain a target MAP of ≥ 65 mmHg.

stem_en: After two hours of aggressive fluid resuscitation and initiation of antibiotics and a norepinephrine infusion, which of the following findings would indicate clinical improvement? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage Note - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 38.9°C (102.0°F)
- Heart Rate: 118 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 24 breaths/minute
- Blood Pressure: 94/52 mmHg
- SpO2: 91% on room air

Neurological:
- Alert to person only, disoriented to time and place. Restless.

Skin:
- Warm, flushed, and dry.

Urine:
- Dark amber, cloudy, with a strong odor.

options_or_rows_en.text: Lactate level has decreased to 2.0 mmol/L.
Skin is cool and clammy.
Urine output is 40 mL/hour.
Serum creatinine remains 1.8 mg/dL.
Mean arterial pressure (MAP) is 70 mmHg.
Client is now oriented to person and place.

rationale_en: Positive outcomes in septic shock management are indicated by improved organ perfusion. This is evidenced by a MAP ≥ 65 mmHg, adequate urine output (≥ 0.5 mL/kg/hr or >30 mL/hr), improved mental status, and clearance of lactate. Cool, clammy skin suggests worsening shock (compensatory vasoconstriction). A stable but still elevated creatinine does not yet show improvement in renal function.

stem_en: The client's MAP remains 60 mmHg despite receiving norepinephrine at 10 mcg/min. The provider orders the addition of a vasopressin infusion. What is the primary rationale for adding vasopressin in this clinical situation?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage Note - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 38.9°C (102.0°F)
- Heart Rate: 118 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 24 breaths/minute
- Blood Pressure: 94/52 mmHg
- SpO2: 91% on room air

Neurological:
- Alert to person only, disoriented to time and place. Restless.

Skin:
- Warm, flushed, and dry.

Urine:
- Dark amber, cloudy, with a strong odor.

options_or_rows_en.text: To replace the body's depleted endogenous stores in a shock state.
To allow for a lower dose of norepinephrine, reducing the risk of peripheral ischemia.
To directly stimulate beta-1 receptors and increase the heart rate.
To provide a potent inotropic effect and increase cardiac contractility.

rationale_en: In septic shock, there is profound vasodilation. Vasopressin is a non-catecholamine vasopressor that acts on V1 receptors, causing vasoconstriction through a different mechanism than norepinephrine (an alpha-agonist). Adding vasopressin can have a synergistic effect, raising blood pressure and allowing the dose of norepinephrine to be reduced. High doses of norepinephrine are associated with significant adverse effects, including severe peripheral vasoconstriction, tachyarrhythmias, and myocardial ischemia.

stem_en: Based on the initial triage assessment, which of the following clinical findings strongly suggest an acute myocardial infarction accompanied by sympathetic nervous system activation? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 155/90 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 110 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/minute
- SpO2: 92% on room air

Diagnostic Testing:
- 12-Lead ECG: Reveals 4 mm ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
- Troponin I: Pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: A resting heart rate of 110 beats/minute.
ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
Visible, profuse diaphoresis.
An oxygen saturation of 92% on room air.
Crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the jaw.

rationale_en: Crushing chest pain radiating to the jaw is the classic ischemic symptom of a myocardial infarction. ST-segment elevation in V1-V4 provides definitive electrocardiographic evidence of an acute anterior wall STEMI. Diaphoresis and tachycardia (HR 110) are direct results of the body's massive sympathetic nervous system (fight-or-flight) response to severe pain and decreased cardiac output. While the SpO2 of 92% is slightly low and warrants oxygen therapy, it is a secondary finding of mild hypoxia, not a direct indicator of sympathetic activation or the infarction itself.

options_or_rows_en.content: While the nurse is preparing the client for immediate transport to the cardiac catheterization laboratory, the client's eyes roll back, and she becomes completely unresponsive. The cardiac monitor alarms, displaying a chaotic, irregular, and rapid waveform with no discernible P waves, QRS complexes, or T waves. The client has no palpable carotid pulse.

stem_en: The client suddenly becomes unresponsive with a chaotic rhythm on the monitor. Arrange the following emergency nursing actions in the most appropriate sequential order.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 155/90 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 110 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/minute
- SpO2: 92% on room air

Diagnostic Testing:
- 12-Lead ECG: Reveals 4 mm ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
- Troponin I: Pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Prepare to administer intravenous epinephrine 1 mg.
Call for the resuscitation team and a defibrillator.
Deliver an unsynchronized electrical shock (defibrillation).
Initiate high-quality cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) starting with chest compressions.

rationale_en: According to Basic Life Support (BLS) and Advanced Cardiovascular Life Support (ACLS) guidelines, the first action upon discovering an unresponsive pulseless client is to call for help and a defibrillator. Immediately after calling for help, the nurse must start high-quality CPR (chest compressions) to maintain minimal perfusion to the brain and heart. As soon as the defibrillator arrives and ventricular fibrillation is confirmed, an unsynchronized shock must be delivered, as early defibrillation is the only definitive treatment for V-Fib. Following the shock, CPR is immediately resumed, and pharmacological interventions like epinephrine are prepared and administered.

stem_en: The resuscitation team is utilizing multiple pharmacological agents during the cardiac arrest and peri-arrest period. Match each medication to its primary pharmacological classification.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 155/90 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 110 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/minute
- SpO2: 92% on room air

Diagnostic Testing:
- 12-Lead ECG: Reveals 4 mm ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
- Troponin I: Pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Amiodarone
Epinephrine
Aspirin
Nitroglycerin
Vasodilator
Antiarrhythmic
Antiplatelet Agent
Vasopressor

rationale_en: Amiodarone is a class III antiarrhythmic used in ACLS to treat refractory ventricular fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia. Epinephrine is a potent vasopressor (alpha- and beta-agonist) given during cardiac arrest to increase aortic diastolic pressure and coronary perfusion. Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent given early in acute coronary syndrome to prevent further clot aggregation in the coronary arteries. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator used to reduce preload and myocardial oxygen demand, relieving ischemic chest pain.

stem_en: During the resuscitation effort, the nurse continuously monitors the client's physiological responses. Which specific clinical finding provides the most definitive evidence of the return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 155/90 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 110 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/minute
- SpO2: 92% on room air

Diagnostic Testing:
- 12-Lead ECG: Reveals 4 mm ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
- Troponin I: Pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: The appearance of an organized sinus rhythm on the cardiac monitor without a palpable pulse.
Bilateral pupillary dilation and unresponsiveness to light.
The presence of occasional, irregular gasping respirations (agonal breathing).
An abrupt and sustained increase in PETCO2 (end-tidal carbon dioxide) to 40 mmHg.

rationale_en: An abrupt and sustained increase in end-tidal carbon dioxide (PETCO2), typically to a normal value of 35-40 mmHg, is the earliest and most reliable non-invasive indicator of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). During cardiac arrest, PETCO2 is low because blood is not circulating to the lungs to release CO2. When the heart restarts and cardiac output is restored, accumulated CO2 is suddenly washed out of the tissues and delivered to the lungs, causing a sharp spike on the capnography monitor. This often occurs even before a pulse can be reliably palpated.

stem_en: The nurse analyzes the client's initial 0800 triage presentation. For each finding, indicate if the cue is expected in uncomplicated hyperthyroidism, or if it specifically indicates a transition to life-threatening thyroid storm.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
Temperature: 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Heart Rate: 152 bpm (irregular)
Blood Pressure: 168/94 mm Hg
Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/min
SpO2: 95% on room air

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Temperature of 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Diffuse anterior neck enlargement
Severe agitation and confusion
Heart rate of 152 bpm
Expected in Uncomplicated Hyperthyroidism
Indicates Thyroid Storm

rationale_en: Thyroid storm is an extreme, life-threatening exacerbation of hyperthyroidism. While hyperthyroidism causes heat intolerance, extreme hyperpyrexia (e.g., 104.2 °F) is a hallmark of thyroid storm. Mild anxiety is common in hyperthyroidism, but severe agitation, delirium, and confusion indicate CNS dysfunction specific to thyroid storm. Resting tachycardia (90-110 bpm) is expected in hyperthyroidism, but extreme tachycardia (>130 bpm) or arrhythmias like atrial fibrillation signify thyroid storm. A goiter is an anatomical feature of Graves' disease and hyperthyroidism, not a specific acute marker of a storm.

stem_en: The nurse analyzes the client's initial 0800 triage presentation. For each finding, indicate if the cue is expected in uncomplicated hyperthyroidism, or if it specifically indicates a transition to life-threatening thyroid storm.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1200: Clinical Trend
options_or_rows_en.content: The client has received IV fluids, propranolol, PTU, Lugol's iodine, and hydrocortisone as prescribed. The client's agitation has decreased, and continuous telemetry demonstrates normal sinus rhythm. A complete blood count (CBC) with differential and a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) were drawn.

stem_en: Based on the 0830 orders, the nurse prepares to administer the prescribed medications. Drag and drop the medications into the correct order of administration to safely manage the thyroid storm.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
Temperature: 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Heart Rate: 152 bpm (irregular)
Blood Pressure: 168/94 mm Hg
Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/min
SpO2: 95% on room air

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer IV propranolol
Administer IV hydrocortisone
Administer Lugol's iodine solution
Administer propylthiouracil (PTU)

rationale_en: The pharmacological sequence in thyroid storm is critical to prevent exacerbating the condition. First, a beta-blocker (propranolol) is administered immediately to block peripheral sympathetic effects, lowering the dangerously high heart rate and preventing cardiovascular collapse. Second, an antithyroid medication like PTU is given to block the synthesis of new thyroid hormones. Third, an iodine solution (Lugol's) is given to block the release of pre-formed thyroid hormones. Crucially, iodine must be delayed for 1-2 hours AFTER PTU; if given first, iodine will act as substrate and fuel massive new thyroid hormone synthesis. Fourth, corticosteroids (hydrocortisone) are administered to treat relative adrenal insufficiency caused by hypermetabolism and to block the peripheral conversion of T4 to active T3.

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer prescribed aspirin for antipyretic relief.
Apply a cooling blanket and ice packs to axillae and groin.
Maintain a cool, dark, and quiet room environment.
Administer prescribed acetaminophen for hyperthermia.
Restrict intravenous fluids to prevent pulmonary edema.
Initiate seizure precautions.

rationale_en: Nursing care for thyroid storm focuses on reducing hyperthermia, preventing injury from extreme agitation, and providing supportive care. Acetaminophen and physical cooling (cooling blankets, ice packs) are used aggressively to lower core temperature. A cool, dark, quiet environment minimizes sympathetic nervous system stimulation, reducing agitation and cardiac demand. Seizure precautions are necessary because severe hyperpyrexia and CNS hyper-excitability place the client at high risk for seizures. Aspirin is contraindicated because salicylates displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins, increasing free active thyroid hormone levels and worsening the storm. Fluid restriction is incorrect; these clients have profound insensible fluid losses from diaphoresis and hyperventilation and require aggressive IV fluid replacement.

stem_en: To manage the client's hypermetabolic state and maintain safety between 0800 and 1200, which nursing interventions are indicated? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1200: Clinical Trend
options_or_rows_en.content: The client has received IV fluids, propranolol, PTU, Lugol's iodine, and hydrocortisone as prescribed. The client's agitation has decreased, and continuous telemetry demonstrates normal sinus rhythm. A complete blood count (CBC) with differential and a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) were drawn.

stem_en: The nurse evaluates the 1200 laboratory results to monitor for severe adverse effects of propylthiouracil (PTU). Which laboratory finding requires immediate notification to the healthcare provider?

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
Temperature: 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Heart Rate: 152 bpm (irregular)
Blood Pressure: 168/94 mm Hg
Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/min
SpO2: 95% on room air

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) of 18 mg/dL (6.4 mmol/L)
Serum potassium of 3.8 mEq/L (3.8 mmol/L)
White blood cell count of 2,800/mm³ (2.8 × 10^9/L)
Free T4 of 4.2 ng/dL

rationale_en: A severe and potentially fatal adverse effect of propylthiouracil (PTU) and methimazole is agranulocytosis (a profound drop in white blood cells, specifically neutrophils). A total WBC count of 2,800/mm³ is abnormally low (leukopenia) and places the client at extreme risk for life-threatening sepsis, warranting immediate provider notification to stop the drug and implement reverse isolation. The potassium and BUN levels are within normal limits. The Free T4 remains elevated (normal 0.8-2.8 ng/dL); while abnormal, this is an expected finding at this stage, as it takes time for circulating hormone levels to fall even after synthesis and release are blocked.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the clinical cues from Stage 1. For each cue, indicate whether it supports early Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), neutropenic fever/sepsis risk, or is a nonspecific finding. Each row may have more than one correct category.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background / Admission orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
A 58-year-old man with relapsed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is admitted to the bone marrow transplant and cellular therapy unit for axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) CAR-T cell infusion. He previously received two lines of chemoimmunotherapy with initial response followed by progressive disease. His past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily and type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily; metformin was held 48 hours before admission per protocol. He has no history of cardiac disease, pulmonary disease, or neurological conditions. Lymphodepleting chemotherapy with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide was completed three days ago, and the CAR-T cell product was infused this morning at 0800 without immediate infusion reaction.

Standing orders include continuous telemetry, strict intake and output (patient is using a urinal, and urine output is measured every 4 hours), neurological assessments every 4 hours using the ICE (Immune Effector Cell-Associated Encephalopathy) scoring tool, vital signs every 4 hours for the first 24 hours then escalating to every 2 hours or more frequently if any signs of cytokine release syndrome (CRS) emerge. Tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV is available at bedside for CRS management per institutional protocol, and dexamethasone is readily accessible. IV normal saline is infusing at 75 mL/hr for maintenance hydration. The patient has a double-lumen peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC).

options_or_rows_en.text: Fever, rigors, and myalgias
Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) 0.1 × 10³/µL
CRP 86 mg/L and ferritin 1,450 ng/mL
Mild fatigue reported at 1600
Supports early Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS)
Supports neutropenic fever/sepsis risk
Nonspecific or expected finding

rationale_en: Recognizing the clinical signs of both CRS and neutropenic fever is critical in the post-CAR-T setting. Fever, rigors, and myalgias are hallmark initial signs of CRS but are also classic symptoms of sepsis in a neutropenic patient. An ANC of 0.1 × 10³/µL places the patient at profound risk for neutropenic fever and sepsis, requiring immediate intervention. The elevation of CRP and ferritin strongly supports the onset of CRS. Mild fatigue is a nonspecific finding often expected following lymphodepleting chemotherapy.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the clinical cues from Stage 1. For each cue, indicate whether it supports early Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), neutropenic fever/sepsis risk, or is a nonspecific finding. Each row may have more than one correct category.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3, 60 hours post-infusion
options_or_rows_en.content:
12 hours after tocilizumab and initiation of dexamethasone. The patient received the tocilizumab infusion without incident and has received three doses of dexamethasone.

On reassessment: temperature 37.6 °C (down from 39.8), heart rate 96 (sinus rhythm, improved from 122), blood pressure 108/68 (up from 88/52 and now stable without vasopressors after one additional 250 mL bolus), respiratory rate 18, SpO₂ 96% on 1 L nasal cannula (weaned from 2 L).

The patient is alert and oriented to year, month, city, and hospital. ICE score has improved from 6/10 to 9/10 (one point off for naming; he was unable to name one of the three objects but could write a full sentence, sustain attention, and follow commands). His wife says, "He seems much more like himself." Lungs are clear; crackles have resolved. Urine output over the past 4 hours is 220 mL (improving).

Repeat labs: CRP 110 mg/L (still elevated but trending down from 245), ferritin 5,400 ng/mL (down from 8,200), fibrinogen 240 mg/dL (improving from 180), LDH 420 U/L, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (improving from 1.6), AST 52 U/L, ALT 48 U/L, D-dimer 1.6 µg/mL FEU, potassium 4.4 mEq/L.

The provider continues dexamethasone on a taper schedule, maintains tocilizumab availability for a second dose if the patient re-spikes fever or becomes hypotensive again, and asks the nurse to continue frequent vital signs and neurological assessments every 4 hours. ICU transfer is deferred for now, pending sustained stability.

stem_en: Based on the Stage 1 findings, which of the following actions should the nurse anticipate or implement? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background / Admission orders
options_or_rows_en.content:

Lymphodepleting chemotherapy with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide was completed three days ago, and the CAR-T cell product was infused this morning at 0800 without immediate infusion reaction.

options_or_rows_en.text: Assume the fever is solely expected CRS and defer infection workup.
Anticipate initiating empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics per neutropenic fever protocol.
Notify the provider and activate the institutional CRS pathway.
Administer acetaminophen as ordered for symptom management.
Obtain blood cultures per protocol, including central-line lumens.
Increase the frequency of vital signs monitoring and continue ICE scoring.

rationale_en: In a profoundly neutropenic patient post-CAR-T infusion, a new fever must be treated concurrently as potential Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) and a neutropenic fever/sepsis medical emergency. The nurse should notify the provider to activate the CRS pathway, obtain blood cultures per protocol, anticipate initiating broad-spectrum antibiotics, administer acetaminophen for symptom management, and increase the frequency of monitoring and ICE scoring. Waiting for culture results before giving antibiotics or assuming the fever is only CRS can cause fatal delays in treating sepsis.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer dexamethasone IV as ordered for concurrent probable ICANS.
Begin the ordered normal saline IV fluid bolus.
Prepare for possible ICU transfer while continuing frequent monitoring.
Apply low-flow oxygen via nasal cannula and raise the head of the bed.
Administer tocilizumab IV as ordered for CRS Grade 2.

rationale_en: Using the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) framework, the nurse must first stabilize oxygenation by applying low-flow oxygen and raising the head of the bed. Second, initiate the IV fluid bolus to address hypotension and support perfusion. Once basic physiological stability is addressed, pharmacological interventions follow: administer tocilizumab (the definitive treatment for CRS) and then dexamethasone (for concurrent ICANS). Finally, prepare for potential ICU transfer while continuing frequent monitoring. Transfer preparation and team communication may occur in parallel, but they must not delay life-saving oxygen, fluids, or ordered medications.

options_or_rows_en.text: Hypotension (BP 88/52) and low-flow oxygen need
ICE score 6/10, disorientation, and attention difficulty
Creatinine rising from 1.0 to 1.6 mg/dL with oliguria
Falling fibrinogen with rising D-dimer
Persistent fever to 39.8 °C and rigors
Indicates CRS progression or severity
Indicates Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS)
Indicates early end-organ or coagulation dysfunction

rationale_en: CRS severity grading is primarily driven by fever, hypotension, and hypoxia. Therefore, hypotension requiring fluids and hypoxia requiring low-flow oxygen, along with persistent fever, indicate CRS severity. ICANS is distinct and indicated by neurological changes such as a dropping ICE score, disorientation, and attention difficulty. Rising creatinine with oliguria and falling fibrinogen with rising D-dimer suggest early end-organ and coagulation dysfunction, which are consequences of severe systemic CRS inflammation.

options.text: an increased need for oxygen support
a resolving fever and improved blood pressure without vasopressors
improving urine output and an ICE score of 9/10
confirms complete resolution of the inflammatory process
indicates that neurotoxicity is fully reversed
is an unreliable marker alone because tocilizumab suppresses CRP production
monitoring can be safely reduced to baseline frequency
the patient can be immediately discharged
frequent monitoring must continue because CRS or ICANS can recur

rationale_en: Clinical improvement after tocilizumab and dexamethasone must be evaluated using multiple parameters. A resolving fever, improved blood pressure without vasopressors, improved urine output, and an ICE score of 9/10 indicate genuine recovery from CRS and ICANS. However, the nurse must recognize that tocilizumab suppresses CRP production by blocking the IL-6 pathway, making a declining CRP an unreliable marker alone. Frequent monitoring must continue because CRS or ICANS can recur or fluctuate after initial improvement.

The patient's continuous seizure activity is likely due to prehospital medication administration errors.

options_or_rows_en.text: Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level
Lactate 6.8 mmol/L
Bicarbonate 16 mEq/L
Mildly elevated CK (480 U/L)
Secondary Consequence
Likely Precipitating Factor / Major Contributor

rationale_en: Medication nonadherence is a common major contributor to status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy, and the subtherapeutic levetiracetam level strongly supports this. Identifying this helps the team target therapy while continuing evaluation for other precipitants after stabilization. Lactate elevation and metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) are expected consequences of sustained seizure-related muscle activity and do not independently require sodium bicarbonate infusion — they resolve when seizures stop. Early CK elevation is a secondary consequence that flags the need for aggressive IV hydration to prevent myoglobin-induced acute kidney injury.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course for Stage 1. The nurse is administering the prescribed fosphenytoin IV. Which of the following are priority nursing interventions during this infusion? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the infusion as an IV push over 1-2 minutes.
Monitor blood pressure every 5 minutes.
Observe for cessation of seizure activity.
Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring.
Ensure the infusion rate does not exceed 150 mg PE/min.

rationale_en: The nurse must monitor for cardiac arrhythmias (continuous telemetry), hypotension (blood pressure every 5 minutes during infusion), and observe for seizure cessation as the therapeutic endpoint. Fosphenytoin can cause QT prolongation, bradycardia, and hypotension through its effect on cardiac sodium channels, particularly at rapid infusion rates. Cardiovascular collapse is a known fatal complication of too-rapid administration. The infusion rate must not exceed 150 mg PE/min.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course for Stage 2. The nurse observes that motor seizure activity persists, SpO₂ is 91%, end-tidal CO₂ is 58 mmHg, and the patient has copious secretions with increasing cyanosis. Which of the following problems should the nurse prioritize at this time?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Refractory seizure activity requiring additional antiepileptics
Developing rhabdomyolysis
Compromised airway and impending respiratory failure
Worsening metabolic acidosis

rationale_en: Even though terminating the seizure is the ultimate goal, the patient cannot survive the seizure if she cannot oxygenate and ventilate. The falling SpO₂, rising CO₂, and copious secretions signal impending respiratory failure. Applying the ABCs framework, airway protection through intubation takes precedence. Furthermore, the continuous infusion agents used for refractory status epilepticus (midazolam, propofol) cause respiratory depression and require a secured airway before they can be safely administered. Therefore, intubation both protects the patient's breathing and enables definitive seizure treatment.

options.text: cessation of visible motor activity does not equal seizure termination
the midazolam dose is too low to produce any effect
the patient is paralyzed from the succinylcholine alone

rationale_en: In status epilepticus, especially after prolonged seizure activity and the administration of neuromuscular blocking agents during RSI (succinylcholine), motor manifestations may cease while cortical seizure activity continues — this is called nonconvulsive status epilepticus. Ongoing electrographic seizure activity causes continued neuronal injury. Continuous EEG is the gold standard for confirming seizure termination. In this case, the ordered therapeutic target for the continuous midazolam infusion is burst-suppression on EEG.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course for Stage 3. The patient's urine output over the first ICU hour is 120 mL, and the urine myoglobin is markedly positive. Which of the following nursing actions is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Decrease the IV fluid rate to 50 mL/hr to prevent fluid volume overload during mechanical ventilation.
Titrate IV isotonic fluids up toward 300 mL/hr as ordered to manage rhabdomyolysis.
Remove the indwelling urinary catheter to reduce the risk of catheter-associated infection.
Administer an IV fluid bolus of dextrose 5% in water to correct metabolic acidosis.

rationale_en: Sustained tonic-clonic muscle activity causes massive skeletal muscle breakdown, releasing myoglobin into the bloodstream. Myoglobin is freely filtered by the glomerulus and precipitates in renal tubules, causing acute kidney injury. The combination of rising CK, dark urine, and positive urine myoglobin confirms rhabdomyolysis. Early aggressive crystalloid hydration (e.g., isotonic fluids titrated to maintain high urine output per protocol, often 1-3 mL/kg/hr or up to 300 mL/hr) is the cornerstone of prevention of renal failure.

Repeat labs at the one-hour ICU mark: lactate 5.2 mmol/L (falling), CK 2,800 U/L (still rising but expected to peak and then fall), potassium 5.3 mEq/L (stable, being monitored closely), creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (slight rise), ABG pH 7.30, PaCO₂ 38 mmHg (improved with mechanical ventilation), PaO₂ 142 mmHg (on FiO₂ 60%), bicarbonate 18 mEq/L (improving). The nurse maintains continuous EEG monitoring and reports any return of electrographic seizure patterns to the provider, keeps the head of bed elevated at 30 degrees, monitors the midazolam infusion, performs neurological assessments (pupil reactivity, response to stimuli) at least hourly, monitors urine output and color for rhabdomyolysis resolution, and maintains seizure precautions including padded side rails and suction at bedside.

options_or_rows_en.text: Decrease the IV fluid rate to 50 mL/hr to prevent fluid volume overload during mechanical ventilation.
Titrate IV isotonic fluids up toward 300 mL/hr as ordered to manage rhabdomyolysis.
Remove the indwelling urinary catheter to reduce the risk of catheter-associated infection.
Administer an IV fluid bolus of dextrose 5% in water to correct metabolic acidosis.

rationale_en: Sustained tonic-clonic muscle activity causes massive skeletal muscle breakdown, releasing myoglobin into the bloodstream. Myoglobin is freely filtered by the glomerulus and precipitates in renal tubules, causing acute kidney injury. The combination of rising CK, dark urine, and positive urine myoglobin confirms rhabdomyolysis. Early aggressive crystalloid hydration (e.g., isotonic fluids titrated to maintain high urine output per protocol, often 1-3 mL/kg/hr or up to 300 mL/hr) is the cornerstone of prevention of renal failure.

stem_en: The nurse is preparing to administer the scheduled immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy before Stage 1 begins. Based on the initial assessment and baseline laboratory data, which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. The patient rates her chest heaviness as 3 out of 10 and states it is not pleuritic.

**LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the PRN ondansetron for the patient's nausea before starting the infusion.
Hold the infusion and immediately notify the oncologist of the laboratory results and patient symptoms.
Proceed with the infusion and monitor the patient's vital signs every 15 minutes.
Hold the infusion and plan to communicate the findings to the oncologist during routine rounds.

rationale_en: The nurse should hold the infusion and immediately notify the oncologist of the elevated troponin I (0.18 ng/mL), elevated BNP, transaminase elevations, new exertional chest symptoms, and resting tachycardia. Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause fulminant myocarditis, which carries a mortality rate of up to 25–50% when diagnosed late. An elevated troponin in a patient on combination ICI therapy with new cardiac symptoms is a red-flag cue that must be reported before administering another dose of the causative agent, because additional dosing could accelerate the immune-mediated injury.

stem_en: The nurse is preparing to administer the scheduled immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy before Stage 1 begins. Based on the initial assessment and baseline laboratory data, which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 Clinical Course
options_or_rows_en.content: 18 to 24 hours after initiation of pulse-dose corticosteroids. The patient has received two doses of methylprednisolone 1 g IV. Repeat vital signs: T 37.0 °C, HR 94 and regular, BP 114/72, RR 18, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm at 94 bpm with resolution of the frequent PVCs; PR interval has shortened to 0.20 seconds, though ST depressions persist. The patient reports chest heaviness at 2 out of 10 and can sit up and speak in full sentences without dyspnea. Repeat labs: troponin I 0.64 ng/mL (declining from 1.42), BNP 390 pg/mL (declining), AST 132 U/L (declining), ALT 148 U/L (declining), total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL, CRP 4.1 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, CK 420 U/L (mildly elevated), blood glucose 218 mg/dL (new hyperglycemia attributed to high-dose corticosteroids). The patient's urine output has averaged 55 mL/hr over the last 8 hours. The care team discusses the treatment plan: a corticosteroid taper over several weeks, ongoing troponin and liver-enzyme monitoring every 6 to 12 hours, cardiology follow-up with a repeat echocardiogram in 48 to 72 hours, and the permanent discontinuation of combination ICI therapy. The oncologist notes that for this patient’s grade 3–4 ICI myocarditis, the treatment plan is permanent discontinuation and the patient should not restart checkpoint inhibitor therapy unless a future specialist oncology/cardiology team explicitly determines otherwise. A new concern at this stage is the steroid-induced hyperglycemia, which requires blood glucose monitoring every 4 to 6 hours and possible sliding-scale insulin coverage.

options_or_rows_en.text: Metastatic disease progression into the cardiopulmonary system.
Immune checkpoint inhibitor–induced myocarditis with concurrent immune-mediated hepatitis.
Viral myocarditis presenting as an isolated infectious process.
Acute coronary syndrome resulting from atherosclerotic plaque rupture.

rationale_en: The combination of diffuse (not territory-specific) ST-segment changes, a new conduction abnormality (prolonging PR interval), troponin elevation, BNP elevation, and concurrent hepatic transaminase elevation in a patient actively receiving combination anti–PD-1/anti–CTLA-4 therapy is the hallmark presentation of ICI myocarditis with multi-organ immune-related adverse events. The concurrent hepatotoxicity suggests a systemic immune-mediated process rather than a single-organ etiology.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ensure continuous telemetry and reliable IV access.
Administer the high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone.
Apply supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 94%.
Prepare the patient for transfer to a higher-acuity monitored cardiac unit.

rationale_en: After oxygen is applied, the nurse rapidly verifies continuous telemetry and patent IV access so high-dose IV methylprednisolone can be administered without delay. These steps should occur immediately and should not postpone the first steroid dose. Transfer is important but follows oxygenation, monitoring/access verification, and initiation of time-critical immunosuppression.

stem_en: The nurse prepares a comprehensive monitoring plan for the patient following the administration of corticosteroids. Which of the following nursing interventions should be included? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. The patient rates her chest heaviness as 3 out of 10 and states it is not pleuritic.

**LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Serial troponin and liver-enzyme monitoring every 6 to 12 hours.
Blood glucose monitoring every 4 to 6 hours.
Discontinuation of cardiac monitoring once the patient's PR interval normalizes.
Assessment for overlapping myositis or myasthenia-like symptoms.
Continuous telemetry with alarms set for arrhythmias and conduction changes.

rationale_en: The nurse should generate a solution set that includes continuous telemetry with alarms set for arrhythmias and conduction changes (especially worsening AV block, which can progress to complete heart block), serial troponin levels to track trajectory, serial liver-enzyme monitoring, and blood glucose monitoring anticipating steroid-induced hyperglycemia. The nurse should also assess for signs of other immune-related adverse events, because ICI myocarditis can overlap with neuromuscular immune toxicity. Discontinuing cardiac monitoring is unsafe because ICI myocarditis can deteriorate rapidly to complete heart block or fatal arrhythmia.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the oncologist writes an order to permanently discontinue all ICI therapy. What is the most appropriate action by the nurse regarding this medication order?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. The patient rates her chest heaviness as 3 out of 10 and states it is not pleuritic.

**LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Document the ICI discontinuation as a temporary hold and inform the patient that therapy will resume after cardiac recovery.
Verify the ICI is held/discontinued in the MAR per the provider order, ensure the adverse drug reaction is documented, and educate the patient about the permanent discontinuation.
Independently delete the ICI order from the patient's active medication list to prevent inadvertent administration by another nurse.
Request an order to transition the patient from combination therapy to single-agent immunotherapy.

rationale_en: The nurse should verify the ICI is held/discontinued in the MAR per the provider order; document the medication as not given with the clinical reason; notify pharmacy/oncology if the order remains active; ensure the adverse drug reaction is documented per facility policy; and educate the patient that the immunotherapy was stopped because of a serious heart-and-liver immune reaction. Permanent discontinuation is the standard of care for grade 3–4 ICI myocarditis, given the high fatality risk with re-exposure.

The care team discusses the treatment plan: a corticosteroid taper over several weeks, ongoing troponin and liver-enzyme monitoring every 6 to 12 hours, cardiology follow-up with a repeat echocardiogram in 48 to 72 hours, and the permanent discontinuation of combination ICI therapy. The oncologist notes that for this patient’s grade 3–4 ICI myocarditis, the treatment plan is permanent discontinuation and the patient should not restart checkpoint inhibitor therapy unless a future specialist oncology/cardiology team explicitly determines otherwise. A new concern at this stage is the steroid-induced hyperglycemia, which requires blood glucose monitoring every 4 to 6 hours and possible sliding-scale insulin coverage.

options_or_rows_en.text: Decline in troponin I from 1.42 to 0.64 ng/mL
Resolution of frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs)
Blood glucose level of 218 mg/dL
Treatment-Related Adverse Effect
Positive Response to Treatment

rationale_en: The decline in troponin I and the resolution of PVCs confirm a positive response to the immunosuppressive therapy, demonstrating that the immune-mediated injury is being suppressed. The blood glucose of 218 mg/dL is a predictable adverse effect of the high-dose methylprednisolone.

stem_en: Review the patient's baseline assessment findings. The nurse should recognize that the combination of escalating mid-back pain, new bilateral leg weakness, and sensory/bladder changes is most highly suggestive of which oncologic emergency?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves are intact. Upper extremity strength is 5 out of 5 bilaterally with normal sensation. Lower extremity motor strength is 4 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps and 4 minus out of 5 in ankle dorsiflexors, with the left side slightly weaker than the right. She has difficulty lifting her legs against gravity with sustained resistance. Deep tendon reflexes at the patellar tendons are hyperreflexic (3+) bilaterally; Achilles reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Babinski sign is equivocal on the right and extensor (positive, upgoing toe) on the left. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: Opioid-induced neurotoxicity
Malignant spinal cord compression
Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration
Hypercalcemia of malignancy

rationale_en: The combination of escalating back pain at the level of known metastases, new motor weakness with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, positive Babinski), a definable sensory level, and autonomic (bladder) dysfunction is the classic presentation of malignant spinal cord compression. This is an oncologic emergency requiring urgent intervention.

stem_en: Review the patient's baseline assessment findings. The nurse should recognize that the combination of escalating mid-back pain, new bilateral leg weakness, and sensory/bladder changes is most highly suggestive of which oncologic emergency?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course — Postoperative Day 1
options_or_rows_en.content: Approximately 30 hours after admission (roughly 18 hours after posterior laminectomy): The patient is transferred back to the oncology unit from the PACU. She is awake, alert, and oriented. Surgical site dressing at the posterior thoracic spine is clean, dry, and intact with a Jackson-Pratt drain in place with 40 mL of serosanguineous output over the past 8 hours. Vital signs: temperature 37.8 °C, heart rate 78, blood pressure 124/74, respiratory rate 16, SpO₂ 97% on 2 L nasal cannula. Pain is 5 out of 10 at the surgical site, managed on IV hydromorphone. On neurological reassessment: lower extremity motor strength is now 3 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps — improved from the 2 out of 5 seen preoperatively. Ankle dorsiflexion is 2 out of 5 bilaterally (improved on the left from 1 out of 5). She can wiggle all toes weakly on command. Deep tendon reflexes remain hyperreflexic (3+) but clonus is no longer sustained — only 1 beat at the left ankle, absent on the right. Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally. Sensory level has improved: she now perceives light touch beginning at T8 and pinprick at T9. Perineal sensation remains absent. The indwelling urinary catheter remains in place, draining clear yellow urine at 60 mL per hour. Blood glucose is 210 mg/dL; a sliding-scale insulin order is in place. Repeat labs: hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (dropped from 10.8, expected surgical loss), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (stable), corrected calcium 10.4 mg/dL (slightly improved from 10.8, bisphosphonate and steroids helping), alkaline phosphatase 298 U/L (stable). The dexamethasone is continued at 4 mg IV every 6 hours with a planned taper over the coming weeks. Radiation oncology plans to initiate adjuvant radiation to the T6–T8 field in 10 to 14 days once the surgical wound has begun to heal. The patient's mood is cautiously hopeful. She asks the nurse, "Will I need to learn to use a wheelchair?"

options_or_rows_en.text: Provide emotional support and reassurance regarding the risk of paralysis
Administer the IV dexamethasone bolus as prescribed
Perform a complete head-to-toe nursing assessment
Prepare the patient for the insertion of an indwelling urinary catheter

rationale_en: Administering the urgent MSCC dexamethasone dose is the highest-priority pharmacological intervention for malignant spinal cord compression and should not be delayed. It reduces vasogenic edema surrounding the compressed cord, which can halt or slow neurological deterioration while awaiting definitive treatment.

options_or_rows_en.text: A localized urinary tract infection secondary to impaired mobility
An expected adverse effect of her recent oxycodone administration
Dehydration requiring an increase in her IV fluid rate
Interruption of descending autonomic pathways indicating progressive spinal cord compression

rationale_en: Urinary retention in the context of thoracic spinal cord compression reflects the interruption of descending autonomic pathways. It is an ominous neurological finding when accompanied by worsening lower-extremity weakness, a rising sensory level, and new perineal sensory change.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course at Stage 2. The neurosurgeon recommends emergent surgical decompression. Which of the following actions should the nurse take to safely prepare the patient for surgery? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves are intact. Upper extremity strength is 5 out of 5 bilaterally with normal sensation.
