She has difficulty lifting her legs against gravity with sustained resistance. Deep tendon reflexes at the patellar tendons are hyperreflexic (3+) bilaterally; Achilles reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Babinski sign is equivocal on the right and extensor (positive, upgoing toe) on the left. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: Explain the surgical risks and prognostic outcome of the laminectomy to the family.
Verify that the surgeon has discussed the procedure and obtained informed consent.
Ensure current laboratories, a type and screen, and imaging are available.
Hold the scheduled enoxaparin dose and communicate this to the surgical team.
Continue serial neurological assessments to document the trajectory of decline.
Administer a stat dose of regular insulin to correct the steroid-induced hyperglycemia to a target of 100 mg/dL.

rationale_en: Preparing a patient for emergent spinal decompression requires verifying informed consent, ensuring safety (holding anticoagulation), preparing necessary preoperative data (labs, imaging, type and screen), and continuing to monitor the rapid neurological decline. Holding the enoxaparin is critical to prevent epidural hematoma. The nurse's role in consent is to verify it was obtained by the provider, not to explain surgical risks or prognosticate.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the patient's daughter asks the nurse, "Will surgery make my mother walk again?" Which is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves are intact. Upper extremity strength is 5 out of 5 bilaterally with normal sensation. Lower extremity motor strength is 4 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps and 4 minus out of 5 in ankle dorsiflexors, with the left side slightly weaker than the right. She has difficulty lifting her legs against gravity with sustained resistance. Deep tendon reflexes at the patellar tendons are hyperreflexic (3+) bilaterally; Achilles reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Babinski sign is equivocal on the right and extensor (positive, upgoing toe) on the left. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: "I understand how frightening this is. The goal of surgery is to take pressure off the spinal cord and prevent further neurologic loss. Some patients regain function, and the fact that your mother still has some leg movement is encouraging, but the neurosurgeon is the best person to discuss expected recovery."
"The damage from the tumor is usually permanent. Surgery is just a palliative measure to reduce pain, so she will likely need a wheelchair."
"Don't worry, the neurosurgeon is highly skilled. The surgery will fix the compression and she will be able to walk again after rehabilitation."
"I am not allowed to discuss her prognosis with you. You must wait until the surgeon comes out of the operating room."

rationale_en: This response balances honesty, empathy, and safe nursing boundaries. It correctly identifies the primary goal of surgery (preventing further loss), provides grounded hope based on clinical data (remaining motor function is a good prognostic sign), and appropriately refers definitive prognostication to the surgeon.

Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally
Sensory level is at T8–T9

Persistent Dysfunction
Improvement from Stage 2 Preoperative Status

rationale_en: Evaluation requires serial comparison. Preoperatively, motor strength had dropped to 2/5; 3/5 is an improvement. The sensory level had risen to T6; dropping back to T8–T9 is an improvement. However, positive Babinski signs reflect persistent upper motor neuron damage, and absent perineal sensation reflects persistent autonomic/sacral dysfunction, which typically recovers last.

stem_en: At Stage 3 (postoperative day 1), the nurse evaluates the patient's neurological response to surgery. For each assessment finding, specify whether it indicates an improvement from the preoperative baseline or indicates persistent spinal cord dysfunction.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course — Postoperative Day 1
options_or_rows_en.content: Approximately 30 hours after admission (roughly 18 hours after posterior laminectomy): The patient is transferred back to the oncology unit from the PACU. She is awake, alert, and oriented. Surgical site dressing at the posterior thoracic spine is clean, dry, and intact with a Jackson-Pratt drain in place with 40 mL of serosanguineous output over the past 8 hours. Vital signs: temperature 37.8 °C, heart rate 78, blood pressure 124/74, respiratory rate 16, SpO₂ 97% on 2 L nasal cannula. Pain is 5 out of 10 at the surgical site, managed on IV hydromorphone. On neurological reassessment: lower extremity motor strength is now 3 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps — improved from the 2 out of 5 seen preoperatively. Ankle dorsiflexion is 2 out of 5 bilaterally (improved on the left from 1 out of 5). She can wiggle all toes weakly on command. Deep tendon reflexes remain hyperreflexic (3+) but clonus is no longer sustained — only 1 beat at the left ankle, absent on the right. Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally. Sensory level has improved: she now perceives light touch beginning at T8 and pinprick at T9. Perineal sensation remains absent. The indwelling urinary catheter remains in place, draining clear yellow urine at 60 mL per hour. Blood glucose is 210 mg/dL; a sliding-scale insulin order is in place. Repeat labs: hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (dropped from 10.8, expected surgical loss), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (stable), corrected calcium 10.4 mg/dL (slightly improved from 10.8, bisphosphonate and steroids helping), alkaline phosphatase 298 U/L (stable). The dexamethasone is continued at 4 mg IV every 6 hours with a planned taper over the coming weeks. Radiation oncology plans to initiate adjuvant radiation to the T6–T8 field in 10 to 14 days once the surgical wound has begun to heal. The patient's mood is cautiously hopeful. She asks the nurse, "Will I need to learn to use a wheelchair?"

options_or_rows_en.text: Bilateral lower extremity motor strength is 3 out of 5
Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally
Sensory level is at T8–T9
Absence of perineal sensation
Persistent Dysfunction
Improvement from Stage 2 Preoperative Status

rationale_en: Evaluation requires serial comparison. Preoperatively, motor strength had dropped to 2/5; 3/5 is an improvement. The sensory level had risen to T6; dropping back to T8–T9 is an improvement. However, positive Babinski signs reflect persistent upper motor neuron damage, and absent perineal sensation reflects persistent autonomic/sacral dysfunction, which typically recovers last.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after the fluid bolus and electrolyte replacements, the patient's condition continues to worsen. Based on the new clinical findings, what is the most likely emerging complication, and which cue is the highest priority to communicate to the provider?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs (Start of Shift)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline assessment at start of night shift: Temperature 38.9 °C (102.0 °F) orally (elevated from 37.2 °C twelve hours earlier), heart rate 112 and regular, blood pressure 96/58 (down from 118/72 earlier in the day), respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Weight is 64.2 kg, up 1.8 kg from admission weight of 62.4 kg. The patient is alert but appears fatigued and in significant distress from pain. Oral examination reveals confluent, deep ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate with white-yellow pseudomembranes and areas of bleeding — consistent with World Health Organization grade IV mucositis. Lips are cracked and bleeding. Thick, ropy saliva pools in the oropharynx. The patient gags and winces when attempting to open her mouth fully. Skin is warm, flushed, and dry. The right upper arm PICC dressing is intact but the insertion site shows new erythema extending approximately 2 cm from the insertion point, with mild tenderness on palpation; no purulent drainage is visible, but the area was not erythematous at the previous dressing assessment 48 hours ago. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds. No stool in 3 days. Peripheral edema is trace bilateral in the lower extremities. Urine output has been 120 mL over the last 8 hours (approximately 15 mL/hr), decreased from 40–50 mL/hr the day prior.

Baseline labs drawn 6 hours prior to the nurse's shift: WBC 0.3 × 10³/µL (ANC less than 100/µL), hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, platelets 18,000/µL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.3), sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (low), chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (low), phosphorus 2.8 mg/dL, calcium 8.0 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL (low), prealbumin 8 mg/dL (low, marking nutrition risk and inflammation alongside elevated CRP), blood glucose 268 mg/dL (elevated; was 312 mg/dL four hours before that), AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, triglycerides 310 mg/dL (elevated), lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated), C-reactive protein 14.2 mg/dL (elevated). Blood cultures were drawn from the PICC and peripherally at the time of the temperature spike but results are pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Localized PICC insertion site infection; new erythema extending 2 cm without purulent drainage.
Hypovolemic shock resulting from severe mucositis-associated dehydration; oliguria of 30 mL over 2 hours.
Sepsis likely originating from a catheter-related bloodstream infection; worsening hypotension despite the fluid bolus.
Medication-induced anaphylaxis; the onset of shivering with rigors after antibiotic and electrolyte administration.

rationale_en: The nurse should recognize that the constellation of findings—fever in a neutropenic patient, hemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids, rising lactate, and new PICC insertion site erythema—is most consistent with sepsis, likely from a catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI). The single most critical cue to emphasize is the persistent hemodynamic deterioration despite the fluid bolus, as it signals progression toward septic shock.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after the fluid bolus and electrolyte replacements, the patient's condition continues to worsen. Based on the new clinical findings, what is the most likely emerging complication, and which cue is the highest priority to communicate to the provider?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Update: Stage 3
options_or_rows_en.content: The PICC-drawn blood culture is confirmed as coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (later speciated as Staphylococcus epidermidis), with the PICC culture turning positive more than 2 hours before the peripheral culture, confirming CRBSI. The provider orders PICC removal. Because urgent central access is required to continue TPN and maintain glycemic stability, a new triple-lumen PICC is placed at a new site in the left arm under ID guidance with repeat cultures planned, and TPN is resumed at 60 mL/hr through the new line. Vancomycin is continued; cefepime is continued for neutropenic fever coverage. After 8 hours on the insulin drip, blood glucose has stabilized at 180–200 mg/dL. Repeat labs: potassium 3.8 mEq/L (improving after aggressive replacement), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (improving), creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (plateau, slight improvement), lactate 1.9 mmol/L (trending down), triglycerides 280 mg/dL (still elevated but decreasing with the reduced TPN rate). Temperature is 38.1 °C (trending down from peak), heart rate 98, blood pressure 110/68, respiratory rate 18, SpO₂ 97% on room air (supplemental oxygen weaned). Urine output has improved to 35–40 mL/hr. The patient reports mouth pain is now 6 out of 10 after the PCA dose was optimized and a scheduled IV acetaminophen was added; she is able to tolerate gentle sodium bicarbonate rinses again with coaching. The new PICC site is clean, dry, and without erythema. The nurse evaluates these trends and determines whether the patient is improving, identifies ongoing monitoring priorities, and plans for nutritional reassessment as the mucositis trajectory changes.

stem_en: At Stage 1, the patient's blood glucose is 324 mg/dL and rising despite sliding-scale insulin coverage, and the TPN infusion continues at 85 mL/hr. What is the most appropriate nursing action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs (Start of Shift)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline assessment at start of night shift: Temperature 38.9 °C (102.0 °F) orally (elevated from 37.2 °C twelve hours earlier), heart rate 112 and regular, blood pressure 96/58 (down from 118/72 earlier in the day), respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Weight is 64.2 kg, up 1.8 kg from admission weight of 62.4 kg. The patient is alert but appears fatigued and in significant distress from pain. Oral examination reveals confluent, deep ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate with white-yellow pseudomembranes and areas of bleeding — consistent with World Health Organization grade IV mucositis. Lips are cracked and bleeding. Thick, ropy saliva pools in the oropharynx. The patient gags and winces when attempting to open her mouth fully. Skin is warm, flushed, and dry. The right upper arm PICC dressing is intact but the insertion site shows new erythema extending approximately 2 cm from the insertion point, with mild tenderness on palpation; no purulent drainage is visible, but the area was not erythematous at the previous dressing assessment 48 hours ago. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds. No stool in 3 days. Peripheral edema is trace bilateral in the lower extremities. Urine output has been 120 mL over the last 8 hours (approximately 15 mL/hr), decreased from 40–50 mL/hr the day prior.

Baseline labs drawn 6 hours prior to the nurse's shift: WBC 0.3 × 10³/µL (ANC less than 100/µL), hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, platelets 18,000/µL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.3), sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (low), chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (low), phosphorus 2.8 mg/dL, calcium 8.0 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL (low), prealbumin 8 mg/dL (low, marking nutrition risk and inflammation alongside elevated CRP), blood glucose 268 mg/dL (elevated; was 312 mg/dL four hours before that), AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, triglycerides 310 mg/dL (elevated), lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated), C-reactive protein 14.2 mg/dL (elevated). Blood cultures were drawn from the PICC and peripherally at the time of the temperature spike but results are pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Pause the TPN for one hour, administer the sliding-scale insulin, and restart the TPN once the blood glucose drops below 200 mg/dL.
Independently stop the TPN infusion immediately to prevent severe hyperglycemia from the continuous dextrose load.
Notify the provider of the worsening trend and anticipate an order for a continuous insulin infusion and a possible reduction in the TPN rate.
Continue monitoring the blood glucose every 6 hours and administer the sliding-scale insulin as ordered.

rationale_en: The nurse should notify the provider that the TPN dextrose load is exceeding the patient's insulin capacity, exacerbated by sepsis-related insulin resistance. The correct approach is rate reduction plus an insulin drip, which requires provider orders. The nurse must not independently stop or adjust the TPN rate.

options_or_rows_en.text: Verify that empiric antibiotic coverage (vancomycin) is actively infusing.
Communicate the early flagging of the PICC culture to the provider.
Remove the infected PICC line under provider orders.
Coordinate with the provider for an alternative central access and glycemic management plan.

rationale_en: The correct sequence ensures safe escalation and source control without compromising essential therapies. First, communicate the differential time to positivity (early flagging of the PICC culture), which strongly suggests CRBSI. Second, ensure that systemic treatment (empiric antibiotics) is actively treating the bacteremia. Third, coordinate alternative central access and a glucose plan, because removing the patient's only central line will abruptly stop the TPN, risking hypoglycemia. Finally, once the replacement plan is secure, remove the infected PICC line.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the patient has not performed mouth rinses in over 6 hours due to severe pain from confluent mucositis with bleeding, and she refuses further oral care attempts. What is the priority nursing intervention?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs (Start of Shift)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline assessment at start of night shift: Temperature 38.9 °C (102.0 °F) orally (elevated from 37.2 °C twelve hours earlier), heart rate 112 and regular, blood pressure 96/58 (down from 118/72 earlier in the day), respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Weight is 64.2 kg, up 1.8 kg from admission weight of 62.4 kg. The patient is alert but appears fatigued and in significant distress from pain. Oral examination reveals confluent, deep ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate with white-yellow pseudomembranes and areas of bleeding — consistent with World Health Organization grade IV mucositis. Lips are cracked and bleeding. Thick, ropy saliva pools in the oropharynx. The patient gags and winces when attempting to open her mouth fully. Skin is warm, flushed, and dry. The right upper arm PICC dressing is intact but the insertion site shows new erythema extending approximately 2 cm from the insertion point, with mild tenderness on palpation; no purulent drainage is visible, but the area was not erythematous at the previous dressing assessment 48 hours ago. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds. No stool in 3 days. Peripheral edema is trace bilateral in the lower extremities. Urine output has been 120 mL over the last 8 hours (approximately 15 mL/hr), decreased from 40–50 mL/hr the day prior.

Baseline labs drawn 6 hours prior to the nurse's shift: WBC 0.3 × 10³/µL (ANC less than 100/µL), hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, platelets 18,000/µL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.3), sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (low), chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (low), phosphorus 2.8 mg/dL, calcium 8.0 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL (low), prealbumin 8 mg/dL (low, marking nutrition risk and inflammation alongside elevated CRP), blood glucose 268 mg/dL (elevated; was 312 mg/dL four hours before that), AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, triglycerides 310 mg/dL (elevated), lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated), C-reactive protein 14.2 mg/dL (elevated). Blood cultures were drawn from the PICC and peripherally at the time of the temperature spike but results are pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use a standard soft-bristle toothbrush to thoroughly clean the oral cavity, as skipping oral care increases the risk of secondary infection.
Coordinate analgesia (PCA bolus or topical analgesic) 15 to 20 minutes before assisting the patient with atraumatic sodium bicarbonate rinses.
Forcefully swab the oral cavity with a lemon-glycerin swab to ensure mucosal integrity is maintained despite the patient's refusal.
Defer all oral care until the patient's pain is completely resolved to avoid causing mucosal trauma against her will.

rationale_en: Oral care is a critical intervention to reduce secondary infection risk, but it must be facilitated through adequate pain management, not forced. The nurse should coordinate analgesia before care and use atraumatic technique (gentle rinses or ultra-soft/sponge swabs) given the severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk.

Repeat labs: potassium 3.8 mEq/L (improving after aggressive replacement), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (improving), creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (plateau, slight improvement), lactate 1.9 mmol/L (trending down), triglycerides 280 mg/dL (still elevated but decreasing with the reduced TPN rate). Temperature is 38.1 °C (trending down from peak), heart rate 98, blood pressure 110/68, respiratory rate 18, SpO₂ 97% on room air (supplemental oxygen weaned). Urine output has improved to 35–40 mL/hr. The patient reports mouth pain is now 6 out of 10 after the PCA dose was optimized and a scheduled IV acetaminophen was added; she is able to tolerate gentle sodium bicarbonate rinses again with coaching. The new PICC site is clean, dry, and without erythema. The nurse evaluates these trends and determines whether the patient is improving, identifies ongoing monitoring priorities, and plans for nutritional reassessment as the mucositis trajectory changes.

options_or_rows_en.text: Lactate decreasing from 3.6 to 1.9 mmol/L
Blood glucose stabilizing at 180–200 mg/dL on the insulin drip
Triglycerides 280 mg/dL
Creatinine plateaued at 1.7 mg/dL
Temperature trending down from 39.4 °C to 38.1 °C
Ongoing Monitoring/Intervention
Clinical Improvement

rationale_en: A decreasing lactate, stabilizing blood glucose, and downtrending temperature all indicate a positive response to sepsis treatment and glycemic management. However, elevated triglycerides related to TPN and a persistently abnormal creatinine require ongoing monitoring and potential future intervention.

stem_en: Across the case, the nurse must evaluate the appropriateness of the nutritional delivery method. At Stage 3, the patient can tolerate gentle mouth rinses with coaching but cannot swallow liquids. Is it appropriate to discontinue the TPN and transition to enteral nutrition?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs (Start of Shift)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline assessment at start of night shift: Temperature 38.9 °C (102.0 °F) orally (elevated from 37.2 °C twelve hours earlier), heart rate 112 and regular, blood pressure 96/58 (down from 118/72 earlier in the day), respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Weight is 64.2 kg, up 1.8 kg from admission weight of 62.4 kg. The patient is alert but appears fatigued and in significant distress from pain. Oral examination reveals confluent, deep ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate with white-yellow pseudomembranes and areas of bleeding — consistent with World Health Organization grade IV mucositis. Lips are cracked and bleeding. Thick, ropy saliva pools in the oropharynx. The patient gags and winces when attempting to open her mouth fully. Skin is warm, flushed, and dry. The right upper arm PICC dressing is intact but the insertion site shows new erythema extending approximately 2 cm from the insertion point, with mild tenderness on palpation; no purulent drainage is visible, but the area was not erythematous at the previous dressing assessment 48 hours ago. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds. No stool in 3 days. Peripheral edema is trace bilateral in the lower extremities. Urine output has been 120 mL over the last 8 hours (approximately 15 mL/hr), decreased from 40–50 mL/hr the day prior.

Baseline labs drawn 6 hours prior to the nurse's shift: WBC 0.3 × 10³/µL (ANC less than 100/µL), hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, platelets 18,000/µL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.3), sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (low), chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (low), phosphorus 2.8 mg/dL, calcium 8.0 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL (low), prealbumin 8 mg/dL (low, marking nutrition risk and inflammation alongside elevated CRP), blood glucose 268 mg/dL (elevated; was 312 mg/dL four hours before that), AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, triglycerides 310 mg/dL (elevated), lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated), C-reactive protein 14.2 mg/dL (elevated). Blood cultures were drawn from the PICC and peripherally at the time of the temperature spike but results are pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: No, because a newly placed PICC line must be used for TPN for at least 72 hours to ensure catheter patency before any enteral trials can begin.
No, because the patient has grade IV mucositis with severe systemic inflammation, and tolerating mouth rinses is not equivalent to tolerating nutritional intake.
Yes, because the patient's ability to tolerate sodium bicarbonate rinses indicates sufficient mucosal recovery to safely swallow clear liquids.
Yes, because prolonged TPN dependence significantly increases the risk of another central line infection, making enteral nutrition the safer immediate option.

rationale_en: TPN must be continued. Transitioning to enteral nutrition requires evidence that the mucosa can tolerate intake without aspiration or severe pain. Tolerating small volumes of rinses that are spit out is not equivalent to swallowing nutritional volumes through an ulcerated esophagus.

stem_en: A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium chloride-containing IV fluid infusing. The nurse reviews the potassium trend in the visual and notes new peaked T waves on telemetry. Place the nursing actions in the order they should be performed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Place the client on continuous cardiac monitoring and assess rhythm.
Notify the provider or rapid response team of worsening hyperkalemia with ECG changes.
Stop the potassium-containing IV fluid.
Prepare to administer prescribed insulin with dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly.
Prepare to administer prescribed IV calcium gluconate.

rationale_en: The harmful potassium source should be stopped first. Continuous monitoring follows because ECG changes are present. The provider or emergency team must be notified, and prescribed calcium protects the myocardium before therapies that shift potassium into cells.

options_or_rows_en.text: Assess and document his decision-making capacity and verbatim statements in the medical record.
Acknowledge his feelings with empathy and mention it to the provider during rounds.
Assure the patient that the healthcare team will not intubate him.
Immediately enter a temporary DNAR/DNI order pending the provider's evaluation.

rationale_en: The nurse must actively listen, assess and document that the patient has decision-making capacity, document his verbatim statements immediately, explain honestly that a provider order is required, and begin the escalation process. Documentation creates a contemporaneous legal and clinical record that protects the patient's autonomy if he later loses capacity.

options_or_rows_en.text: Communicate the refusal immediately, activate the rapid response team/chain of command, and ensure an authorized provider evaluates capacity and enters orders.

rationale_en: A conscious, capacitated patient has the right to actively refuse medical interventions. The nurse cannot unilaterally change the code status.

options_or_rows_en.content: After Mr. Jeffries clarifies he does not want endotracheal intubation, mechanical ventilation, or CPR (but accepts oxygen and comfort meds), the nurse documents his verbatim statements and capacity. At 0745, the attending's office states he is scrubbing for a case and unavailable until 1400. The covering hospitalist, Dr. Reeves, is paged and responds at 0800: 'I am rounding on the other side of the hospital. I'll see him around 1100.' The code status remains Full Code.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course
options_or_rows_en.content:
At 0930, portable CXR shows a large recurrent right-sided pleural effusion with mediastinal shift and a new left pleural effusion.
At 0945, patient becomes acutely dyspneic. SpO₂ 88% on 3 L. Nurse increases O₂ to 6 L face mask; SpO₂ improves to 90%. 
At 0950, nurse pages Dr. Reeves about the acute respiratory failure and wishes against intubation. Dr. Reeves replies he will come 'as soon as I can.'
At 1030, SpO₂ is 89% on 6 L face mask, RR 30, HR 118, BP 102/64. Patient says between breaths: 'Please... don't let them... put a tube in me.' Nurse switches to non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min; SpO₂ rises to 91%.

stem_en: Stage 1: Fifteen minutes after the vancomycin loading dose begins, the client develops intense itching, flushing limited to the face/neck/upper trunk, chest wheals, tachycardia, and mild hypotension. He has no wheezing, stridor, tongue swelling, throat tightness, or generalized urticaria. Which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
Age: 67 years. Admitted from the emergency department with two blood-culture sets showing gram-positive cocci in clusters with preliminary MRSA identification. Suspected source: infected left forearm arteriovenous fistula with erythema, warmth, induration, tenderness, and purulent drainage.
History: stage 3b chronic kidney disease; baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL; estimated baseline creatinine clearance 44 mL/min; type 2 diabetes; hypertension; obesity; bilateral sensorineural hearing loss managed with hearing aids. No documented drug allergies.
Orders: vancomycin 2,000 mg IV loading dose over at least 2 hours, then 1,250 mg IV every 12 hours; vancomycin trough before the fourth maintenance dose; normal saline 75 mL/hr; strict intake and output; daily basic metabolic panel; repeat blood cultures in 48 hours; transthoracic echocardiogram; vascular surgery consult. Home medications continued, including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily. No premedication is ordered before the first vancomycin dose.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue the infusion at the same rate because flushing is an expected effect during the first dose.
Remove the peripheral IV catheter and request an alternative antibiotic immediately.
Administer intramuscular epinephrine and document a vancomycin allergy.
Stop the infusion, keep the IV patent with normal saline, place the client supine with legs slightly elevated, obtain vital signs, and notify the provider.
Slow the infusion rate without stopping it, then reassess when the dose is complete.

rationale_en: The regional upper-body flushing, pruritus, timing soon after the infusion starts, absence of airway findings, and absence of generalized urticaria are most consistent with vancomycin flushing syndrome. The immediate nursing response is to stop the infusion, maintain access, support circulation, reassess, and notify the provider. Antihistamine administration and restarting at a slower rate may follow provider orders.

stem_en: Stage 1: Fifteen minutes after the vancomycin loading dose begins, the client develops intense itching, flushing limited to the face/neck/upper trunk, chest wheals, tachycardia, and mild hypotension. He has no wheezing, stridor, tongue swelling, throat tightness, or generalized urticaria. Which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: 0815 provider response
options_or_rows_en.content: The infectious disease provider orders: hold vancomycin; obtain a stat vancomycin level; switch to renally dosed daptomycin 6 mg/kg IV every 48 hours; hold lisinopril; obtain a renal ultrasound; request nephrology consult. The stat vancomycin level later returns at 24.1 mcg/mL.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue the infusion at the same rate because flushing is an expected effect during the first dose.
Remove the peripheral IV catheter and request an alternative antibiotic immediately.
Administer intramuscular epinephrine and document a vancomycin allergy.
Stop the infusion, keep the IV patent with normal saline, place the client supine with legs slightly elevated, obtain vital signs, and notify the provider.
Slow the infusion rate without stopping it, then reassess when the dose is complete.

rationale_en: The regional upper-body flushing, pruritus, timing soon after the infusion starts, absence of airway findings, and absence of generalized urticaria are most consistent with vancomycin flushing syndrome. The immediate nursing response is to stop the infusion, maintain access, support circulation, reassess, and notify the provider. Antihistamine administration and restarting at a slower rate may follow provider orders.

stem_en: Stage 1: The flushing and itching resolve after the infusion is stopped and diphenhydramine is administered. The provider restarts the remaining dose at a slower rate without recurrence of symptoms. Complete the nursing documentation and handoff statement.

options_or_rows_en.content:

Home medications continued, including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily. No premedication is ordered before the first vancomycin dose.

options.text: permanent avoidance of all glycopeptide antibiotics
premedication as ordered and an infusion rate no faster than 10 mg/min

rationale_en: The clinical pattern and successful slower rechallenge support a rate-related infusion reaction rather than a true IgE-mediated allergy. Documenting the exact reaction, interventions, and future rate/premedication needs protects the client from both recurrence and unnecessary loss of a key MRSA therapy.

options_or_rows_en.content:
Age: 67 years. Admitted from the emergency department with two blood-culture sets showing gram-positive cocci in clusters with preliminary MRSA identification. Suspected source: infected left forearm arteriovenous fistula with erythema, warmth, induration, tenderness, and purulent drainage.

Orders: vancomycin 2,000 mg IV loading dose over at least 2 hours, then 1,250 mg IV every 12 hours; vancomycin trough before the fourth maintenance dose; normal saline 75 mL/hr; strict intake and output; daily basic metabolic panel; repeat blood cultures in 48 hours; transthoracic echocardiogram; vascular surgery consult. Home medications continued, including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily. No premedication is ordered before the first vancomycin dose.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1400 baseline assessment and ED laboratory results
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital signs: temperature 38.6 °C, heart rate 96/min regular, blood pressure 142/84 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air.
Assessment: alert and oriented; no rash, flushing, urticaria, or angioedema; lungs clear; chronic 1+ bilateral lower-extremity edema; right antecubital 18-gauge peripheral IV flushes easily. Hearing is functional with hearing aids in place.
Labs drawn at 1200: WBC 14,800/µL with 82% neutrophils and 9% bands; hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL; platelets 228,000/µL; sodium 138 mEq/L; potassium 4.6 mEq/L; bicarbonate 22 mEq/L; BUN 32 mg/dL; creatinine 1.8 mg/dL; eGFR 38 mL/min; glucose 198 mg/dL; lactate 2.2 mmol/L; albumin 2.8 g/dL. Urinalysis: trace protein, no casts, no bacteria.

options_or_rows_en.title: 15 minutes after vancomycin starts
options_or_rows_en.content:
The 2,000 mg loading dose was started at 1430 by infusion pump to run over 120 minutes. At 1445, the client reports sudden intense itching of the face, neck, and upper chest and says his skin feels like it is burning. He denies shortness of breath, throat tightness, tongue swelling, or difficulty swallowing.
Assessment: face, neck, and upper trunk are diffusely flushed with a clear lower boundary near the nipple line; lower trunk and extremities are unaffected. Scattered raised, pruritic, blanchable wheals are present across the anterior chest. No stridor, wheezing, lip or tongue angioedema, or urticaria below the waist. He is anxious but alert and breathing comfortably.
Vital signs: heart rate 118/min, blood pressure 108/62 mm Hg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1 response after intervention
options_or_rows_en.content: The infusion is stopped, the IV is kept patent with normal saline, the client is positioned supine with legs slightly elevated, vital signs are repeated, and the provider is notified. Diphenhydramine 25 mg IV and acetaminophen 650 mg PO are administered as ordered. Symptoms improve after 30 minutes. The provider orders vancomycin to restart at half the original rate. The remaining dose completes without recurrent symptoms. The event is documented as an infusion-related adverse drug reaction rather than a drug allergy.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0830 pharmacist call
options_or_rows_en.content:
The client has received three maintenance doses of vancomycin 1,250 mg IV every 12 hours without recurrent flushing; each dose was premedicated with diphenhydramine and infused over at least 2 hours. The trough drawn at 0730 before the fourth maintenance dose returns at 0830: 22.4 mcg/mL. Institutional trough-surrogate protocol flags this as supratherapeutic.
Pharmacist recommendation being communicated to the infectious disease provider: reduce vancomycin to 1,000 mg IV every 12 hours, recheck the trough before the third dose on the new regimen, and increase monitoring for nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
Morning labs: WBC 12,200/µL, creatinine 1.7 mg/dL, BUN 28 mg/dL, potassium 4.4 mEq/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, glucose 164 mg/dL. Urine output previous 24 hours: 1,440 mL.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0730 new data before morning dose
options_or_rows_en.content:
The client has received vancomycin 1,000 mg IV every 12 hours since the dose adjustment. Current trough: 18.6 mcg/mL, within the institutional surrogate target range.
Vital signs: temperature 37.4 °C, heart rate 82/min, blood pressure 148/88 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air.
Morning labs: creatinine 2.4 mg/dL, BUN 44 mg/dL, potassium 5.1 mEq/L, bicarbonate 19 mEq/L, eGFR 26 mL/min. Overnight urine output: 180 mL in 12 hours. Urine is dark amber and concentrated.
The client reports new persistent bilateral ringing in the ears that began overnight. Repeat blood cultures from day 2 show no growth at 48 hours; echocardiogram shows no vegetations; AV fistula erythema and drainage are improving. Lower-extremity edema is now 2+ bilaterally.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0815 provider response
options_or_rows_en.content: The infectious disease provider orders: hold vancomycin; obtain a stat vancomycin level; switch to renally dosed daptomycin 6 mg/kg IV every 48 hours; hold lisinopril; obtain a renal ultrasound; request nephrology consult. The stat vancomycin level later returns at 24.1 mcg/mL.

Administer the revised dose using the ordered premedication and slower infusion precautions.

stem_en: Stage 2: The pharmacist recommends increased ototoxicity monitoring. The client has baseline bilateral hearing loss and uses hearing aids. Which nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
Age: 67 years. Admitted from the emergency department with two blood-culture sets showing gram-positive cocci in clusters with preliminary MRSA identification. Suspected source: infected left forearm arteriovenous fistula with erythema, warmth, induration, tenderness, and purulent drainage.
History: stage 3b chronic kidney disease; baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL; estimated baseline creatinine clearance 44 mL/min; type 2 diabetes; hypertension; obesity; bilateral sensorineural hearing loss managed with hearing aids. No documented drug allergies.
Orders: vancomycin 2,000 mg IV loading dose over at least 2 hours, then 1,250 mg IV every 12 hours; vancomycin trough before the fourth maintenance dose; normal saline 75 mL/hr; strict intake and output; daily basic metabolic panel; repeat blood cultures in 48 hours; transthoracic echocardiogram; vascular surgery consult. Home medications continued, including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily. No premedication is ordered before the first vancomycin dose.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask the client to remove hearing aids and perform a repeatable bedside hearing screen, such as a whispered voice test in each ear.
Chart hearing as within normal limits because the client can converse while wearing hearing aids.
Ask specifically about new tinnitus, dizziness, vertigo, or subjective hearing change.
Document a dated patient-specific baseline and compare future assessments with that baseline.
Delay further screening until the client reports complete hearing loss.
Focus only on serum creatinine because ototoxicity cannot be screened at the bedside by a nurse.

rationale_en: Ototoxicity monitoring within RN scope includes a repeatable bedside hearing screen, targeted symptom questions, and documentation of an individualized baseline. This is especially important because pre-existing hearing loss reduces auditory reserve; future concern is based on change from this client's baseline, not comparison with normal hearing.

stem_en: Stage 3: Classify each finding based on how it should affect the nurse's interpretation of the scheduled 0800 vancomycin dose.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
Age: 67 years. Admitted from the emergency department with two blood-culture sets showing gram-positive cocci in clusters with preliminary MRSA identification. Suspected source: infected left forearm arteriovenous fistula with erythema, warmth, induration, tenderness, and purulent drainage.
History: stage 3b chronic kidney disease; baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL; estimated baseline creatinine clearance 44 mL/min; type 2 diabetes; hypertension; obesity; bilateral sensorineural hearing loss managed with hearing aids. No documented drug allergies.
Orders: vancomycin 2,000 mg IV loading dose over at least 2 hours, then 1,250 mg IV every 12 hours; vancomycin trough before the fourth maintenance dose; normal saline 75 mL/hr; strict intake and output; daily basic metabolic panel; repeat blood cultures in 48 hours; transthoracic echocardiogram; vascular surgery consult. Home medications continued, including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily. No premedication is ordered before the first vancomycin dose.

options_or_rows_en.text: Creatinine increased to 2.4 mg/dL with urine output 180 mL in 12 hours.
New persistent bilateral tinnitus began overnight.
Vancomycin trough is 18.6 mcg/mL, within the institutional surrogate target range.
Repeat blood cultures show no growth at 48 hours.
The AV fistula site has less erythema and drainage.
Priority toxicity cue: hold and escalate
Does not prove the next dose is safe
Infection improving, but not a reason to ignore toxicity

rationale_en: The creatinine rise with oliguria and new tinnitus are priority toxicity cues, even though the trough appears within the institutional target range. The trough does not prove safety when end-organ toxicity is present. Improving cultures and fistula appearance are favorable infection-response cues, but they do not justify administering another vancomycin dose into worsening renal clearance.

stem_en: Stage 3: Vancomycin has been held and the antibiotic plan is being changed because of acute kidney injury and new tinnitus. The nurse reviews the medication administration record and sees that lisinopril 10 mg daily is still active and the morning dose has not been given. Potassium is 5.1 mEq/L. What should the nurse do?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
Age: 67 years. Admitted from the emergency department with two blood-culture sets showing gram-positive cocci in clusters with preliminary MRSA identification. Suspected source: infected left forearm arteriovenous fistula with erythema, warmth, induration, tenderness, and purulent drainage.
History: stage 3b chronic kidney disease; baseline creatinine 1.6 mg/dL; estimated baseline creatinine clearance 44 mL/min; type 2 diabetes; hypertension; obesity; bilateral sensorineural hearing loss managed with hearing aids. No documented drug allergies.
Orders: vancomycin 2,000 mg IV loading dose over at least 2 hours, then 1,250 mg IV every 12 hours; vancomycin trough before the fourth maintenance dose; normal saline 75 mL/hr; strict intake and output; daily basic metabolic panel; repeat blood cultures in 48 hours; transthoracic echocardiogram; vascular surgery consult. Home medications continued, including lisinopril 10 mg daily and carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily. No premedication is ordered before the first vancomycin dose.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer lisinopril and recheck potassium with the next morning laboratory draw.
Discontinue lisinopril permanently in the medication administration record without contacting the provider.
Administer lisinopril because it is a home medication and the blood pressure is 148/88 mm Hg.
Hold the lisinopril dose and notify the provider to clarify whether it should be temporarily discontinued.

rationale_en: The clinical picture has changed: the client now has acute kidney injury, oliguria, and potassium 5.1 mEq/L. An ACE inhibitor can further reduce glomerular filtration pressure and can worsen hyperkalemia. The nurse should hold the dose for safety and promptly clarify the order with the provider rather than administering it as routine or independently discontinuing it permanently.

stem_en: Postoperative day 0: The admission record lists Mandarin as the client's primary language, but no ongoing postoperative language-access plan is documented. Which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 0: admission record and initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
1530: Mrs. Chen, age 82, is admitted to the orthopedic surgical unit after uncomplicated elective right total hip arthroplasty for osteoarthritis. Primary language: Mandarin. She can exchange simple greetings in English but cannot reliably discuss medications, follow multistep instructions, or read English discharge documents. She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment. Her adult son lives 2 hours away and works full-time. She does not own a smartphone and uses a corded landline at home.

History: hypertension, mild chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, eGFR 42 mL/min), and type 2 diabetes. No history of venous thromboembolism.

Orders: rivaroxaban 10 mg PO daily for 35 days for VTE prophylaxis; acetaminophen 1 g PO every 6 hr scheduled; oxycodone 5 mg PO every 4 hr PRN breakthrough pain; home amlodipine and metformin; incentive spirometry every 1-2 hr while awake; sequential compression devices while in bed; physical therapy consult on postoperative day 1; posterior hip precautions: no flexion beyond 90 degrees, no internal rotation, and no adduction past midline.

Initial findings at 1600: T 36.8 °C, HR 82, BP 138/78, RR 16, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. Alert and oriented to person and place with interpreter phone assistance. Pain 5/10. Dressing clean, dry, intact; drain output 50 mL sanguineous since OR. Right pedal pulse 2+, capillary refill <3 sec, sensation intact, and dorsiflexion/plantarflexion present. Foley catheter draining clear yellow urine. Call light within reach and bed alarm activated. The nurse observes the client leaning across the bed rail for water, suggesting she may not understand fall-prevention instructions.

Baseline labs: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (pre-op 12.1), Hct 32%, platelets 198,000/µL, WBC 9,200/µL, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, BUN 24 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR 38 mL/min, K⁺ 4.0 mEq/L, Na⁺ 139 mEq/L, glucose 162 mg/dL, albumin 3.1 g/dL.

Chart concern: Mandarin is listed as the primary language. Informed consent was signed with an interpreter present, but no ongoing postoperative language-access plan is documented.

options_or_rows_en.text: Wait for the surgical team to update the language-access plan during morning rounds.
Ask the son to interpret by telephone because he will help after discharge.
Activate qualified interpreter services now, document the preferred language in the care plan, and flag the need for interpreter-supported discharge teaching.
Use simple English phrases and gestures until the formal discharge teaching session.

rationale_en: The nurse should act immediately on the identified language-access gap. Qualified interpreter services are needed for assessment, symptom reporting, safety teaching, and informed participation in care; documenting the need also allows the team to plan complex discharge teaching ahead of time.

stem_en: Postoperative day 0: The admission record lists Mandarin as the client's primary language, but no ongoing postoperative language-access plan is documented. Which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Morning discharge note
options_or_rows_en.content:
About 66 hr after surgery: T 36.7 °C, HR 72, BP 126/70, RR 14, SpO₂ 99% on room air. Pain 2/10 at rest and 4/10 with movement, controlled with scheduled acetaminophen alone; client declined the last two oxycodone doses. Incision clean and dry. Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, eGFR 42 mL/min. Hgb 9.8 g/dL and stable. Glucose 132 mg/dL.

Final readiness verification: The Visiting Nurse Association accepts the referral. First home visit for skilled nursing and PT is confirmed for the day after discharge. The nurse calls the receiving home health nurse using SBAR and communicates language needs, the need for an interpreter for teaching, social situation, functional status, equipment, rivaroxaban dose/duration and adherence concerns, renal-function trend, bleeding surveillance, son's role as the communication contact, and the confirmed follow-up appointment. The home health agency confirms that a Mandarin-speaking home health aide is available.

The nurse calls the surgeon's office to confirm the 2-week follow-up appointment and requests that a Mandarin interpreter be scheduled. The son arrives at 0900. With video interpreter assistance, the nurse reviews discharge instructions with the client and son together, confirms that the son understands how to reach the surgeon's office, provides the home health agency phone number, and gives the client a 7-day pill organizer labeled in Mandarin. The client is discharged at 1030 by wheelchair to her son's car with the walker, raised toilet seat, and reacher/grabber. The nurse documents that all identified discharge barriers are resolved or have a confirmed community plan of care.

stem_en: Postoperative day 1: The nurse uses an English handout to teach hip precautions and rivaroxaban safety. With interpreter phone assistance, the client cannot state the medication name, purpose, bleeding signs, or missed-dose instructions. Which nursing action is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 0: admission record and initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
1530: Mrs. Chen, age 82, is admitted to the orthopedic surgical unit after uncomplicated elective right total hip arthroplasty for osteoarthritis. Primary language: Mandarin. She can exchange simple greetings in English but cannot reliably discuss medications, follow multistep instructions, or read English discharge documents. She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment. Her adult son lives 2 hours away and works full-time. She does not own a smartphone and uses a corded landline at home.

History: hypertension, mild chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, eGFR 42 mL/min), and type 2 diabetes. No history of venous thromboembolism.

Orders: rivaroxaban 10 mg PO daily for 35 days for VTE prophylaxis; acetaminophen 1 g PO every 6 hr scheduled; oxycodone 5 mg PO every 4 hr PRN breakthrough pain; home amlodipine and metformin; incentive spirometry every 1-2 hr while awake; sequential compression devices while in bed; physical therapy consult on postoperative day 1; posterior hip precautions: no flexion beyond 90 degrees, no internal rotation, and no adduction past midline.

Initial findings at 1600: T 36.8 °C, HR 82, BP 138/78, RR 16, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. Alert and oriented to person and place with interpreter phone assistance. Pain 5/10. Dressing clean, dry, intact; drain output 50 mL sanguineous since OR. Right pedal pulse 2+, capillary refill <3 sec, sensation intact, and dorsiflexion/plantarflexion present. Foley catheter draining clear yellow urine. Call light within reach and bed alarm activated. The nurse observes the client leaning across the bed rail for water, suggesting she may not understand fall-prevention instructions.

Baseline labs: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (pre-op 12.1), Hct 32%, platelets 198,000/µL, WBC 9,200/µL, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, BUN 24 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR 38 mL/min, K⁺ 4.0 mEq/L, Na⁺ 139 mEq/L, glucose 162 mg/dL, albumin 3.1 g/dL.

Chart concern: Mandarin is listed as the primary language. Informed consent was signed with an interpreter present, but no ongoing postoperative language-access plan is documented.

options_or_rows_en.text: Repeat the same English handout more slowly and ask yes/no questions to confirm understanding.
Send the client home with written instructions because home health nursing will reinforce teaching tomorrow.
Document the failed teach-back, do not treat the client as discharge-ready, and arrange qualified Mandarin interpreter-supported comprehensive teaching.
Ask the son to interpret the medication instructions by phone and document education as completed.

rationale_en: Teach-back failure is evidence that the client does not yet understand information required for safe self-care. The nurse should change the teaching approach, arrange qualified language support, and delay discharge-readiness clearance until understanding is demonstrated.

options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: Notify the charge nurse and surgical team that the planned discharge may be unsafe until barriers are addressed.
Reassess and document discharge readiness after services, teaching, mobility, equipment, follow-up, and medication-safety plans are confirmed.
Proceed with the planned discharge because vital signs and the incision are stable, and arrange home services after discharge if problems occur.
Coordinate with PT for additional walker training, stair assessment, and equipment recommendations.
Request social work/case management involvement to evaluate support, payer requirements, home health needs, and alternative post-acute options.
Arrange certified Mandarin interpreter-supported discharge teaching and translated written materials.

rationale_en: All of the barrier-focused actions are needed: the team must be alerted that discharge is not yet safe, social work/case management must address support and post-acute services, certified interpreter-supported teaching and translated materials must be arranged, PT must complete mobility/stair/equipment planning, and the nurse must reassess readiness after the plans are confirmed. Stable vital signs and a clean incision do not make discharge safe when mobility, teaching, anticoagulant monitoring, language access, and home support barriers remain unresolved.

options_or_rows_en.title: Morning assessment, PT note, and education attempt
options_or_rows_en.content:
About 18 hr after surgery: T 37.2 °C, HR 78, BP 132/74, RR 14, SpO₂ 97% on room air. Pain 4/10 at rest and 7/10 with movement using an interpreter phone. Drain removed per order; incision approximated with staples, mild ecchymosis, no erythema, and scant serous drainage. Foley removed; client voided 250 mL. Oral medications tolerated.

Repeat labs: Hgb 10.2 g/dL, creatinine 1.5 mg/dL, eGFR 36 mL/min, glucose 148 mg/dL.

PT session: Client sits at the edge of the bed and stands with a front-wheeled walker using moderate assistance from two people. She is unsteady, attempts to lift the walker, loses balance once but is caught by PT, and cannot safely rise from a standard-height chair independently. PT documents that she is not safe for discharge home without continued walker training and a home-safety evaluation. PT recommends at least two more sessions and home PT after discharge.

Teaching attempt: The nurse attempts hip-precaution and anticoagulant teaching using an English handout. The client nods and smiles but cannot demonstrate understanding. With interpreter phone assistance, she cannot state the medication name, purpose, bleeding signs to report, or what to do if a dose is missed. The nurse documents failed teach-back and the need for a certified Mandarin interpreter for comprehensive discharge teaching.

Unresolved discharge barriers: lives alone with no confirmed support for the first 48-72 hr; unsafe walker use; discharge teaching incomplete because of language barrier; no home health referral; uncertain rivaroxaban adherence; second-floor walk-up with no stair assessment; no confirmed follow-up appointment; creatinine trending upward while receiving a renally cleared anticoagulant.

options_or_rows_en.title: Coordination update, renal trend, and teach-back session
options_or_rows_en.content:
About 42 hr after surgery: T 36.9 °C, HR 74, BP 128/72, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% on room air. Pain 3/10 at rest and 5/10 with movement. Incision clean with staples intact and no signs of infection. Repeat creatinine 1.4 mg/dL after hydration and adequate oral intake; eGFR 39 mL/min. Hgb 10.0 g/dL.

Social work/case management interviews the client using a telephone interpreter. Her son can be present on the day of discharge and the following day, but not beyond that. She receives Meals on Wheels and has Medicare with a supplemental plan. Case management determines that she qualifies for Medicare home health services because she is homebound and requires skilled nursing plus home PT. The home health referral is initiated.

PT session: Client demonstrates safe walker use on level ground with standby assistance and can perform sit-to-stand from a raised toilet seat independently. Stairs have not yet been attempted. Afternoon PT stair training is planned.

Interpreter-mediated education: A certified in-person Mandarin interpreter is present. The nurse teaches posterior hip precautions, rivaroxaban purpose and 10 mg once-daily schedule for 35 days, bleeding signs, missed-dose instructions, pain medication safety, and when to call the surgeon or seek emergency care. The client accurately repeats key points in Mandarin, demonstrates hip precautions with a model, and identifies the rivaroxaban pill. She states she would need her son to call the surgeon because she cannot navigate the English phone tree alone. The nurse flags this as a remaining barrier.

Afternoon PT: Client negotiates four stairs up and down with a walker and one handrail with standby assistance. PT clears her for discharge home with home PT, front-wheeled walker, raised toilet seat, and reacher/grabber. The nurse requests a Mandarin-translated home exercise program. The surgeon writes a discharge order for postoperative day 3 morning pending nursing confirmation of discharge readiness.

options_or_rows_en.text: Notify the prescribing provider of the creatinine/eGFR trend and confirm the anticoagulant plan.
Hold the rivaroxaban independently because the creatinine increased on postoperative day 1.
Teach the client to report blood in urine or stool, black stools, unusual bruising, bleeding gums, dizziness, or weakness.
Tell the client that the 10 mg dose must be taken with a full meal for absorption.
Include renal-function trend, anticoagulant adherence, and bleeding surveillance in the home health handoff.
Inappropriate
Appropriate

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes a relevant renal-function trend, communicates it to the prescriber, reinforces bleeding precautions and missed-dose safety, and includes monitoring needs in the handoff. The nurse should not independently hold an ordered anticoagulant, and the 10 mg rivaroxaban dose for hip replacement prophylaxis may be taken with or without food.

Baseline labs: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (pre-op 12.1), Hct 32%, platelets 198,000/µL, WBC 9,200/µL, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, BUN 24 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR 38 mL/min, K⁺ 4.0 mEq/L, Na⁺ 139 mEq/L, glucose 162 mg/dL, albumin 3.1 g/dL.

Chart concern: Mandarin is listed as the primary language. Informed consent was signed with an interpreter present, but no ongoing postoperative language-access plan is documented.

options_or_rows_en.text: Provide large-print Mandarin instructions with the surgeon and home health phone numbers, or help set up accessible landline speed-dial instructions.
Ask the surgeon's office to schedule a Mandarin interpreter for the follow-up appointment.
Take no further action because the client correctly repeated when to seek help.
Tell the client to call 911 for all postoperative questions because she cannot use the office phone tree.
Communicate the phone-access barrier and family-contact plan to the home health agency during handoff.
Confirm that the son agrees to serve as the designated phone contact for the surgeon's office and document the plan.

rationale_en: A safe discharge plan must account for the client's actual ability to access help, not only her ability to name danger signs. The nurse should build redundancy through a documented family contact, accessible written/phone supports, home health reinforcement, and interpreter support for follow-up care.

stem_en: Postoperative day 2: Interpreter-supported teach-back is successful, but the client states she cannot independently navigate the surgeon's English-language phone system. Which actions should the nurse include in the discharge plan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Morning discharge note
options_or_rows_en.content:
About 66 hr after surgery: T 36.7 °C, HR 72, BP 126/70, RR 14, SpO₂ 99% on room air. Pain 2/10 at rest and 4/10 with movement, controlled with scheduled acetaminophen alone; client declined the last two oxycodone doses. Incision clean and dry. Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, eGFR 42 mL/min. Hgb 9.8 g/dL and stable. Glucose 132 mg/dL.

Final readiness verification: The Visiting Nurse Association accepts the referral. First home visit for skilled nursing and PT is confirmed for the day after discharge. The nurse calls the receiving home health nurse using SBAR and communicates language needs, the need for an interpreter for teaching, social situation, functional status, equipment, rivaroxaban dose/duration and adherence concerns, renal-function trend, bleeding surveillance, son's role as the communication contact, and the confirmed follow-up appointment. The home health agency confirms that a Mandarin-speaking home health aide is available.

The nurse calls the surgeon's office to confirm the 2-week follow-up appointment and requests that a Mandarin interpreter be scheduled. The son arrives at 0900. With video interpreter assistance, the nurse reviews discharge instructions with the client and son together, confirms that the son understands how to reach the surgeon's office, provides the home health agency phone number, and gives the client a 7-day pill organizer labeled in Mandarin. The client is discharged at 1030 by wheelchair to her son's car with the walker, raised toilet seat, and reacher/grabber. The nurse documents that all identified discharge barriers are resolved or have a confirmed community plan of care.

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 0: admission record and initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
1530: Mrs. Chen, age 82, is admitted to the orthopedic surgical unit after uncomplicated elective right total hip arthroplasty for osteoarthritis. Primary language: Mandarin. She can exchange simple greetings in English but cannot reliably discuss medications, follow multistep instructions, or read English discharge documents. She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment. Her adult son lives 2 hours away and works full-time. She does not own a smartphone and uses a corded landline at home.

History: hypertension, mild chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, eGFR 42 mL/min), and type 2 diabetes. No history of venous thromboembolism.

Orders: rivaroxaban 10 mg PO daily for 35 days for VTE prophylaxis; acetaminophen 1 g PO every 6 hr scheduled; oxycodone 5 mg PO every 4 hr PRN breakthrough pain; home amlodipine and metformin; incentive spirometry every 1-2 hr while awake; sequential compression devices while in bed; physical therapy consult on postoperative day 1; posterior hip precautions: no flexion beyond 90 degrees, no internal rotation, and no adduction past midline.

Initial findings at 1600: T 36.8 °C, HR 82, BP 138/78, RR 16, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. Alert and oriented to person and place with interpreter phone assistance. Pain 5/10. Dressing clean, dry, intact; drain output 50 mL sanguineous since OR. Right pedal pulse 2+, capillary refill <3 sec, sensation intact, and dorsiflexion/plantarflexion present. Foley catheter draining clear yellow urine. Call light within reach and bed alarm activated. The nurse observes the client leaning across the bed rail for water, suggesting she may not understand fall-prevention instructions.

Baseline labs: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (pre-op 12.1), Hct 32%, platelets 198,000/µL, WBC 9,200/µL, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, BUN 24 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR 38 mL/min, K⁺ 4.0 mEq/L, Na⁺ 139 mEq/L, glucose 162 mg/dL, albumin 3.1 g/dL.

Chart concern: Mandarin is listed as the primary language. Informed consent was signed with an interpreter present, but no ongoing postoperative language-access plan is documented.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client requires Mandarin interpretation for teaching and clinical communication.
The client lives alone in a second-floor walk-up after the son leaves, and home safety/stair navigation need assessment.
Creatinine trended up transiently and returned to baseline; ongoing renal-function awareness is needed.
Equipment, functional status, follow-up appointment, and son's role as the phone contact have been confirmed.
Rivaroxaban 10 mg daily is prescribed for 35 days; adherence and bleeding signs need monitoring.
The client preferred hot tea during hospitalization and declined the last two oxycodone doses.

rationale_en: The handoff must transmit the risks that could cause transition failure: language needs, anticoagulant adherence and bleeding monitoring, renal-function context, social isolation and home layout, mobility/equipment needs, follow-up, and the designated communication contact. These data allow the home health nurse to continue the safety plan without information loss.

options_or_rows_en.title: Morning assessment, PT note, and education attempt
options_or_rows_en.content:
About 18 hr after surgery: T 37.2 °C, HR 78, BP 132/74, RR 14, SpO₂ 97% on room air. Pain 4/10 at rest and 7/10 with movement using an interpreter phone. Drain removed per order; incision approximated with staples, mild ecchymosis, no erythema, and scant serous drainage. Foley removed; client voided 250 mL. Oral medications tolerated.

Repeat labs: Hgb 10.2 g/dL, creatinine 1.5 mg/dL, eGFR 36 mL/min, glucose 148 mg/dL.

PT session: Client sits at the edge of the bed and stands with a front-wheeled walker using moderate assistance from two people. She is unsteady, attempts to lift the walker, loses balance once but is caught by PT, and cannot safely rise from a standard-height chair independently. PT documents that she is not safe for discharge home without continued walker training and a home-safety evaluation. PT recommends at least two more sessions and home PT after discharge.

Teaching attempt: The nurse attempts hip-precaution and anticoagulant teaching using an English handout. The client nods and smiles but cannot demonstrate understanding. With interpreter phone assistance, she cannot state the medication name, purpose, bleeding signs to report, or what to do if a dose is missed. The nurse documents failed teach-back and the need for a certified Mandarin interpreter for comprehensive discharge teaching.

Unresolved discharge barriers: lives alone with no confirmed support for the first 48-72 hr; unsafe walker use; discharge teaching incomplete because of language barrier; no home health referral; uncertain rivaroxaban adherence; second-floor walk-up with no stair assessment; no confirmed follow-up appointment; creatinine trending upward while receiving a renally cleared anticoagulant.

options_or_rows_en.title: Coordination update, renal trend, and teach-back session
options_or_rows_en.content:
About 42 hr after surgery: T 36.9 °C, HR 74, BP 128/72, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% on room air. Pain 3/10 at rest and 5/10 with movement. Incision clean with staples intact and no signs of infection. Repeat creatinine 1.4 mg/dL after hydration and adequate oral intake; eGFR 39 mL/min. Hgb 10.0 g/dL.

Social work/case management interviews the client using a telephone interpreter. Her son can be present on the day of discharge and the following day, but not beyond that. She receives Meals on Wheels and has Medicare with a supplemental plan. Case management determines that she qualifies for Medicare home health services because she is homebound and requires skilled nursing plus home PT. The home health referral is initiated.

PT session: Client demonstrates safe walker use on level ground with standby assistance and can perform sit-to-stand from a raised toilet seat independently. Stairs have not yet been attempted. Afternoon PT stair training is planned.

Interpreter-mediated education: A certified in-person Mandarin interpreter is present. The nurse teaches posterior hip precautions, rivaroxaban purpose and 10 mg once-daily schedule for 35 days, bleeding signs, missed-dose instructions, pain medication safety, and when to call the surgeon or seek emergency care. The client accurately repeats key points in Mandarin, demonstrates hip precautions with a model, and identifies the rivaroxaban pill. She states she would need her son to call the surgeon because she cannot navigate the English phone tree alone. The nurse flags this as a remaining barrier.

Afternoon PT: Client negotiates four stairs up and down with a walker and one handrail with standby assistance. PT clears her for discharge home with home PT, front-wheeled walker, raised toilet seat, and reacher/grabber. The nurse requests a Mandarin-translated home exercise program. The surgeon writes a discharge order for postoperative day 3 morning pending nursing confirmation of discharge readiness.

stem_en: The nurse in the telemetry unit responds to a monitor alarm indicating bradycardia. The client's ECG strip displays sinus bradycardia at 42 beats per minute. Which assessment findings would indicate to the nurse that the client is experiencing hemodynamic compromise and requires active clinical intervention? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Acute chest pain or discomfort
Warm, dry skin with capillary refill of 2 seconds
Blood pressure of 82/46 mmHg
Dizziness and lightheadedness upon sitting up
Frequent premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) on the monitor
Increased urine output of 60 mL/hour

rationale_en: Hemodynamic compromise in bradycardia is marked by poor perfusion to vital organs. Signs include hypotension (BP < 90/60 mmHg), central nervous system signs (dizziness, syncope, confusion), cardiac perfusion issues (chest pain), and ventricular irritability (frequent PVCs) due to myocardial hypoxia. Dry, warm skin and normal urine output indicate preserved perfusion.

stem_en: A client is experiencing sustained sinus tachycardia at a rate of 140 beats per minute. Which findings, if noted by the nurse during assessment, would indicate that the tachycardia is compromising cardiac output and organ perfusion? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Systolic blood pressure of 88 mmHg
New-onset confusion or restlessness
Warm, flushed skin with strong radial pulses
Client reports sharp substernal chest pressure
Shortness of breath and crackles in lung bases
Urine output of 45 mL/hour over the last 4 hours

rationale_en: At high heart rates (sustained sinus tachycardia > 140 bpm), diastolic filling time is significantly reduced, which decreases stroke volume and cardiac output. Signs of compromise include pulmonary congestion (shortness of breath, crackles), hypotension (SBP < 90 mmHg), cerebral hypoperfusion (confusion, restlessness), and myocardial ischemia (chest pain). Flushed warm skin and normal urine output (>30 mL/hr) do not suggest compromise.

stem_en: A client has just received a rapid IV bolus of adenosine 6 mg for the conversion of supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). Which transient side effects or clinical findings should the nurse anticipate immediately following administration? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Shortness of breath or dyspnea
A brief period of asystole or cardiac pause on the monitor
Substernal chest pressure or discomfort
Generalized seizure activity
Facial flushing and warmth
Hypertensive crisis (BP >180/120 mmHg)

rationale_en: Adenosine slows conduction through the AV node, which often causes a brief, transient period of asystole (cardiac pause), which can be alarming but is expected and usually self-limiting. Other common, transient side effects include peripheral vasodilation (facial flushing), bronchospasm (shortness of breath/dyspnea), and chest pressure due to adenosine receptor activation in the heart. Seizures and hypertensive crisis are not side effects of adenosine.
